greek_head: Ἦ πολλὴν χάριν ὀφείλω σοι τῆς Θεαιτήτου γνωρίσεως ὦ Θεόδωρε ἅμα καὶ τῆς τοῦ ξένου
greek_tokens: eimi polus xaris ofellw su o qeaithtos gnwrisis eimi qeodwros ama kai o o cenos taxa de ge eimi swkraths ofeilhsis outos triplasios epeidan o te politikos apergazomai su kai o filosofos eien outws outos eimi filos qeodwros fhmi akouw eimi o peri logismos kai o gewmetrikos kratistos
english_head: Really I am greatly indebted to you, Theodorus, for my acquaintance with Theaetetus and with the Stranger, too. Presently, Socrates, you will be three times as much indebted, when they have worked out the statesman and the philosopher for you. Indeed! My dear Theodorus, can I believe my ears? Were those really the words of the great calculator and geometrician?

greek_head: Πῶς ὦ Σώκρατες Τῶν ἀνδρῶν ἕκαστον θέντος τῆς ἴσης ἀξίας οἳ τῇ
greek_tokens: pws eimi swkraths o androw ekastos tiqhmi o isos acios e o timaw pleos allhlwn afisthmi h kata o analogia o o umeteros texnh eu ge nh o hmeteros qeos eimi swkraths o ammwn kai dikaios kai panu men oun mnhmonikos epiplhssw egw o peri o logismos amarthma kai sos men anti outos eis auqis meteimi su de egw eimi cenos mhdamos apokamnw xarizomai alla echs eite o politikos anhr
english_head: Why, what do you mean, Socrates? When you rated sophist, statesman, and philosopher at the same value, though they are farther apart in worth than your mathematical proportion can express. By Ammon, our special divinity, that is a good hit, Socrates; evidently you haven’t forgotten your mathematics, and you are quite right in, finding fault with my bad arithmetic. I will get even with you at some other time; but now, Stranger, I turn to you. Do not grow tired of being kind to us, but go on and tell us about the statesman or the philosopher,

greek_head: πρότερον εἴτε τὸν φιλόσοφον προαιρῇ προελόμενος διέξελθε Ταῦτ' ὦ Θεόδωρε ποιητέον ἐπείπερ ἅπαξ γε
greek_tokens: proteros eite o filosofos proairew proairew diecerxomai outos eimi qeodwros poihteos epei apac ge enxeirew kai ou apostateon prin an autos pros o telos erxomai alla gar peri qeaithtos ode tis xrh draw egw o peri dianapauomai autos metalambanw autos o suggumnasths ode swkratew h pws sumbouleuw kaqa eipon metalambanw neos gar eimi raios ferw pas ponos anapauw
english_head: whichever you prefer to take first. That is the thing to do, Theodorus, since we have once begun, and we must not stop until we have finished with them. But what shall I do about Theaetetus here? In what respect? Shall we give him a rest and take his schoolmate here, the young Socrates, in his place? What is your advice? Make the change as you suggest. They are young, and if they have a chance to rest by turns, they will bear any labor better.

greek_head: Καὶ μὴν κινδυνεύετον ὦ ξένε ἄμφω ποθὲν ἐμοὶ συγγένειαν ἔχειν τινά τὸν μέν γε οὖν ὑμεῖς κατὰ τὴν τοῦ
greek_tokens: kai meis kinduneuw eimi cenos amfw pinw egw suggeneia exw tis o men ge oun su kata o o proswpon fus omoios egw fainw fhmi o de egw o
english_head: And besides, Stranger, it seems to me that they are both related to me after a fashion; one of them anyhow, as you say, looks like me in his cast of countenance, and the other has the same name and appellation, which implies some sort of kinship.

greek_head: κλῆσις ὁμώνυμος οὖσα καὶ ἡ πρόσρησις παρέχεταί τινα οἰκειότητα δεῖ δὴ τούς γε συγγενεῖς ἡμᾶς ἀεὶ προθύμως
greek_tokens: klhsis omwnumos eimi kai o prosrhsis parexw tis oikeioths deomai dh o ge suggeneus egw aei proqumos dia logos anagnwrizw qeaithtos men oun autos te summignumi xqes dia logos kai nun akouw apokrinw swkraths de oudeteros deomai de skeptomai kai outos egw men oun eis auqis sos de nun apokrinw outos eimi eimi swkraths akouw dh swkraths nai sugxwrew oun os legw panu men oun
english_head: Of course we ought always to be eager to get acquainted with our relatives by debating with them. Now I myself had an argument with Theaetetus yesterday and have been listening to his answers just now, but I do not know Socrates in either way and must examine him, too. But let him reply to you now; my turn will come by and by. Very well; Socrates, do you hear what Socrates says? Yes. And do you agree? Certainly.

greek_head: Οὐ τὰ σὰ κωλύειν φαίνεται δεῖ δὲ ἴσως ἔτι ἧττον τἀμὰ διακωλύειν ἀλλὰ δὴ μετὰ τὸν σοφιστὴν ἀναγκαῖον ὡς
greek_tokens: ou o sos kwluw fainw deomai de isos eti hsswn amos diakwluw alla dh meta o sofisths anagkaion ws egw fainw politikos o anhr diazhtew egw kai egw legos poteros o episthmwn tis egw kai outos qeteos h pws outws o episthmh ara dialhpteon wsper hnika o proteros skopaw taxa an ou men dh kata tautos ge eimi swkraths fainw egw tmhma tis meis
english_head: There seems to be no objection on your part, and I suppose there should be still less on mine. Well, then, after the sophist, I think it is our next duty to seek for the statesman; so please tell me: should we rank him also among those who have a science, or not? Yes. Must the sciences, then, be divided as when we were examining the sophist? Perhaps. In that case, Socrates, I think the division will not be along the same lines. How will it be?

greek_head: Κατ' ἄλλο Ἔοικέν γε
greek_tokens: kata allos eoika ge o oun politikos atrapos ph tis aneuriskw deomai gar autos aneuriskw kai xwris afairew apo o allos idea autos eis episfragizw kai o allos ektroph eis allos eidos epishmainw passw o episthmh ws eimi duo eidos dianoeomai o yuxh egw poiew outos hdh sos oimh o ergnumi eimi cenos alla ou emos gignomai
english_head: Along other lines. Very likely. Where, then, shall we find the statesman’s path? For we must find it, separate it from the rest, and imprint upon it the seal of a single class; then we must set the mark of another single class upon all the other paths that lead away from this, and make our soul conceive of all sciences as of two classes. This, Stranger, is now your affair, I think, not mine.

greek_head: Δεῖ γε μήν ὦ Σώκρατες αὐτὸ εἶναι καὶ σόν ὅταν ἐμφανὲς ἡμῖν γένηται
greek_tokens: deomai ge meis eimi swkraths autos eimi kai sos otan emfanhs egw gignomai kalos eipon ara oun ou ariqmhtikos men kai tis eteros outos suggeneus texnh yilos o pracis eimi o de gignwskw parexw monos eimi outws o de ge peri tektonikos au kai sumpas xeirourgia wsper en o pracis eneimi sumfuton o
english_head: And yet, Socrates, it must be your affair, too, when we have found the path. Quite true. Are not arithmetic and certain other kindred arts pure sciences, without regard to practical application, which merely furnish knowledge? Yes, they are. But the science possessed by the arts relating to carpentering and to handicraft in general is inherent in their application,

greek_head: ἐπιστήμην κέκτηνται καὶ συναποτελοῦσι τὰ γιγνόμενα ὑπ' αὐτῶν σώματα πρότερον οὐκ ὄντα
greek_tokens: episthmh ktaomai kai sunapotelew o gignomai upo autos swma proteros ou eimi tis meis outos toinun sumpas episthmh diairw o men praktikos proseipon o de monos gnwstikos eimi su outos ws eis episthmh o oloc eidos duo poteros oun o politikos kai basileus kai despoths kai etai oikonomos tiqhmi ws eis pas outos prosagoreuw h tosoutos texnh autos eimi fhmi osos onoma erw mallon de egw deuro epomai ph
english_head: and with its aid they create objects which did not previously exist. To be sure. In this way, then, divide all science into two arts, calling the one practical, and the other purely intellectual. Let us assume that all science is one and that these are its two forms. Shall we then assume that the statesman, king, master, and householder too, for that matter, are all one, to be grouped under one title, or shall we say that there are as many arts as names? But let me rather help you to understand in this way. In what way?

greek_head: Τῇδε εἴ τῴ τις τῶν δημοσιευόντων ἰατρῶν ἱκανὸς συμβουλεύειν ἰδιωτεύων αὐτός ἆρ' οὐκ ἀναγκαῖον αὐτῷ
greek_tokens: ode ei tis tis o dhmosieuw iatros ikanos sumbouleuw idiwteuw autos ara ou anagkaion autos prosagoreuw onoma o texnh tautos osper os sumbouleuw nai tis de ostis basileuw xwra andris parainew deinos idiwths eimi autos ara ou fhmi exw autos o episthmh os dew o arxw autos ktaomai fhmi
english_head: By this example: If anyone, though himself in private station, is able to advise one of the public physicians, must not his art be called by the same name as that of the man whom he advises? Yes. Well, then, if a man who is himself in private station is wise enough to advise him who is king of a country, shall we not say that he has the science which the ruler himself ought to possess? We shall.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν ἥ γε ἀληθινοῦ βασιλέως βασιλική Ναί
greek_tokens: alla meis o ge alhqinos basileus basilikos nai outos de o ktaomai ou antomai arxw antomai idiwths eimi tugxanw pantws kata ge o texnh autos basilikos orqos proserew dikaios goun kai meis oikonomos ge kai despoths tautos tis meis tis de megas sxhma oikhsis h mikros au polis ogkos mhs tis pros arxh diaferw oudeis
english_head: But certainly the science of a true king is kingly science? Yes. And will not he who possesses this science, whether he happen to be a ruler or a private citizen, rightly be called kingly, when considered purely with reference to his art? At least he has a right to be. And surely the householder and the master of a family are the same. Yes, of course. Well, so far as government is concerned, is there any difference between the grandeur of a large house and the majesty of a small state? No.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ὃ νυνδὴ διεσκοπούμεθα φανερὸν ὡς ἐπιστήμη μία περὶ πάντ' ἐστὶ ταῦτα ταύτην δὲ εἴτε βασιλικὴν
greek_tokens: oukoun o nundi diaskopew faneros ws episthmh eis peri panth eimi outos outos de eite basilikos eite politikos eite oikonomikos tis onomazw mhdeis autos diaferw tis gar alla meis ode ge dhlos ws basileus apas xeir kai sumpanti o swma mikros atta eis o katexw o arxh dunamai pros o o yuxazw sunesis kai rwmh dhlos o dh gnwstikos mallon h o xeirotexnikos kai
english_head: Then as for the point we were just discussing, it is clear that all these are the objects of one science, and whether a man calls this the art of kingship or statesmanship or householding, let us not quarrel with him. By no means. But this is plain, that any king can do little with his hands or his whole body toward holding his position, compared with what he can do with the sagacity and strength of his soul. Yes, that is plain.

greek_head: ὅλως πρακτικῆς βούλει τὸν βασιλέα φῶμεν οἰκειότερον εἶναι Τί μήν
greek_tokens: oloc praktikos boulomai o basileus fhmi oikeios eimi tis meis o ara politikos kai politikos kai basilikos kai basilikos eis tautos ws eis pas outos sunqew dhlos oukoun poreuw an echs ei meta outos o gnwstikos diorizw panu ge prosexw dh o new an ara en autos tis diafuh katanoew frazw poa
english_head: Shall we say, then, that the king is more akin to the intellectual than to the manual or the practical in general? Certainly. Shall we, therefore, put all these together as one—the political art and the statesman, the royal art and the king? Obviously. Then we should be proceeding in due order if we should next divide intellectual science? Certainly. Now pay attention to see if we can perceive any natural line of cleavage in it. Tell us of what sort it is.

greek_head: Τοιάνδε λογιστική πού τις ἡμῖν ἦν τέχνη Ναί
greek_tokens: toiosde logistikos pou tis egw eimi texnh nai o gnwstikos ge oimh pantapasi texnh pws de ou gignwskw dh logistikos o en o ariqmew diafora mhs tis pleos ergnumi didwmi h o gignwskw krinw tis meis kai gar arxitektwn ge pas ou autos ergatikos alla ergaths arxw nai parexw ge pou gignwskw alla ou xeirourgia outws
english_head: Of this sort. We recognized a sort of art of calculation. Yes. It is, I suppose, most certainly one of the intellectual arts. Of course. And shall we grant to the art of calculation, when it has found out the difference between numbers, any further function than that of passing judgement on them when found out? No, certainly not. Every architect, too, is a ruler of workmen, not a workman himself. Yes. As supplying knowledge, not manual labor. True.

greek_head: Δικαίως δὴ μετέχειν ἂν λέγοιτο τῆς γνωστικῆς ἐπιστήμης
greek_tokens: dikaios dh metexw an legw o gnwstikos episthmh panu ge outos de ge oimh proshkw krinw mh telos exw mhde apallassw kaqa o logisths apallassw prostassw de ekastos o ergaths o ge prosforos ews an apergazomai o prosstazw orqos oukoun gnwstikos men o te toioutos sumpas kai oposos sunepomai o logistikos krisis de kai epitacis
english_head: So he may fairly be said to participate in intellectual science. Certainly. But it is his business, I suppose, not to pass judgement and be done with it and go away, as the calculator did, but to give each of the workmen the proper orders, until they have finished their appointed task. You are right. Then all such sciences, and all those that are in the class with calculating, are alike intellectual sciences,

greek_head: διαφέρετον ἀλλήλοιν τούτω τὼ γένη Φαίνεσθον
greek_tokens: diaferw allhlwn outos o genos fainw ara oun sumpas o gnwstikos ei o men epitaktikos meros o de kritikos diairew proseipon emmelhs an fhmi diairew kata ge o emos doca alla meis o ge koinh tis prassw agaphtos omonoew pws de ou outos toinun mexri an autos koinwnew eateos o ge o allos docasma xairw tis meis
english_head: but these two classes differ from one another in the matter of judging and commanding. Am I right? I think so. Then if we bisected intellectual science as a whole and called one part the commanding and the other the judging part, might we say we had made fitting division? Yes, in my opinion. And surely when men are doing anything in common it is pleasant for them to agree. Of course it is. On this point, then, so long as we ourselves are in agreement, we need not bother about the opinions of others. Of course not.

greek_head: Φέρε δή τούτοιν τοῖν τέχναιν ἡμῖν τὸν βασιλικὸν ἐν ποτέρᾳ θετέον ἆρ' ἐν τῇ κριτικῇ καθάπερ τινὰ θεατήν
greek_tokens: ferw dh outos o texnh egw o basilikos en poteros qeteos ara en o kritikos kaqa tis qeaths h mallon o epitaktikos ws eimi autos texnh tiqhmi despozw ge pws gar ou mallon o epitaktikos dh texnh palin an eaw qeateon ei ph diisthmi kai egw dokew ode ph kaqa o o kaphlos texnh o o autopwlhs diaorizw texnh kai
english_head: Now to which of these two classes is the kingly man to be assigned? Shall we assign him to the art of judging, as a kind of spectator, or rather to the art of commanding, inasmuch as he is a ruler? Rather to the latter, of course. Then once more we must see whether the art of command falls into two divisions. It seems to me that it does, and I think there is much the same distinction between the kingly class and the class of heralds

greek_head: τὸ βασιλικὸν γένος ἔοικεν ἀπὸ τοῦ τῶν κηρύκων γένους ἀφωρίσθαι
greek_tokens: o basilikos genos eoika apo o o khruc genos aforizw pws pwleomai pou proteros ergon allotrios paradexomai deuteros pwlew palin e kaphlos panu men oun oukoun kai o khrukikos fulon epitassw allotrios nohma paradexomai autos deuteros epitassw palin eteros alhqhs tis oun eis tautos mignumi basilikos ermhneutikos
english_head: as between the art of men who sell what they themselves produce and that of retail dealers. How so? Retail dealers receive and sell over again the productions of others, which have generally been sold before. Certainly. And in like manner heralds receive the purposes of others in the form of orders, and then give the orders a second time to others. Very true. Shall we, then, join the art of the king in the same class with the art of the interpreter,

greek_head: κελευστικῇ μαντικῇ κηρυκικῇ καὶ πολλαῖς ἑτέραις τούτων τέχναις συγγενέσιν αἳ σύμπασαι τό γ' ἐπιτάττειν ἔχουσιν
greek_tokens: keleustikos mantikos khrukikos kai polus eteros outos texnh suggenhs o sumpas o ge epitassw exw h boulomai kaqa eikazw nundi kai onoma pareikazw epei kai sxedon anwnumos eimi tugxanw o o autepitakths genos kai outos outos diairew o men o basilh genos eis o autepitaktikos tiqhmi o de allos pas amelew onoma eteros autos paraxwrew tiqhmi tis o gar arxw eneka egw o meqodos eimi
english_head: the boatswain, the prophet, the herald, and many other kindred arts, all of which involve giving orders? Or, as we just now made a comparison of functions, shall we now by comparison make a name also—since the class of those who issue orders of their own is virtually nameless—and assign kings to the science of giving orders of one’s own, disregarding all the rest and leaving to someone else the task of naming them? For the object of our present quest is the ruler,

greek_head: ἀλλ' οὐχὶ τοῦ ἐναντίου Πάνυ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: alla ou o enantios panu men oun oukoun epei outos metrios afisthmi apo ekeinos allotrioths diorizw pros oikeioths outos autos palin au diairew anagkaion ei tis tomh eti exw upeikw en outos panu ge kai meis fainw exw alla epakolouqew suntemnw ph pas oposos an arxw dianoeomai epitacis
english_head: not his opposite. Quite right. Then since a reasonable distinction between this class and the rest has been made, by distinguishing the commands given as one’s own or another’s, shall we again divide this class, if there is in it any further line of section? Certainly. I think there is one; please help me in making the section. On what line? Take the case of all those whom we conceive of as rulers who give commands: shall we not find that they all issue commands

greek_head: προσχρωμένους ἆρ' οὐχ εὑρήσομεν γενέσεώς τινος ἕνεκα προστάττοντας
greek_tokens: prosxraomai ara ou euriskw genesis tis eneka prostassw pws de ou kai meis o ge gignomai pas dixa dialambanw ou pantapasi xalepos ph o men ayuxos autos eimi pou sumpas o de emyuxos nai outos de ge autos o o gnwstikos meros epitaktikos eimi eiper boulomai temnw temnw kata tis o men epi o o ayuxos genesis autos tassw
english_head: for the sake of producing something? Of course. Furthermore it is not at all difficult to divide all that is produced into two classes. How? Of the whole class, some have life and others have no life. Yes. And on these same lines we may, if we like, make a division of the part of intellectual science which commands. In what way? By assigning one part of it to the production of lifeless, the other to that of living objects;

greek_head: τὸ δ' ἐπὶ ταῖς τῶν ἐμψύχων καὶ πᾶν οὕτως ἤδη διαιρήσεται δίχα
greek_tokens: o de epi o o emyuxos kai pas outws hdh diairew dixa pantapasi ge o men toinun autos paraleipw o de analambanw analambanw de merizw eis duo o sumpas legw de autos analhpteon eimi poteros pantws pou o peri o zwion epitaktikos ou gar dh o ge o basilikos episthmh eimi pote o ayuxos epistatew oios arxitektonikos alla gennaios en o
english_head: and in this way the whole will be divided into two parts. Certainly. Let us then leave one half and take up the other, and then let us divide that entire half into two parts. Which half shall we take up? That which issues commands relating to living objects, assuredly. For certainly the science of the king is not, like that of the architect, one which supervises lifeless objects; it is a nobler science,

greek_head: ζῴοις καὶ περὶ αὐτὰ ταῦτα τὴν δύναμιν ἀεὶ κεκτημένον Ὀρθῶς
greek_tokens: zwion kai peri autos outos o dunamis aei ktaomai orqos o ge meis o zwion genesis kai trofh o men tis an eidon monotrofia eimi o de koinos o en o agelh qremma epimeleia orqos alla ou meis o ge politikos euriskw idiotrofos wsper bohlaths h tis ippokomos alla ippoforbos te kai bouforbos mallon proseoika fainw ge dh erw nun
english_head: since it exercises its power among living beings and in relation to them alone. True. Now you may notice that the breeding and nurture of living beings is sometimes the nurture of a single animal and sometimes the common care of creatures in droves. True. But we shall find that the statesman is not one who tends a single creature, like the driver of a single ox or the groom who tends a horse; he has more resemblance to a man who tends a herd of cattle or a drove of horses. That seems to be true, now that you mention it.

greek_head: Πότερον οὖν τῆς ζῳοτροφίας τὴν τῶν συμπόλλων κοινὴν τροφὴν ἀγελαιοτροφίαν ἢ κοινοτροφικήν τινα ὀνομάζομεν
greek_tokens: poteros oun o zwotrofia o o sumpolloi koinos trofh agelaiotrofia h koinotrofikos tis onomazw opoteros an en o logos sumbainw kalos ge eimi swkraths kan diafulassw o mh spoudazw epi o onoma plousios eis o ghras anafainw fronhsis nun de outos men kaqa diakeleuomai poihteos o de agelaiotrofikos ara ennoew ph
english_head: Shall we call the art of caring for many living creatures the art of tending a herd or something like community management? Whichever we happen to say. Good, Socrates! If you preserve this attitude of indifference to mere names, you will turn out richer in wisdom when you are old. But now we will, as you suggest, not trouble ourselves about the name; but do you see a way in which a man may show that the art of herding is twofold,

greek_head: τις δίδυμον ἀποφήνας τὸ ζητούμενον ἐν διπλασίοισι τὰ νῦν ἐν τοῖς ἡμίσεσιν εἰς τότε ποιήσει ζητεῖσθαι
greek_tokens: tis didumos apofainw o zhtew en diplasios o nun en o hmisus eis tote poihsis zhtew proqumeomai kai egw dokew o men anqrwpos eteros tis eimi o de au qhraw allos trofh pantapasi ge proqumos kai andreios diairew mh mentoi outos ge eis auqis kata dunamis pasxw o poios mh mikros morion eis pros megas kai polus
english_head: and may thereby cause that which is now sought among a double number of things to be sought among half as many? I am quite willing to try. I think one kind is the care of men, the other that of beasts. You made the division with perfect willingness and courage. However, let us do our best not to fall again into your error. What error? We must not take a single small part, and set it off against many large ones, nor disregard species in making our division.

greek_head: ἀφαιρῶμεν μηδὲ εἴδους χωρίς ἀλλὰ τὸ μέρος ἅμα εἶδος ἐχέτω κάλλιστον μὲν γὰρ ἀπὸ τῶν ἄλλων εὐθὺς διαχωρίζειν
greek_tokens: afairew mhde eidos xwris alla o meros ama eidos exw kalos men gar apo o allos euqus diaxwrizw o zhtew an orqos exis kaqa oligos su proteros oiomai exw o diairesis epispeudw o logos eidon epi anqrwpos poreuw alla gar eimi filos leptourgew ou asfalhs dia mesos de asfalhs eimi temnw kai mallon idea an tis prostugxanw outos de diaferw o
english_head: On the contrary, the part must be also a species. It is a very fine thing to separate the object of our search at once from everything else, if the separation can be made correctly, and so, just now, you thought you had the right division and you hurried our discussion along, because you saw that it was leading towards man. But, my friend, it is not safe to whittle off shavings; it is safer to proceed by cutting through the middle, and in that way one is more likely to find classes.

greek_head: πᾶν πρὸς τὰς ζητήσεις Πῶς ὦ ξένε λέγεις τοῦτο
greek_tokens: pas pros o zhthsis pws eimi cenos legw outos peirateon eti safhs frazw eunoia o sew fusis eimi swkraths en o men oun paristhmi o nun dhlow mhdeis endehs adunatos epixeirhteon de tis kai mikros pleos autos proagw eis o prosqen safhneia eneka poios oun dh frazw diairew egw ou orqos arti draw toiosde oios ei tis anqrwpinos epixeirew dixa
english_head: This makes all the difference in the conduct of research. What do you mean by that, Stranger? I must try to speak still more clearly, Socrates, out of regard for your capacity. Just at present it is impossible to make the matter entirely plain, but I will try to lay it before you a little more fully for the sake of clearness. What is it, then, that you say we did wrongly in making our division just now? It was very much as if, in undertaking to divide the human race into two parts,

greek_head: διελέσθαι γένος διαιροῖ καθάπερ οἱ πολλοὶ τῶν ἐνθάδε διανέμουσι τὸ μὲν Ἑλληνικὸν ὡς ἓν ἀπὸ πάντων ἀφαιροῦντες
greek_tokens: diairew genos diairew kaqa e polus o enqade dianemw o men ellhnikos ws eis apo pas afairew xwris sumpas de o allos genesis apeiros eimi kai ameiktos kai asumfwnos pros allhlwn barbaros eis klhsis proseipon autos dia outos o eis klhsis kai genos eis autos eimi prosdokaw h o ariqmos tis au nomizw kata eidos duo diairew murias apotemnw apo pas
english_head: one should make the division as most people in this country do; they separate the Hellenic race from all the rest as one, and to all the other races, which are countless in number and have no relation in blood or language to one another, they give the single name barbarian; then, because of this single name, they think it is a single species. Or it was as if a man should think he was dividing number into two classes by cutting off a myriad from all the other numbers, with the notion that he was making one separate class,

greek_head: ὡς ἓν εἶδος ἀποχωρίζων καὶ τῷ λοιπῷ δὴ παντὶ θέμενος ἓν ὄνομα διὰ τὴν κλῆσιν αὖ καὶ τοῦτ' ἀξιοῖ γένος ἐκείνου χωρὶς
greek_tokens: ws eis eidos apoxwrizw kai o loipazw dh pas tiqhmi eis onoma dia o klhsis au kai outos aciow genos ekeinos xwris eteros eis gignomai kallion de pou kai mallon kata eidos kai dixa diairew an ei o men ariqmos artios kai perissos tis temnw o de au o anqrwpos genos arshn kai qallw ludos de h fruc h tis eteros pros apas tassw aposxizw tote hnika aforaw genos ama kai meros euriskw
english_head: and then should give one name to all the rest, and because of that name should think that this also formed one class distinct from the other. A better division, more truly classified and more equal, would be made by dividing number into odd and even, and the human race into male and female; as for the Lydians and Phrygians and various others they could be opposed to the rest and split off from them when it was impossible to find and separate two parts,

greek_head: ἑκάτερον τῶν σχισθέντων Ὀρθότατα ἀλλὰ γὰρ τοῦτο αὐτό ὦ ξένε πῶς
greek_tokens: ekateros o sxizw orqos alla gar outos autos eimi cenos pws an tis genos kai meros enarghs gignwskw ws ou tautos eimi alla eteros allhlwn eimi beltistos androw ou faulos prostassw swkraths egw men kai nun makros o deon apo o protiqhmi logos planaw su de eti pleos egw keleuw planaw nun men oun wsper eoika epaneimi
english_head: each of which formed a class. Very true; but that’s just the trouble, Stranger: how can we get a clearer knowledge of class and part, and see that they are not the same thing, but different? Socrates, you most excellent young man, it is no small task you impose upon me. We have already strayed away from our subject more than we ought, and you wish us to wander still farther afield. So for the present let us return to our subject, as is proper; then we will go on the trail of this other matter by and by, when we have time.

greek_head: πάλιν ταῦτα δὲ εἰς αὖθις κατὰ σχολὴν καθάπερ ἰχνεύοντες μέτιμεν οὐ μὴν ἀλλὰ τοῦτό γε αὖ παντάπασιν φύλαξαι μή
greek_tokens: palin outos de eis auqis kata sxolh kaqa ixneuw meteimi ou meis alla outos ge au pantapasi fulassw mh pote para egw doca autos enarghs diaorizw akouw o poios eidos te kai meros eteros allhlwn eimi tis meis ws eidos men otan eimi tis kai meros autos anagkaion eimi o pragma ostis an eidos legw meros de eidos oudeis anagkh outos egw h ekeinos mallon eimi swkraths aei fhmi legw outos eimi
english_head: Only take very good care not to imagine that you ever heard me declare flatly— What? That class and part are separate from one another. But what did you say? That when there is a class of anything, it must necessarily be a part of the thing of which it is said to be a class; but there is no necessity that a part be also a class. Please always give this, rather than the other, as my doctrine. I will do so.

greek_head: Φράσον δή μοι τὸ μετὰ τοῦτο Ποῖον
greek_tokens: frazw dh egw o meta outos poios o o apoplanhsis opoqen egw deuro agw oimh men gar malista oqen erwtaw su o agelaiotrofia oph diaireteon eipon mala proqumos duh eimi zwion genos o men anqrwpinos eteros de o allos sumpas qhraw eis alhqhs kai egw dh tote fainw meros afairew hgeomai katalimpanw o loipos au pas genos eis ostis pas tautos
english_head: Then please go on to the next point. What is it? That from which our present digression started. For I think it started when you were asked how the art of herding should be divided and said with great readiness that there were two kinds of living beings, the human race and a second one, a single class, comprising all the beasts. True. And it was clear to me at the time that you removed a part and then thought that the remainder was one class

greek_head: ἐπονομάζειν ἔσχες ὄνομα θηρία καλέσας Ἦν καὶ ταῦτα οὕτως
greek_tokens: eponomazw exw onoma qhrion kalew eimi kai outos outws o de ge eimi pas andreios taxa an ei pou fronimos eimi tis zwion eteros oios dokew o o geranos h tis toioutos allos o kata tautos isos dionomazw kaqa kai su geranos men eis genos antitiqhmi o allos zwion kai semnunw autos eautou o de allos meta o anqrwpos sullambanw eis autos oudeis allos plhn isos qhrion proseipon
english_head: because you were able to call them all by the same name of beasts. That is true, too. But indeed, my most courageous young friend, perhaps, if there is any other animal capable of thought, such as the crane appears to be, or any other like creature, and it perchance gives names, just as you do, it might in its pride of self oppose cranes to all other animals, and group the rest, men included, under one head, calling them by one name, which might very well be that of beasts. Now let us try to be on our guard against all that sort of thing.

greek_head: πειραθῶμεν οὖν ἡμεῖς ἐξευλαβεῖσθαι πάνθ' ὁπόσα τοιαῦτα Πῶς
greek_tokens: peiraw oun egw eceulabeomai panth oposos toioutos pws mh pas o o zwion genos diairew ina hsswn autos pasxw oudeis gar deomai kai gar oun kai tote amartanw outos tis dh o gnwstikos osos epitaktikos egw meros eimi pou o zwotrofikos genos agelaios meis zwion eimi gar nai
english_head: How can we guard against it? By not dividing the whole class of living beings, that so we may avoid such errors. Well, there is no need of dividing the whole. No, certainly not, for it was in that way that we fell into our former error. What do you mean? That part of intellectual science which involves giving commands was a part of our animal-tending class, with especial reference to animals in herds, was it not? Yes. Well, even at that stage of our discussion all animals had already been divided into tame and wild.

greek_head: Διῄρητο τοίνυν ἤδη καὶ τότε σύμπαν τὸ ζῷον τῷ τιθασῷ καὶ ἀγρίῳ τὰ μὲν γὰρ ἔχοντα τιθασεύεσθαι φύσιν
greek_tokens: diairew toinun hdh kai tote sumpas o zwion o tiqasos kai agrios o men gar exw tiqaseuw fus hmeros proseresqai o de mh eqelw agrios kalos os de ge qhreuw episthmh en o hmeros eimi te kai eimi epi o agelaios meis zhthteos qremma nai mh toinun diairw wsper tote pros apas apoblepw mhde speudw ina dh taxus gignomai
english_head: For if their nature admits of domestication they are called tame; if it does not, they are called wild. Excellent. But the science we are hunting for was, and is, to be sought among tame creatures, more specifically creatures in herds. Yes. Let us, then, not make our division as we did before, with a view to all, nor in a hurry, with the idea that we may thus reach political science quickly,

greek_head: πρὸς τῇ πολιτικῇ πεποίηκε γὰρ ἡμᾶς καὶ νῦν παθεῖν τὸ κατὰ τὴν παροιμίαν πάθος
greek_tokens: pros o politikos poiew gar egw kai nun pasxw o kata o paroimia paqos poios ou hsuxos eu diairew anuw bradus kai kalos ge eimi cenos poiew outos eimi palin de oun ek arxh o koinotrofikos peirw diairew isos gar kai outos o su proqumeomai diaperainw o logos autos su kallion mhnusis kai egw frazw poios dh ode ei tis pollakis ara diakouw ou gar dh
english_head: for that has already brought upon us the proverbial penalty. What penalty? The penalty of having made less speed, because we made too much haste and did not make our division right. And it was a good thing for us, Stranger I do not deny it. So let us begin again and try to divide the art of tending animals in common; for perhaps the information you desire so much will come to you in the ordinary course of our conversation better than by other means. Tell me— What? Whether, as I suppose, you have often heard people speak of this,—

greek_head: προστυχής γε αὐτὸς οἶδ' ὅτι γέγονας ταῖς ἐν τῷ Νείλῳ τιθασείαις τῶν ἰχθύων καὶ τῶν ἐν ταῖς βασιλικαῖς λίμναις
greek_tokens: prostuxhs ge autos oida ostis gignomai o en o neilos tiqaseia o ixqus kai o en o basilikos limnh en men gar krainw taxa an isos eaw aisqanomai panu men oun kai outos qeaomai ekeinos polus akouw kai meis xhnobwtia ge kai geranobwtia ei kai mh planaw peri o qessalikos pedion punqanomai goun kai pisteuw eimi tis meis
english_head: for I know you never actually saw the preserves of fish in the Nile and in the ponds of the Persian king. But perhaps you have noticed the like in fountain-pools. Yes, I have often seen the fish in fountain-pools and have heard many tales of those foreign preserves. And surely, even if you have not wandered over the plains of Thessaly, you have heard of goose-farms and crane-farms there and you believe that they exist. Yes, of course.

greek_head: Τοῦδ' ἕνεκά τοι πάντα ἠρώτησα ταῦτα διότι τῆς τῶν ἀγελαίων τροφῆς ἔστι μὲν ἔνυδρον ἔστι δὲ καὶ
greek_tokens: ode eneka su pas erwtaw outos dioti o o agelaios trefw eimi men enudros eimi de kai chrobatikos eimi gar oun ara oun kai sos sundokew outos dew dixazw o koinotrofikos episthmh epi ekateros outos o meros autos epinemw ekateros o men eteros ugrotrofikos onomazw o de eteros chrotrofikon egw kai meis kai o basilikos outws ou zhtew
english_head: The reason why I asked you all these questions is that the rearing of flocks is in part aquatic and in part an affair of the dry land. Yes, that is true. Then do you agree that we ought to divide the art of tending animals in common into corresponding parts, assigning one part of it to each of these two, and calling one the art of aquatic-herding and the other the art of land-herding? Yes, I agree. And surely we shall not have to ask

greek_head: ὁποτέρας ἐστὶ τῆς τέχνης δῆλον δὴ γὰρ παντί Πῶς δ' οὔ
greek_tokens: opoteros eimi o texnh dhlos dh gar pas pws de ou pas men dh o ge chrotrofikon o agelaiotrofia diairew an fulon pws o pthnos te kai pezos diorizw alhqhs tis de o politikos eimi peri o pezos zhthteos h ou oiomai kai o afrwn ws epos eipon docazw outws egw o de pezonomikos kaqa arti o ariqmos deomai temnw dixa apofainw dhlos
english_head: to which of these two arts kingship belongs, for that is clear to everyone. Of course. Anybody could doubtless make a division of the art of tending herds on land. What would the division be? Into the tending of flying and walking animals. Very true. And statesmanship is to be sought in connection with walking animals, is it not? Any fool, so to speak, would believe that, don’t you think? Of course. And the art of tending animals that walk must, like an even number, be divided in half. Evidently.

greek_head: Καὶ μὴν ἐφ' ὅ γε μέρος ὥρμηκεν ἡμῖν ὁ λόγος ἐπ' ἐκεῖνο δύο τινὲ καθορᾶν ὁδὼ τεταμένα φαίνεται τὴν μὲν
greek_tokens: kai meis epi o ge meros ormaw egw o logos epi ekeinos duo tis kaqoraw odos teinw fainw o men qassw pros megas meros mikros diairew o de osper en o prosqen legw ostis deomai mesotomew ws malista outos exw mallon makros ge meis ecesti oun opoteros an boulomai outos poreuw tis de amfoteros adunatos ama ge eimi qaumastos en meros ge meis dhlos ostis dunatos
english_head: And now I think I see two paths leading in that direction in which our argument has started: the quicker way, by separating a relatively small part and a larger, and the other way, which is more in accord with what we said a while ago about the need of making the division as nearly in the middle as we can, but is longer. So we can proceed by whichever of the two we wish. Can we not go by both? Not by both at once, silly boy; but obviously we can take them in turn.

greek_head: Ἐν μέρει τοίνυν ἔγωγε ἀμφοτέρας αἱροῦμαι Ῥᾴδιον ἐπειδὴ τὸ λοιπὸν βραχύ κατ' ἀρχὰς μὴν
greek_tokens: en meros toinun egw amfoteros airew radios epei o loipos braxus kata arxh meis kai mesow ama o poreia xalepos an eimi egw o prostagma nun de epei dokew outos o makros proteros eimi nealhs gar eimi raios autos poreuw o de dh diairesis oraw legos o pezos egw o hmeros osos agelaios diairew eimi fusis dixa tis o o men o genesis akeros eimi o de kerasforos
english_head: Then I choose both in turn. That is easy enough, since we have but a short distance to go. At the beginning, certainly, or middle of our journey it would have been hard to comply with your demand. But now, since this is your wish, let us go first by the longer way, for we are fresher now and shall get along on it more easily. So attend to the division. Go on. The tame walking animals which live in herds are divided by nature into two classes. How by nature? Because one class is naturally without horns, and the other has horns.

greek_head: Φαίνεται Τὴν δὴ πεζονομικὴν διελὼν ἀπόδος ἑκατέρῳ τῷ μέρει
greek_tokens: fainw o dh pezonomikos diairew apodidwmi ekateros o meros logos xraomai an gar onomazw autos boulomai eimi su periplekw mallon o deon pws oun xrh legw wde o pezonomikos episthmh dixa diairew o morion eteros epi o kerasforos meros o o agelh epitassw o de eteros epi o o akeratos
english_head: That is obvious. Now divide the art of tending herds of walking animals into two parts, assigning one to each class of animals; and define the parts, for if you try to give them names, the matter will become needlessly complicated. How shall I speak of them then? In this way: say that the science which tends herds of walking animals is divided into two parts, one of which is assigned to the horned portion of the herd, the other to the hornless portion. Assume that I have said that;

greek_head: Ταῦτ' ἔστω ταύτῃ λεχθέντα πάντως γὰρ ἱκανῶς δεδήλωται
greek_tokens: outos eimi outos legw pantws gar ikanos dhlow kai meis o ge basileus egw au katafanhs ostis kolobos agelh tis keras nomeuw pws gar ou dhlos outos toinun kataqrauw o gignomai autos peirw apodidwmi panu ge poteros oun boulomai o sxistos te kai o kalew mwnuc diairew autos h o koinogonia te kai idiogonia manqanw gar pou o poios
english_head: for you have made it perfectly clear. And furthermore our king is very clearly the herdsman of a herd devoid of horns. Of course; that is evident. Let us then try to break up this herd and give the king the part that belongs to him. Very well. Shall we make our division on the basis of having or not having cloven hoofs, or on that of mixing or not mixing the breed? You know what I mean. No. What is it?

greek_head: Ὅτι τὸ μὲν τῶν ἵππων καὶ ὄνων πέφυκεν ἐξ ἀλλήλων γεννᾶν
greek_tokens: ostis o men o ippos kai onos fuw ek allhlwn genna nai o de ge loipos eti o leia agelh o hmeros amighs genos pros allhlwn pws de ou tis de o politikos ara epimeleia exw fainw poteros koinogenhs fusis h tis idiogenhs dhlos ostis o ameiktos outos dh deomai kaqa o emprosqen ws eoika egw dixa diastellw deomai gar oun
english_head: Why, I mean that horses and asses can breed from each other. Oh yes. But the rest of the herd of hornless tame animals cannot cross the breed. That is true, of course. Well then, does the statesman appear to have charge of a kind that mixes or of one that does not mix the breed? Evidently of one that is unmixed. So I suppose we must proceed as we have done heretofore and divide this into two parts. Yes, we must.

greek_head: Καὶ μὴν τό γε ζῷον ὅσον ἥμερον καὶ ἀγελαῖον σχεδὸν πλὴν γενοῖν δυοῖν πᾶν ἤδη κατακεκερμάτισται τὸ
greek_tokens: kai meis o ge zwion osos hmeros kai agelaios sxedon plhn genos duo pas hdh katakermatizw o gar o kuwn ou epacios katariqmew genos ws en agelaios qremma ou gar oun alla tis dh o duo diairew os kai dikaios ge qeaithtos te kai sos dianemw epei kai gewmetria aptw o o diametron dhpou kai palin o o diametron diametron pws eipon
english_head: And yet tame gregarious animals have all, with the exception of about two species, been already divided; for dogs are not properly to be counted among gregarious creatures. No, they are not. But how shall we divide the two species? As you and Theaetetus ought by rights to divide them, since you are interested in geometry. How do you mean? By the diameter, of course, and again by the diameter of the square of the diameter. What do you mean by that?

greek_head: Ἡ φύσις ἣν τὸ γένος ἡμῶν τῶν ἀνθρώπων κέκτηται μῶν ἄλλως πως εἰς τὴν πορείαν πέφυκεν ἢ καθάπερ ἡ
greek_tokens: o fusis os o genos egw o anqrwpos ktaomai mhs allos pws eis o poreia fuw h kaqa o diametros o dunamis dipous ou allos kai meis o ge o loipos genos palin eimi kata dunamis au o hmeteros dunamis diametros eiper duo ge eimi pous dis fuw pws de ou eimi kai dh kai sxedon o boulomai dhlow manqanw pros dh outos eteros au tis o pros gelws
english_head: Is the nature which our human race possesses related to walking in any other way than as the diameter which is the square root of two feet? No. And the nature of the remaining species, again, considered from the point of view of the square root, is the diameter of the square of our root, if it is the nature of twice two feet. Of course; and now I think I almost understand what you wish to make plain. Socrates, do we see that besides this something else has turned up

greek_head: εὐδοκιμησάντων ἄν ὦ Σώκρατες ἆρα καθορῶμεν ἡμῖν γεγονὸς ἐν τοῖς διῃρημένοις
greek_tokens: eudokimew an eimi swkraths ara kaqoraw egw gignomai en o diairew o poios anqrwpinos egw ama genos sullagxanw kai suntrexw genos o o eimi gennaios kai ama euxerhs kaqoraw kai mala atopos sumbainw tis de ou eoika ustatos afikneomai o bradus nai outos ge ode de ou ennoew ws eti geloios o basileus fainw meta o agelh sundiaqew kai sundromos
english_head: in these divisions of ours which would be a famous joke? No. What is it? Our human race shares the same lot and runs in the same heat as the most excellent and at the same time most easy-going race of creatures. Yes, I see that; it is a very queer result. Indeed? But is it not reasonable that they arrive last, who are the slowest? Yes, that is true. And do we fail to notice this further point, that the king appears in a still more ridiculous light, running along with the herd and paired in the race with the man of all others

greek_head: πεπορευμένος τῷ τῶν ἀνδρῶν αὖ πρὸς τὸν εὐχερῆ βίον ἄριστα γεγυμνασμένῳ
greek_tokens: poreuw o o androw au pros o euxerhs bios ariston gumnazw pantapasi men oun nun gar eimi swkraths ekeinos eimi katafanhs mallon o erw tote en o peri o sofisths zhthsis o poios ostis o toiosde meqodos o logos oute semnos mallon melw h mh o te mikros oudeis atimazw pro o megas aei de kaqa eautou perainw alhqhs eoika oukoun meta outos ina mh egw fqanw erwtaw o
english_head: who is most in training for a life of careless ease? Certainly he does. For now, Socrates, we have shown more clearly the truth of that which we said yesterday in our search for the sophist. What was it? That this method of argument pays no more heed to the noble than to the ignoble, and no less honor to the small than to the great, but always goes on its own way to the most perfect truth. So it seems. Then shall I now, without waiting for you to ask me, guide you of my own accord along that shorter way referred to a moment ago that leads

greek_head: βραχυτέραν ὁδὸν ἥτις τότε ἦν ἐπὶ τὸν τοῦ βασιλέως ὅρον αὐτός σοι πρότερον ἔλθω
greek_tokens: braxus odos ostis tote eimi epi o o basileus oros autos su proteros erxomai sfodra ge legos dh dew tote euqus o pezos o dipous pros o tetrapous genos dianemw kateidon de anqrwpinos eti monos o pthnos sullagxanw o dipous agelh palin o yilos kai o pterofuew temnw temnw de autos kai tote hdh o anqrwponomikos dhlow texnh ferw o politikos kai basilikos oios hnioxos eis autos enisthmi paradidwmi o o polis hnia ws oikeios kai autos outos eimi o episthmh
english_head: to the definition of the king? By all means. I say, then, that we ought at that time to have divided walking animals immediately into biped and quadruped, then seeing that the human race falls into the same division with the feathered creatures and no others, we must again divide the biped class into featherless and feathered, and when that division is made and the art of herding human beings is made plain, we ought to take the statesmanlike and kingly man and place him as a sort of charioteer therein, handing over to him the reins of the state, because that is his own proper science.

greek_head: Καλῶς καὶ καθαπερεὶ χρέος ἀπέδωκάς μοι τὸν λόγον προσθεὶς τὴν ἐκτροπὴν οἷον τόκον καὶ ἀναπληρώσας
greek_tokens: kalos kai kaqaperei xreos apodidwmi egw o logos prostiqhmi o ektroph oios tokos kai anaplhrow autos ferw dh kai suneirw epanerxomai epi o arxh mexri o teleutaw o logos o onoma o o politikos texnh panu men oun o gnwstikos toinun episthmh egw eimi kata arxh meros epitaktikos outos de apeikazw o morion autepitaktikos
english_head: You have cleared up the argument finely, and as if it were a debt you were paying, you threw in the digression as interest and for good measure. Now let us go back to the beginning and join together the definition of the name of the statesman’s art link by link to the end. By all means. In the first place we said that intellectual science had a part that gives commands; and a portion of this was called by a comparison the part that gives its own commands;

greek_head: ἐρρήθη ζῳοτροφικὴ δὲ πάλιν αὐτεπιτακτικῆς οὐ τὸ σμικρότατον τῶν γενῶν ἀπεσχίζετο καὶ ζῳοτροφικῆς εἶδος
greek_tokens: erw zwotrofikos de palin autepitaktikos ou o mikros o genos aposxizw kai zwotrofikos eidos agelaiotrofikos agelaiotrofikos de au pezonomikos o de pezonomikos malista apotemnw texnh o akeratos fusis qreptikos outos de au o meros ou elasswn triploos sumplekw anagkaion an eis eis tis autos onoma sunagw boulomai genesis ameiktos nomeutikos episthmh prosagoreuw
english_head: and again the art of rearing living beings was singled out, which is by no means the smallest part of the art which gives its own commands; and a class of rearing living beings was herd-tending, and a part of this again the herding of walking animals; and from the herding of walking animals the art of rearing those without horns was divided. And of this in turn one part will have to be treated as no less than threefold, if it is to be called by one comprehensive name, and it will be called (1) the science (2) of tending herds (3) which do not cross breeds.

greek_head: τὸ δ' ἀπὸ τούτου τμῆμα ἐπὶ ποίμνῃ δίποδι μέρος ἀνθρωπονομικὸν ἔτι λειφθὲν μόνον τοῦτ' αὐτό ἐστιν ἤδη τὸ
greek_tokens: o de apo outos tmhma epi poimnh dipous meros anqrwponomikos eti leipw monos outos autos eimi hdh o zhtew ama basilikos tautos kalew kai politikos pantapasi men oun ara ge eimi swkraths alhqhs egw outos kaqa su nun erw outws eimi kai prassw o poios dh o pantapasi ikanos erw o protiqhmi h outos autos kai malista o zhthsis elleipw o o logos
english_head: But the only possible further subdivision of this is the art of herding human beings, and this is at last what we are looking for, the single art called both kingly and statesmanlike. That is perfectly true. And yet, Socrates, have we truly accomplished this, exactly as you have said? Accomplished what? The perfectly satisfactory discussion of our subject. Or is our investigation incomplete in just this detail, that we have given a definition after a fashion,

greek_head: εἰρῆσθαι μέν πως οὐ μὴν παντάπασί γε τελέως ἀπειργάσθαι Πῶς εἶπες
greek_tokens: erw men pws ou meis pantapasi ge teleios apergazomai pws eipon egw egw peiraw outos autos o dianoeomai nun eti mallon dhlow legos an oukoun o nomeutikos egw polus fainw arti texnh eis tis eimi o politikos kai eis tis agelh epimeleia nai outos de ge diaorizw o logos ou ippos eimi trofos oude allos qhraw alla anqrwpos koinotrofikos episthmh outws
english_head: but have not perfectly completed it? What do you mean? I will try to make still plainer to us both the thought which I now have in mind. Please do so. We found just now that there were many arts of herding, and one of them was the art of statesmanship, which was the care of one particular kind of herd, did we not? Yes. And our argument defined this, not as the tending of horses or other beasts, but as the science of tending men in common. Yes, it did.

greek_head: Τὸ δὴ τῶν νομέων πάντων διάφορον καὶ τὸ τῶν βασιλέων θεασώμεθα
greek_tokens: o dh o nomeus pas diaforos kai o o basilh qeaomai o poios ei tis o allos tis texnh allos onoma exw koinh o agelh suntrofos eimi fhmi kai prospoiew pws fhmi oios e emporos kai gewrgos kai sitourgos pas kai pros outos gumnasths kai o o iatros genos oistos ostis o peri o anqrwpinos nomeus os politikos kalew
english_head: Let us, then, observe the point of difference between kings and all other herdsmen. What point of difference? Let us see whether anyone who is designated by the name of another art says and claims that he is fellow manager of the herd in common with any of the other kinds of herdsmen. What do you mean? For instance, merchants, husbandmen, and all who prepare grain for use, and also gymnastic trainers and physicians would certainly all dispute with the herdsmen of humanity, whom we have called statesmen,

greek_head: παντάπασι τῷ λόγῳ διαμάχοιντ' ἂν οὗτοι σύμπαντες ὡς σφεῖς τῆς τροφῆς ἐπιμελοῦνται τῆς ἀνθρωπίνης οὐ μόνον
greek_tokens: pantapasi o logos diamaxomai an outos sumpas ws sfeis o trefw epimeleomai o anqrwpinos ou monos agelaios anqrwpos alla kai o o arxw autos oukoun orqos an legw isos kai outos men episkeptomai ode de oida ostis boukolos ge oudeis amfisbhthsis peri outos oudeis alla autos o agelh trofos o bouforbos autos iatros autos oios numfeuths kai peri o o gignomai tokos
english_head: and would assert that they themselves take care of the tending of humanity, and not the tending of the common herd only, but even that of the rulers themselves, would they not? And would they be right? Perhaps. We will examine that matter; but this we know, that no one will ever raise such a contention against any neatherd, but the herdsman himself tends the herd, he is their physician, he is their matchmaker,

greek_head: καὶ λοχείας μόνος ἐπιστήμων τῆς μαιευτικῆς ἔτι τοίνυν παιδιᾶς καὶ μουσικῆς ἐφ' ὅσον αὐτοῦ τὰ θρέμματα φύσει
greek_tokens: kai loxeia monos episthmos o maieutikos eti toinun paidih kai mousikh epi osos autos o qremma fusis metalambanw ou allos kreisswn paramuqeomai kai khlas praunw meta te organon kai yilos o stoma o o eautou poimnh ariston metaxeirizomai mousikh kai dh kai o allos peri nomeus o autos tropos eimi gar orqos pws oun egw o logos orqos fainw kai akeraios
english_head: and he alone knows the midwife’s science of aiding at the birth of their offspring. Moreover, so far as the nature of the creatures allows them to enjoy sport or music, no one can enliven or soothe them better than he; whether with instruments or merely with his voice he performs the music best suited to his own herd; and the same applies to the other herdsmen. Is not that the case? You are quite right. Then how can our discourse about the king be right and free from error,

greek_head: ὁ περὶ τοῦ βασιλέως ὅταν αὐτὸν νομέα καὶ τροφὸν ἀγέλης ἀνθρωπίνης θῶμεν μόνον ἐκκρίνοντες μυρίων ἄλλων ἀμφιςβητούντων
greek_tokens: o peri o basileus otan autos nomehs kai trofos agelh anqrwpinos qazw monos ekkrinw murw allos amfisbhtew oudamos oukoun orqos oligos emprosqen fobew upopteuw mh legw men tis tugxanw sxhma basilikos ou meis apergazomai ge eimi pw dia akribeia o politikos ews an o perixew autos kai o sunnomeus autos antipoiew periairew kai xwrizw apo ekeinos kaqaros monos autos apofainw
english_head: when we pick him out alone as herdsman and tender of the human herd, while countless others dispute his claim? It cannot possibly be right. We suspected a little while ago that although we might be outlining a sort of kingly shape we had not yet perfected an accurate portrait of the statesman, and could not do so until, by removing those who crowd about him and contend with him for a share in his herdsmanship, we separated him from them and made him stand forth alone and uncontaminated. Was our fear justified?

greek_head: Ὀρθότατα μὲν οὖν Τοῦτο τοίνυν ὦ Σώκρατες ἡμῖν ποιητέον εἰ μὴ
greek_tokens: orqos men oun outos toinun eimi swkraths egw poihteos ei mh mellw epi o telos kataisxunw o logos alla meis oudamos outos ge drasteos palin toinun ek allos arxh deomai kaqa eteros odos poreuw tis poa dh sxedon paidih egkerannumi suxnazw gar meros deomai megas muqos prosxraomai kai o loipos dh kaqa
english_head: It certainly was. Then we must attend to that, Socrates, if we are not to end our argument in disgrace. But we certainly must not do that. Then we must begin again from a new sarting-point and travel by a different road. By what road? By one which offers us some amusement; for there is a famous story a great part of which it is really our duty to insert into our discussion; and then after that we can proceed as before,

greek_head: ἐν τοῖς πρόσθεν μέρος ἀεὶ μέρους ἀφαιρουμένους ἐπ' ἄκρον ἀφικνεῖσθαι τὸ ζητούμενον οὐκοῦν χρή
greek_tokens: en o prosqen meros aei meros afairew epi akron afikneomai o zhtew oukoun xrh panu men oun alla dh o muqos egw panu prosexw o new kaqa e pais pantws ou polus ekfeugw paidih eths legos an eimi toinun kai eti eimi o pala legw polus te allos kai dh kai o peri o atreus te kai queston legw eris fasma akouw gar pou kai apomnhmoneuw o fhmi gignomai tote o peri o xruseos arnos isos shmeion frazw
english_head: by eliminating part after part, and in that way reach the ultimate object of our search. Shall we do that? By all means. Then please pay careful attention to my story, just as if you were a child; and anyway you are not much too old for children’s tales. Please tell the story. Of the portents recorded in ancient tales many did happen and will happen again. Such an one is the portent connected with the tale of the quarrel between Atreus and Thyestes. You have doubtless heard of it and remember what is said to have taken place. You refer, I suppose, to the token of the golden lamb.

greek_head: Οὐδαμῶς ἀλλὰ τὸ περὶ τῆς μεταβολῆς δύσεώς τε καὶ ἀνατολῆς ἡλίου καὶ τῶν ἄλλων ἄστρων ὡς ἄρα ὅθεν μὲν
greek_tokens: oudamos alla o peri o metaboleus dusis te kai anatolh hlios kai o allos astron ws ara oqen men anatellw nun eis outos tote o topos duw anatellw de ek o enantios tote de dh marturew ara o qeos atreus metaballw autos epi o nun sxhma legw gar oun dh kai outos kai meis au kai o ge basileios os arxw kronos polus akouw
english_head: Oh no; I mean the change in the rising and setting of the sun and the other heavenly bodies, how in those times they used to set in the quarter where they now rise, and used to rise where they now set, but the god at the time of the quarrel, you recall, changed all that to the present system as a testimony in favor of Atreus. Yes, I’ve heard that, too. And again we have often heard the tale of the reign of Cronus.

greek_head: Πλείστων μὲν οὖν Τί δέ τὸ τοὺς ἔμπροσθεν φύεσθαι γηγενεῖς καὶ μὴ
greek_tokens: pleistos men oun tis de o o emprosqen fuw ghgenhs kai mh ek allhlwn gennaw kai outos eis o pala legw outos toinun eimi men sumpas ek tautos paqos kai pros outos eteros murios kai outos eti qaumastos dia de xronos plhqos o men autos aposbennumi o de diaspeirw eromai xwris ekastos apo allhlwn o de eimi pas
english_head: Yes, very often. And how about the story that the ancient folk were earthborn and not begotten of one another? That is one of the old tales, too. Well, all these stories and others still more remarkable have their source in one and the same event, but in the lapse of ages some of them have been lost and others are told in fragmentary and disconnected fashion. But no one has told the event which is the cause of them all,

greek_head: τούτοις αἴτιον τὸ πάθος οὐδεὶς εἴρηκεν νῦν δὲ δὴ λεκτέον εἰς γὰρ τὴν τοῦ βασιλέως ἀπόδειξιν πρέψει ῥηθέν
greek_tokens: outos aitios o paqos oudeis erw nun de dh lekteos eis gar o o basileus apodeicis prepw erw kallista eipon kai legos mhdeis elleipw akouw an o gar pas ode tote men autos o qeos sumpodhgew poreuw kai sugkuklew tote de anhkw otan o periodos o proshkw autos metron lambanw hdh xronos o de palin automatos eis enantios periagw
english_head: and so I must tell it now; for that will help us to make clear the nature of the king. Very good; just tell your tale and omit nothing. Listen then. During a certain period God himself goes with the universe as guide in its revolving course, but at another epoch, when the cycles have at length reached the measure of his allotted time, he lets it go,

greek_head: ζῷον ὂν καὶ φρόνησιν εἰληχὸς ἐκ τοῦ συναρμόσαντος αὐτὸ κατ' ἀρχάς τοῦτο δὲ αὐτῷ τὸ ἀνάπαλιν ἰέναι διὰ τόδ' ἐξ
greek_tokens: zwion eimi kai fronhsis lagxanw ek o sunarmozw autos kata arxh outos de autos o anapalin eimi dia ode ek anagkh emfutos gignomai dia o poios dh o kata tautos kai wsautws exw aei kai tautos eimi o pas qeios proshkw monos swma de fusis ou outos o tacis os de ouranos kai kosmos eponomazw polus men kai makarios para o gennaw metalambanw atar oun dh koinwnew ge
english_head: and of its own accord it turns backward in the opposite direction, since it is a living creature and is endowed with intelligence by him who fashioned it in the beginning. Now this reversal of its motion is an inevitable part of its nature for the following reason. What reason? Absolute and perpetual immutability is a property of only the most divine things of all, and body does not belong to this class. Now that which we call heaven and the universe has received from its creator many blessed qualities, but then, too, it partakes also of a bodily nature;

greek_head: καὶ σώματος ὅθεν αὐτῷ μεταβολῆς ἀμοίρῳ γίγνεσθαι διὰ παντὸς ἀδύνατον κατὰ δύναμίν γε μὴν ὅτι μάλιστα ἐν τῷ
greek_tokens: kai swma oqen autos metaboleus amoiros gignomai dia pas adunatos kata dunamis ge meis ostis malista en o autos kata tautos eis fora kinew dio o anakuklhsis lagxanw ostis mikros o eautou kinhsis parallacis autos de eautou strefw aei sxedon oudeis dunatos plhn o o kinew au pas hgeomai kinew de outos tote men allos auqis de enantios ou qemis ek pas dh outos o kosmos mhte autos xrh fainw strefw eautou aei mhte au oloc aei upo qeaomai strefw dissos kai enaniaw
english_head: therefore it is impossible for it to be entirely free from change; it moves, however, so far as it is able to do so, with a single motion in the same place and the same manner, and therefore it has acquired the reverse motion in a circle, because that involves the least deviation from its own motion. But to turn itself for ever is hardly possible except for the power that guides all moving things; and that this should turn now in one direction and now in the opposite direction is contrary to divine law. As the result of all this, we must not say either that the universe turns itself always, or that it is always turned by God in two opposite courses,

greek_head: περιαγωγάς μήτ' αὖ δύο τινὲ θεὼ φρονοῦντε ἑαυτοῖς ἐναντία στρέφειν αὐτόν ἀλλ' ὅπερ ἄρτι ἐρρήθη καὶ μόνον λοιπόν
greek_tokens: periagwgh mhte au duo tis qeos fronew eautou enantios strefw autos alla osper arti erw kai monos loipos tote men upo allos sumpodhgew qeia aitios o zaw palin epiktaomai kai lambanw aqanasia episkeuasths para o dhmiourgew tote de otan anihmi dia eautou autos eimi kata kairos afihmi toioutos wste anapalin poreuw polus periodos murias dia dh o megas eimi kai isorropos epi mikros bainw pous eimi
english_head: or again that two divinities opposed to one another turn it. The only remaining alternative is what I suggested a little while ago, that the universe is guided at one time by an extrinsic divine cause, acquiring the power of living again and receiving renewed immortality from the Creator, and at another time it is left to itself and then moves by its own motion, being left to itself at such a moment that it moves backwards through countless ages, because it is immensely large and most evenly balanced, and turns upon the smallest pivot.

greek_head: Φαίνεται γοῦν δὴ καὶ μάλα εἰκότως εἰρῆσθαι πάνθ' ὅσα διελήλυθας
greek_tokens: fainw goun dh kai mala eoikotws erw panth osos dierxomai logizomai dh sunnoew o paqos ek o nun legw o pas fhmi eimi o qaumasths aitios eimi gar oun dh outos autos o poios o o o pas fora tote men epi o nun kuklew ferw tote de epi enantios pws dh outos o metabolh hgeomai deomai o peri o
english_head: All that account of yours appears, at any rate, very reasonable. Then, in the light of what has been said, let us consider and gain understanding of the event which we said was the cause of all those wonderful portents; for it is really just this. Just what? The fact that at certain periods the universe has its present circular motion, and at other periods it revolves in the reverse direction. How was this the cause? We cannot help believing that of all the changes which take place in the heavens this reversal is

greek_head: οὐρανὸν γιγνομένων τροπῶν πασῶν εἶναι μεγίστην καὶ τελεωτάτην τροπήν
greek_tokens: ouranos gignomai tropew pas eimi megas kai teleios troph eoika goun megas toinun kai metabolh xrh nomizw gignomai tote o entos egw oikew autos kai outos eoika metabolh de megas kai polus kai pantoios sumferw ara ou oida o o zwion fus ostis xalepos anexw pws de ou fqora toinun ek anagkh tote megas sumbainw o te allos zwion kai dh kai o o anqrwpos
english_head: the greatest and most complete. It certainly seems to be so. Therefore we must also believe that at the same time the greatest changes come upon us who dwell within the heavens. That is likely too. And animals cannot well endure many great and various changes at once. That is a familiar fact, is it not? Of course. Inevitably, then, there is at that time great destruction of animals in general, and only a small part of the human race survives;

greek_head: γένος ὀλίγον τι περιλείπεται περὶ δὲ τούτους ἄλλα τε παθήματα πολλὰ καὶ θαυμαστὰ καὶ καινὰ συμπίπτει μέγιςτον
greek_tokens: genos oligos tis perileipomai peri de outos allos te paqhma polus kai qaumasths kai kainos sumpitnw megas de ode kai sunepomai o o pas aneilicis tote otan o o nun kaqisthmi enantios gignomai troph o poios os hlikia ekastos exw o zwion outos proteros men isthmi pas kai epauw pas osos eimi qnhtos epi o geraios eidon poreuw metaballw de palin epi
english_head: and the survivors have many experiences wonderful and strange, the greatest of which, a consequence of the reversal of everything at the time when the world begins to turn in the direction opposed to that of its present revolution, is this. What is that experience? First the age of all animals, whatever it was at the moment, stood still, and every mortal creature stopped growing older in appearance

greek_head: τοὐναντίον οἷον νεώτερον καὶ ἁπαλώτερον ἐφύετο καὶ τῶν μὲν πρεσβυτέρων αἱ λευκαὶ τρίχες ἐμελαίνοντο τῶν δ' αὖ
greek_tokens: enantion oios neos kai apalos efuw kai o men presbus o leukos qric melainw o de au geneiaw o pareia leainw palin epi o parerxomai oraw ekastos kaqisthmi o de hbaw o swma leainw kai mikros kaqa hmeros kai nuc ekastos gignomai palin eis o o neogenhs pais fus apeimi kata te o yuxh kai kata o swma afomoiow o de enteuqen hdh marainw komidh o pampan ekafanizw o de au biaios teleutaw en o tote xronos o o nekros swma o autos outos pasxw paqhma dia
english_head: and then reversed its growth and became, as it were, younger and more tender; the hoary locks of the old men grew dark, and bearded cheeks grew smooth again as their possessors reverted to their earlier ages, and the bodies of young men grew smoother and smaller day by day and night by night, until they became as new-born babes, to which they were likened in mind and body; and then at last they wasted away entirely and wholly disappeared. And the bodies of those who died by violence in those times quickly underwent the same changes,

greek_head: τάχους ἄδηλον ἐν ὀλίγαις ἡμέραις διεφθείρετο Γένεσις δὲ δὴ τίς τότ' ἦν ὦ ξένε ζῴων καὶ
greek_tokens: taxos adhlos en oligos hmeros diafqeirw genesis de dh tis tote eimi eimi cenos zwion kai tis tropos ek allhlwn gennaw dhlos eimi swkraths ostis o men ek allhlwn ou eimi en o tote fusis gennaw o de ghgenhs eimi pote genos legw outos eimi o kata ekeinos o xronos ek gaia palin anastrefw apomnhmoneuw de upo o hmeteros progonos o proteros e teleutaw men o proteros
english_head: were destroyed, and disappeared in a few days. But then, Stranger, how did animals come into existence in those days? How were they begotten of one another? It is clear, Socrates, that being begotten of one another was no part of the natural order of that time, but the earth-born race which, according to tradition, once existed, was the race which returned at that time out of the earth; and the memory of it was preserved by our earliest ancestors, who were born in the beginning of our period and therefore were next neighbors to the end of the previous period of the world’s revolution,

greek_head: περιφορᾷ τὸν ἑξῆς χρόνον ἐγειτόνουν τῆσδε δὲ κατ' ἀρχὰς ἐφύοντο τούτων γὰρ οὗτοι κήρυκες ἐγένονθ' ἡμῖν τῶν λόγων
greek_tokens: perifora o echs xronos geitonew ode de kata arxh efuw outos gar outos khruc gignomai egw o logos e nun upo polus ou orqos apistew o gar enteuqen oimh xrh sunnoew epomai gar eimi o o presbuths epi o o pais eimi fus ek o teleutaw au keimai de en gaia palin ekei sunisthmi kai anabiwskomai epomai o tropaomai sunanakukleomai eis enantios o genesis kai ghgenhs dh kata outos o
english_head: with no interval between. For they were to us the heralds of these stories which are nowadays unduly disbelieved by many people. For you must, I think, consider what would result. It is a natural consequence of the return of the old to childhood that those who are dead and lying in the earth take shape and come to life again, since the process of birth is reversed along with the reversal of the world’s revolution; for this reason they are inevitably earth-born,

greek_head: λόγον ἐξ ἀνάγκης φυομένους οὕτως ἔχειν τοὔνομα καὶ τὸν λόγον ὅσους μὴ θεὸς αὐτῶν εἰς ἄλλην μοῖραν ἐκόμισεν
greek_tokens: logos ek anagkh fuw outws exw onoma kai o logos osos mh qeos autos eis allos moira komizw komidh men oun outos ge epomai o emprosqen alla dh o bios os epi o kronos fhmi eimi dunamis poteros en ekeinos eimi o troph h en ode o men gar o astron te kai hlios metabolh dhlos ws en ekateros sumpitnw o troph gignomai kalos o logos sumparakolouqew o de arow
english_head: and hence arises their name and the tradition about them, except those of them whom God removed to some other fate. Certainly that follows from what preceded. But was the life in the reign of Cronus, which you mentioned, in that previous period of revolution or in ours? For evidently the change in the course of the stars and the sun takes place in both periods. You have followed my account very well.

greek_head: περὶ τοῦ πάντα αὐτόματα γίγνεσθαι τοῖς ἀνθρώποις ἥκιστα τῆς νῦν ἐστι καθεστηκυίας φορᾶς ἀλλ' ἦν καὶ τοῦτο τῆς
greek_tokens: peri o pas automatos gignomai o anqrwpos hkistos o nun eimi kaqisthmi fora alla eimi kai outos o emprosqen tote gar autos proteros o kuklhsis arxw epimeleomai oloc o qeos os de au kata topos tautos outos upo qea arxw panth eimi o o kosmos mera dialambanw kai dh kai o zwion kata genos kai agelh oios nomazw qeios dialambanw daimwn autarkhs eis pas ekastos ekastos
english_head: No, the life about which you ask, when all the fruits of the earth sprang up of their own accord for men, did not belong at all to the present period of revolution, but this also belonged to the previous one. For then, in the beginning, God ruled and supervised the whole revolution, and so again, in the same way, all the parts of the universe were divided by regions among gods who ruled them, and, moreover, the animals were distributed by species and flocks among inferior deities as divine shepherds, each of whom was in all respects the independent guardian of the creatures under his own care,

greek_head: ὢν οἷς αὐτὸς ἔνεμεν ὥστε οὔτ' ἄγριον ἦν οὐδὲν οὔτε ἀλλήλων ἐδωδαί πόλεμός τε οὐκ ἐνῆν οὐδὲ στάσις τὸ παράπαν
greek_tokens: eimi os autos enihmi wste oute agrios eimi oudeis oute allhlwn edwdh polemos te ou eneimi oude stasis o parapan allos su osos o toioutos eimi katakosmhsis epomai murios an eaw legw o de oun o anqrwpos legw automatos peri bios dia o toiosde eromai qeos enihmi autos autos epistath kaqa nun anqrwpos zwion eimi eteros qeios allos genos faulos eautou nomeuw nemw de ekeinos politeia te ou eimi oude kthsis
english_head: so that no creature was wild, nor did they eat one another, and there was no war among them, nor any strife whatsoever. To tell all the other consequences of such an order of the world would be an endless task. But the reason for the story of the spontaneous life of mankind is as follows: God himself was their shepherd, watching over them, just as man, being an animal of different and more divine nature than the rest, now tends the lower species of animals. And under his care there were no states,

greek_head: γυναικῶν καὶ παίδων ἐκ γῆς γὰρ ἀνεβιώσκοντο πάντες οὐδὲν μεμνημένοι τῶν πρόσθεν ἀλλὰ τὰ μὲν τοιαῦτα ἀπῆν
greek_tokens: gunaikow kai pais ek gaia gar anabiwskomai pas oudeis mimnhskw o prosqen alla o men toioutos apeimi pas karpos de afqonos exw apo te dendron kai polus ulh allos ou upo gewrgia fuw alla automatos anadidwmi o gaia gumnos de kai astrwtos quraulew o polus nemw o gar o wra autos alupon kerannumi malakos de eunh exw anaapouw ek
english_head: nor did men possess wives or children; for they all came to life again out of the earth, with no recollection of their former lives. So there were no states or families, but they had fruits in plenty from the trees and other plants, which the earth furnished them of its own accord, without help from agriculture. And they lived for the most part in the open air, without clothing or bedding; for the climate was tempered for their comfort, and the abundant grass that grew up out of the earth furnished them soft couches.

greek_head: γῆς πόας ἀφθόνου τὸν δὴ βίον ὦ Σώκρατες ἀκούεις μὲν τὸν τῶν ἐπὶ Κρόνου τόνδε δ' ὃν λόγος ἐπὶ Διὸς εἶναι τὸν
greek_tokens: gaia poa afqonos o dh bios eimi swkraths akouw men o o epi kronos ode de os logos epi zeus eimi o nuni pareimi autos aisqanomai krinw de autos o eudaimwn ara an dunamai te kai eqelw oudamos boulomai dhta egw su tropos tis diakrinw panu men oun ei men toinun e trofimos o kronos pareimi autos outws polus sxolazw kai dunamis pros o mh monos anqrwpos alla kai qhraw dia logos dunamai suggignomai
english_head: That, Socrates, was the life of men in the reign of Cronus; but the life of the present age, which is said to be the age of Zeus, you know by your own experience. Would you be able and willing to decide which of them is the more blessed? Certainly not. Shall I, then, make some sort of a judgement for you? Do so, by all means. Well, then, if the foster children of Cronus, having all this leisure and the ability to converse not only with human beings but also with beasts,

greek_head: κατεχρῶντο τούτοις σύμπασιν ἐπὶ φιλοσοφίαν μετά τε θηρίων καὶ μετ' ἀλλήλων ὁμιλοῦντες καὶ πυνθανόμενοι
greek_tokens: kataxraomai outos sumpas epi filosofia meta te qhraw kai meta allhlwn omilew kai punqanomai para pas fusis ei tis tis idios dunamis exw aisqanomai tis diaforos o allos eis sunagurmos fronhsis eukritos ostis o nun e tote murios pros eudaimonia diaferw ei de empiplhmi sitos adhn kai poths dialegw pros allhlwn kai o qhrion muqos oios dh kai o nun peri autos
english_head: made full use of all these opportunities with a view to philosophy, talking with the animals and with one another and learning from every creature that, through possession of some peculiar power he may have had in any respect beyond his fellows perceptions tending towards an increase of wisdom, it would be easy to decide that the people of those old times were immeasurably happier than those of our epoch. Or if they merely ate and drank till they were full and gossiped with each other and the animals, telling such stories as are even now told about them,

greek_head: λέγονται καὶ τοῦτο ὥς γε κατὰ τὴν ἐμὴν δόξαν ἀποφήνασθαι καὶ μάλ' εὔκριτον ὅμως δ' οὖν ταῦτα μὲν ἀφῶμεν ἕως ἂν
greek_tokens: legw kai outos os ge kata o emos doca apofainw kai mala eukritos omws de oun outos men afihmi ews an egw mhnuths tis ikanos fainw poteros e tote o epiqumia exw peri te episthmh kai o o logos xreia e de eneka o muqos ageirw outos lekteos ina o meta outos eis o prosqen perainw epei gar pas outos xronos teleiow kai metabolh dew gignomai kai
english_head: in that case, too, it would, in my opinion, be very easy to reach a decision. However, let us pass those matters by, so long as there is no one capable of reporting to us what the desires of the people in those days were in regard to knowledge and the employment of speech. The reason why we revived this legend must be told, in order that we may get ahead afterwards. For when the time of all those conditions was accomplished and the change was to take place and all the earth-born race had at length been used up,

greek_head: δὴ καὶ τὸ γήινον ἤδη πᾶν ἀνήλωτο γένος πάσας ἑκάστης τῆς ψυχῆς τὰς γενέσεις ἀποδεδωκυίας ὅσα ἦν ἑκάστῃ προςταχθὲν
greek_tokens: dh kai o ghinos hdh pas analiskw genos passw ekastos o yuxazw o genesis apodidwmi osos eimi ekastos prosstazw tosoutos eis gaia sperma piptw tote dh o pas o men kubernhths oios phdalion oiac afihmi eis o eautou periwph afisthmi o de dh kosmos palin anastrefw meiromai te kai sumfutos epiqumia pas oun e kata o topos sunarxw o megas daimwn qeos gignwskw hdh o gignomai afihmi au o mera o
english_head: since every soul had fulfilled all its births by falling into the earth as seed its prescribed number of times, then the helmsman of the universe dropped the tiller and withdrew to his place of outlook, and fate and innate desire made the earth turn backwards. So, too, all the gods who share, each in his own sphere, the rule of the Supreme Spirit, promptly perceiving what was taking place, let go the parts of the world which were under their care.

greek_head: κόσμου τῆς αὑτῶν ἐπιμελείας ὁ δὲ μεταστρεφόμενος καὶ συμβάλλων ἀρχῆς τε καὶ τελευτῆς ἐναντίαν ὁρμὴν ὁρμηθείς

english_head: And as the universe was turned back and there came the shock of collision, as the beginning and the end rushed in opposite directions, it produced a great earthquake within itself and caused a new destruction of all sorts of living creatures. But after that, when a sufficient time had elapsed, there was rest now from disturbance and confusion, calm followed the earthquakes, and the world went on its own accustomed course in orderly fashion, exercising care and rule

greek_head: αὐτὸς τῶν ἐν αὑτῷ τε καὶ ἑαυτοῦ τὴν τοῦ δημιουργοῦ καὶ πατρὸς ἀπομνημονεύων διδαχὴν εἰς δύναμιν κατ' ἀρχὰς
greek_tokens: autos o en eautou te kai eautou o o dhmiourgew kai pathr apomnhmoneuw didaxh eis dunamis kata arxh men oun akribhs apotelew teleutaw de amblus outos de autos o swmatoeidhs o sugkrasis aitios o o pala pote fusis suntrofos ostis polus eimi metexw atacia prin eis o nun kosmos afikneomai para men gar o suntiqhmi pas kalos ktaomai para de o emprosqen
english_head: over itself and all within itself, and remembering and practising the teachings of the Creator and Father to the extent of its power, at first more accurately and at last more carelessly; and the reason for this was the material element in its composition, because this element, which was inherent in the primeval nature, was infected with great disorder before the attainment of the existing orderly universe. For from its Composer the universe has received only good things; but from its previous condition it retains in itself and creates in the animals all the elements of harshness and injustice

greek_head: ἕξεως ὅσα χαλεπὰ καὶ ἄδικα ἐν οὐρανῷ γίγνεται ταῦτα ἐξ ἐκείνης αὐτός τε ἔχει καὶ τοῖς ζῴοις ἐναπεργάζεται μετὰ
greek_tokens: ecis osos xalepos kai adikos en ouranos gignomai outos ek ekeinos autos te exis kai o zwion enapergazomai meta men oun o kubernhths o zwion trefw en eautou mikros men flauros megas de entiktw agaqos xwrizw de ekeinos o eggus xronos aei o afesis kallista pas diagw proeimi de o xronos kai lhqh eggignomai en autos mallon kai dunasteuw o o palaios anarmostia
english_head: which have their origin in the heavens. Now as long as the world was nurturing the animals within itself under the guidance of the Pilot, it produced little evil and great good; but in becoming separated from him it always got on most excellently during the time immediately after it was let go, but as time went on and it grew forgetful, the ancient condition of disorder prevailed more and more

greek_head: πάθος τελευτῶντος δὲ ἐξανθεῖ τοῦ χρόνου καὶ σμικρὰ μὲν τἀγαθά πολλὴν δὲ τὴν τῶν ἐναντίων κρᾶσιν ἐπεγκεραννύμενος

english_head: and towards the end of the time reached its height, and the universe, mingling but little good with much of the opposite sort, was in danger of destruction for itself and those within it. Therefore at that moment God, who made the order of the universe, perceived that it was in dire trouble, and fearing that it might founder in the tempest of confusion and sink in the boundless sea of diversity,

greek_head: πόντον δύῃ πάλιν ἔφεδρος αὐτοῦ τῶν πηδαλίων γιγνόμενος τὰ νοσήσαντα καὶ λυθέντα ἐν τῇ καθ' ἑαυτὸν προτέρᾳ περιόδῳ
greek_tokens: pontos duh palin efedros autos o phdalion gignomai o nosew kai luw en o kaqa eautou proteros periodos strefw kosmew te kai epanorqow aqanatos autos kai aghraos apergazomai outos men oun telos apas eromai o de epi o o basileus apodeicis ikanos ek o prosqen aptw o logos strefw gar au o kosmos o epi o nun genesis odos o o hlikia au palin isthmi kai kainos enantios apodidwmi o tote o men gar upo mikroths oligos deon afanizw o zwion aucanw o de ek gaia neogenhs swma polia fus palin apoqnhskw eis gaia kateimi kai allos te pas metaballw
english_head: he took again his place as its helmsman, reversed whatever had become unsound and unsettled in the previous period when the world was left to itself, set the world in order, restored it and made it immortal and ageless. So now the whole tale is told; but for our purpose of exhibiting the nature of the king it will be enough to revert to the earlier part of the story. For when the universe was turned again into the present path of generation, the age of individuals came again to a stop, and that led to new processes, the reverse of those which had gone before. For the animals which had grown so small as almost to disappear grew larger, and those newly born from the earth with hoary hair died and passed below the earth again. And all other things changed,

greek_head: ἀπομιμούμενα καὶ συνακολουθοῦντα τῷ τοῦ παντὸς παθήματι καὶ δὴ καὶ τὸ τῆς κυήσεως καὶ γεννήσεως καὶ τροφῆς μίμημα
greek_tokens: apomimeomai kai sunakolouqew o o pas paqhma kai dh kai o o kuhsis kai gennhsis kai trefw mimhma sunepomai o pas upo anagkh ou gar ecesti etai en gaia dia eteros sunisthmi fuw zwion alla kaqa o kosmos prostassw autokratwr eimi o eautou poreia outws dh kata tautos kai o meros autos dia eautou kaqa osos oios su eimi fuw te kai genna kai trefw prostassw upo o
english_head: imitating the condition of the universe and conforming to it, and so too pregnancy and birth and nurture necessarily imitated and conformed to the rest; for no living creature could any longer come into being by the union of other elements, but just as the universe was ordered to be the ruler of its own course, so in the same way the parts were ordered, so far as they could, to grow and beget and give nourishment of themselves under the same guidance.

greek_head: ὁμοίας ἀγωγῆς οὗ δὲ ἕνεκα ὁ λόγος ὥρμηκε πᾶς ἐπ' αὐτῷ νῦν ἐσμὲν ἤδη περὶ μὲν γὰρ τῶν ἄλλων θηρίων πολλὰ ἂν
