english_head: And now we have come at last to the point for the sake of which this whole discourse was begun. For much might be said, and at great length, about the other animals, their previous forms and the causes of their several changes; but about mankind there is less to say and it is more to our purpose. For men, deprived of the care of the deity who had possessed and tended us, since most of the beasts who were by nature unfriendly had grown fierce, and they themselves were feeble and unprotected, were ravaged by the beasts

greek_head: αὐτῶν καὶ ἔτ' ἀμήχανοι καὶ ἄτεχνοι κατὰ τοὺς πρώτους ἦσαν χρόνους ἅτε τῆς μὲν αὐτομάτης τροφῆς ἐπιλελοιπυίας
greek_tokens: autos kai etai amhxanos kai atexnos kata o proteros eimi xronos ate o men automatos trefw epileipw porizw de ou efisthmi pw dia o mhdeis autos xreia proteros anagkazw ek outos pas en megas aporia eimi oqen dh o pala legw para qea dwron egw dwreomai meta anagkh didaxh kai paideusis pur men para promhqeus texnh de para hfaistos
english_head: and were in the first ages still without resources or skill; the food which had formerly offered itself freely had failed them, and they did not yet know how to provide for themselves, because no necessity had hitherto compelled them. On all these accounts they were in great straits; and that is the reason why the gifts of the gods that are told of in the old traditions were given us with the needful information and instruction,—fire by Prometheus, the arts by Hephaestus and the goddess who is his fellow-artisan, seeds and plants by other deities.

greek_head: καὶ τῆς συντέχνου σπέρματα δὲ αὖ καὶ φυτὰ παρ' ἄλλων καὶ πάνθ' ὁπόσα τὸν ἀνθρώπινον βίον συγκατεσκεύακεν
greek_tokens: kai o suntexnos sperma de au kai futas para allos kai panth oposos o anqrwpinos bios sugkataskeuazw ek outos gignomai epei o men ek qea osper erw nundi o epimeleia epileipw anqrwpos dia eautou te dew o te diagwgh kai o epimeleia autos eautou exw kaqa oloc o kosmos os summimeomai kai sunepomai o aei xronos nun men outws tote de ekeinos zaw
english_head: And from these has arisen all that constitutes human life, since, as I said a moment ago, the care of the gods had failed men and they had to direct their own lives and take care of themselves, like the whole universe, which we imitate and follow through all time, being born and living now in our present manner and in that other epoch in the other manner.

greek_head: τε καὶ φυόμεθα καὶ τὸ μὲν δὴ τοῦ μύθου τέλος ἐχέτω χρήσιμον δὲ αὐτὸν ποιησόμεθα πρὸς τὸ κατιδεῖν ὅσον ἡμάρτομεν
greek_tokens: te kai fuw kai o men dh o muqos telos exw xrhsimos de autos poiew pros o kateidon osos amartanw apofainw o basilikos te kai politikos en o prosqen logos pws oun kai posos amarthma fhmi eimi gignomai egw o men braxus o de mala gennaios kai polus megas kai pleos h tote pws ostis men erwtaw o ek o nun perifora kai genesis basileus kai politikos o ek o enantios periodos
english_head: So, then, let our tale be finished; but we will turn it to account for opening our eyes to the great error we made in the exposition of the king and the statesman in our earlier discussion. How, then, did we err, and what is the great error you say we have committed? In one way we made a comparatively slight error, in another a very important one, much greater and more far-reaching than the first. How did we do that? When we were asked about the king and the statesman of the present movement of the world and mode of generation, we told of the shepherd of the human flock in the time of the reverse movement,

greek_head: ποιμένα τῆς τότε ἀνθρωπίνης ἀγέλης εἴπομεν καὶ ταῦτα θεὸν ἀντὶ θνητοῦ ταύτῃ μὲν πάμπολυ παρηνέχθημεν ὅτι δὲ
greek_tokens: poimhn o tote anqrwpinos agelh eipon kai outos qeos anti qnhtos outos men pampolus paraferw ostis de sumpas o polis arxw autos apofainw ostis de tropos ou dieipon outos de au o men legw alhqhs ou meis oloc ge oude safhs erw dio kai braxus h kata ekeinos amartanw alhqhs deomai toinun o tropos ws eoika diorizw o arxh o polis outws teleios o politikos egw erw prosdokaw kalos
english_head: and he was a god, not a man, besides. That was a very great error. Then when we declared that he was ruler of the whole state, but did not fully tell in what manner he ruled, what we said was true, though it was not complete nor clear, and therefore our error was less in this case than in the other. True. Apparently, then, we must expect a complete description of the statesman only when we have defined the manner of his rule over the state. Very good.

greek_head: Διὰ ταῦτα μὴν καὶ τὸν μῦθον παρεθέμεθα ἵνα ἐνδείξαιτο περὶ τῆς ἀγελαιοτροφίας μὴ μόνον ὡς πάντες αὐτῆς
greek_tokens: dia outos meis kai o muqos paratiqhmi ina endeiknumi peri o agelaiotrofia mh monos ws pas autos amfisbhtew o zhtew o nun alla ekeinos autos enarghs eidon os proshkw monos kata o paradeigma poimhn te kai boukolos o anqrwpinos epimeleia exw trefw outos monos aciow o prosrhma orqos oimh de ge eimi swkraths outos men eti megas h
english_head: And this is why I introduced the myth, not only in order to show that all men compete for the care of the flock with him whom we are now seeking, but also that we may more clearly see him who alone ought to have the care of human beings as shepherds and neatherds care for their flocks and herds, and therefore alone deserves to be honored with that appellation. Quite right. I think, Socrates, that the form of the divine shepherd is greater

greek_head: κατὰ βασιλέα εἶναι τὸ σχῆμα τὸ τοῦ θείου νομέως τοὺς δ' ἐνθάδε νῦν ὄντας πολιτικοὺς τοῖς ἀρχομένοις ὁμοίους τε
greek_tokens: kata basileus eimi o sxhma o o qeios nomeus o de enqade nun eimi politikos o arxw omoios te eimi mallon polus o fusis kai paraplhsios paideia metalambanw kai trefw pantws pou zhthteos ge meis oudeis an eimi oute hsswn oute mallon eiqe outws eite ekeinos fuw pws gar ou ode dh palin epanerxomai os gar fhmi autepitaktikos men eimi texnh epi zwion ou meis idios ge alla
english_head: than that of the king, whereas the statesmen who now exist here are by nature much more like their subjects, with whom they share much more nearly the same breeding and education. Certainly. And yet they would have to be investigated with precisely the same care, whether their nature be like that of their subjects or like that of the divine shepherd. Of course. Then let us go back to this point: the art which we said gave its own orders and had to do with living beings,

greek_head: κοινῇ τὴν ἐπιμέλειαν ἔχουσαν καὶ προσείπομεν δὴ τότε εὐθὺς ἀγελαιοτροφικήν μέμνησαι γάρ
greek_tokens: koinh o epimeleia exw kai proseipon dh tote euqus agelaiotrofikos mimnhskw gar nai outos toinun ph diamartanw o gar politikos oudamos sullambanw oude onomazw alla egw elaunw kata o onomasia ekfeugw pws o o agelh ekastos trefw o men allos pou pas meteimi nomeus o politikos de ou meteimi epiferw
english_head: but had charge of them not singly but in common, and which we at once called the art of the herdsman,—do you remember? Yes. Well, it was in connection with that, somewhere, that we made our mistake; for we never included or named the statesman; unobserved by us he slipped out of our nomenclature. How so? All the other herdsmen have this in common that they feed their respective herds; but the statesman does not, yet we gave him the name of herdsman, when we ought to have given him one

greek_head: τοὔνομα δέον τῶν κοινῶν ἐπενεγκεῖν τι σύμπασιν Ἀληθῆ λέγεις εἴπερ ἐτύγχανέ γε ὄν
greek_tokens: onoma deon o koinos epiferw tis sumpas alhqhs legw eiper tugxanw ge eimi pws de ou eimi o ge qerapeuw pou pas koinos mhdeis diorizw trefw mhde tis allos pragmateia alla h tis agelaiokomikos h qerapeutikos h kai tis epimelhtikos autos onomazw ws kata pas ecesti perikaluptw kai o politikos ama o allos epei dew outos shmainw o logos
english_head: which is common to them all. True, if there were such a name. Is not caring for herds common to them all, with no especial mention of feeding or any other activity? If we called it an art of tending herds or caring for them or managing them, as all herdsmen do, we could wrap up the statesman with the rest, since the argument showed that we ought to do so. Quite right; but how would the next

greek_head: Ὀρθῶς ἀλλ' ἡ μετὰ τοῦτο διαίρεσις αὖ τίνα τρόπον ἐγίγνετ' ἄν
greek_tokens: orqos alla o meta outos diairesis au tis tropos gignomai an kata tautos kaqa o emprosqen diairew o agelaiotrofikos pezos te kai apthn kai ameiktos te kai akeratos o autos an pou outos diairew kai o agelaiokomikos o te nun kai o epi kronos basileios perilambanw an eimi omoios en o logos fainw zhtew de au tis o meta outos dhlos ostis legw outws o o agelaiokomikos
english_head: division be made? Just as we divided the art of feeding herds before by distinguishing between those that go on foot and the winged, and the unmixed breeds and the hornless, we might divide the art of tending herds by these same distinctions, embracing in the word both the kingship of the present time and that of the time of Cronus. Evidently; but again I wonder what the next step is. It Is clear that if we had used the word tending herds,

greek_head: ὀνόματος οὐκ ἄν ποτε ἐγένεθ' ἡμῖν τό τινας ἀμφισβητεῖν ὡς οὐδ' ἐπιμέλεια τὸ παράπαν ἐστίν ὥσπερ τότε δικαίως
greek_tokens: onoma ou an pote gignomai egw o tis amfisbhtew ws oude epimeleia o parapan eimi wsper tote dikaios amfiamfisbhtew mhdeis eimi texnh en egw acios outos o qreptikos prosrhma ei de oun tis eaw polus proteros autos kai mallon proshkw h tis o basilh orqos epimeleia de ge anqrwpinos sumpas koinwnia oudeis an eqelw eteros mallon kai proteros o basilikos
english_head: we should never have met with the contention that there is no caring for them at all in statesmanship, though the earlier contention was justified that there is no art in the case of human beings that deserves the name of feeding, and if there be such an art, it belongs much more to many others than to the king. Quite right. But no other art would advance a stronger claim than that of kingship to be the art of caring for the whole human community

greek_head: φάναι καὶ κατὰ πάντων ἀνθρώπων ἀρχῆς εἶναι τέχνη Λέγεις ὀρθῶς
greek_tokens: fainw kai kata pas anqrwpos arxh eimi texnh legw orqos meta outos de ge eimi swkraths ara ennoew ostis pros autos dh o telos suxnos au diamartanw o poios ode ws ara ei kai dianoeomai ostis malista o dipous agelh eimi tis qreptikos texnh oudeis tis mallon egw dew basilikos autos euqus kai politikos ws apotelew prosagoreuw tis meis proteros men o legw onoma metaskeuwreomai
english_head: and ruling all mankind. You are right. And after all this, Socrates, do we see that another great error was committed at the very end? What was it? Why, it was this: No matter how strong our belief that there was an art of feeding the biped herd, we ought not to have called it kingship and statecraft on the spot, as if it were all quite settled. What ought we to have done, then? In the first place, as we said, we ought to have remodelled the name,

greek_head: πρὸς τὴν ἐπιμέλειαν μᾶλλον προσαγαγόντας ἢ τὴν τροφήν ἔπειτα ταύτην τέμνειν οὐ γὰρ σμικρὰς ἂν ἔχοι
greek_tokens: pros o epimeleia mallon prosagw h o trofh epeita outos temnw ou gar mikros an exw tmhsis eti poa h te o qew an pou diairew nomehs xwris kai o anqrwpinos epimelhths orqos auqis de ge o aponemw epimelhtikos dixa temnw anagkaion eimi tis o biaios te kai ekousios tis dh
english_head: making it denote care, rather than feeding, and then we ought to have divided the art, for it may still admit of not unimportant divisions. What are they? There is one by which we might have divided the divine shepherd from the human caretaker. Quite right. And again it was essential that the art of caretaking thus isolated and assigned to man be divided into two parts. On what line of division? On that of compulsory and voluntary. Why is that? Because this was about the point at which we made our mistake before;

greek_head: Καὶ ταύτῃ που τὸ πρότερον ἁμαρτάνοντες εὐηθέστερα τοῦ δέοντος εἰς ταὐτὸν βασιλέα καὶ τύραννον συνέθεμεν
greek_tokens: kai outos pou o proteros amartanw euhqhs o deon eis tautos basileus kai turannos suneqemen anomoios eimi autos te kai o o arxh ekateros tropos alhqhs nun de ge palin epanorqow kaqa eipon o anqrwpinos epimelhtikos dixa diairw o biaios te kai ekousios panu men oun kai o men ge pou o biaios turannikos o de ekousios kai ekousios dipous agelaiokomikos zwion proseipon politikos o exw au texnh outos kai epimeleia ontws eimi basileus kai politikos apofainw
english_head: we were more simple-minded than we should have been, and we put the king and the tyrant together, whereas they and their respective modes of ruling are quite unlike. True. But now shall we, as I said, correct ourselves and divide the care of humanity into two parts, by the criterion of the compulsory and the voluntary? By all means. And if we call the art of those who use compulsion tyrannical or something of the sort and the voluntary care of voluntary bipeds political, may we not declare that he who possesses this latter art of caretaking is really the true king and statesman?

greek_head: Καὶ κινδυνεύει γε ὦ ξένε τελέως ἂν ἡμῖν οὕτως ἔχειν ἡ περὶ τὸν πολιτικὸν ἀπόδειξις
greek_tokens: kai kinduneuw ge eimi cenos teleios an egw outws exw o peri o politikos apodeicis kalos an eimi swkraths egw exw deomai de mh sos monos outos alla amos meta sew koinh sundokew nun de kata ge o emos oupw fainw teleios o basileus egw sxhma exw alla kaqa andriantopoios para kairos eniote speudw plew kai megas o deon ekastos o
english_head: Well, Stranger, it looks as though our account of the statesman were complete now. That would be a fine thing for us, Socrates. But not you alone must think so; I must think so, too, in agreement with you. As a matter of fact, however, in my opinion our figure of the king is not yet perfect, but like statue-makers who sometimes in their misapplied enthusiasm make too numerous and too large additions and thus delay the completion of their several works,

greek_head: ἔργων ἐπεμβαλλόμενοι βραδύνουσι καὶ νῦν ἡμεῖς ἵνα δὴ πρὸς τῷ ταχὺ καὶ μεγαλοπρεπῶς δηλώσαιμεν τὸ τῆς ἔμπροσθεν
greek_tokens: ergnumi epemballw bradunw kai nun egw ina dh pros o taxus kai megaloprephs dhlow o o emprosqen amarthma diecodos o basileus nomizw prepw megas paradeigma poiew qaumastos ogkos araomai o muqos megas o deon anagkazw autos meros prosxraomai dio makros o apodeicis poiew kai pantws o muqos telos ou epeqemen alla atexnos o
english_head: we too, at this time, wishing to make quick progress, and also to make clear in a grand style the error of our previous course, and, moreover, fancying that the use of great illustrations was proper in the case of a king, have taken up a marvellous mass of myth and have consequently been obliged to use a greater part of it than we should. So we have made our discourse too long and after all have never made an end of the tale,

greek_head: λόγος ἡμῖν ὥσπερ ζῷον τὴν ἔξωθεν μὲν περιγραφὴν ἔοικεν ἱκανῶς ἔχειν τὴν δὲ οἷον τοῖς φαρμάκοις καὶ τῇ συγκράσει
greek_tokens: logos egw wsper zwion o ecwqen men perigrafh eoika ikanos exw o de oios o farmakon kai o sugkrasis o xrwma enargeia ou apolambanw pw grafeus de kai sumpas xeirourgia lecis kai logos dhlow pas zwion mallon prepw o dunamai epomai o de allos dia xeirourgia outos men orqos oph de egw oupw fhmi ikanos erw dhlow
english_head: but our talk, just like a picture of a living creature, seems to have a good enough outline, but not yet to have received the clearness that comes from pigments and the blending of colors. And yet it is more fitting to portray any living being by speech and argument than by painting or any handicraft whatsoever to persons who are able to follow argument; but to others it is better to do it by means of works of craftsmanship. That is true; but explain wherein you think our exposition is still deficient.

greek_head: Χαλεπόν ὦ δαιμόνιε μὴ παραδείγμασι χρώμενον ἱκανῶς ἐνδείκνυσθαί τι τῶν μειζόνων κινδυνεύει γὰρ ἡμῶν

english_head: It is difficult, my dear fellow, to set forth any of the greater ideas, except by the use of examples; for it would seem that each of us knows everything that he knows as if in a dream and then again, when he is as it were awake, knows nothing of it all. What do you mean by that? I seem at present in absurd fashion to have touched upon our experience in regard to knowledge. In what respect? Why, my friend, the very example I employ requires another example.

greek_head: Τί οὖν λέγε μηδὲν ἐμοῦ γε ἕνεκα ἀποκνῶν Λεκτέον ἐπειδὴ καὶ σύ γε ἕτοιμος ἀκολουθεῖν
greek_tokens: tis oun legos mhdeis egw ge eneka apoknew lekteos epei kai su ge etoimos akolouqew o gar pou pais oida otan arti gramma empeiros gignomai o poios ostis o stoixeion ekastos en o braxus kai radios o sullabh ikanos diaisqanomai kai alhqhs frazw peri ekeinos dunatos gignomai
english_head: Indeed? What is it? Don’t hesitate to tell on my account. I will tell, since you on your part are prepared to listen. We know that children, when they are just getting some knowledge of letters— Well? Recognize the several letters well enough in the short and easy syllables, and can make correct statements about them. Yes, of course.

greek_head: Πῶς γὰρ οὔ Ταὐτὰ δέ γε ταῦτα ἐν ἄλλαις ἀμφιγνοοῦντες πάλιν

english_head: And then again in other syllables they are in doubt about those same letters, and err in opinion and speech about them. Yes, certainly. Would not the easiest and best way to lead them to the letters which they do not yet know be this? What? To lead them first to those cases in which they had correct opinions about these same letters and then to lead them and set them beside the groups which they did not yet recognize

english_head: and by comparing them to show that their nature is the same in both combinations alike, and to continue until the letters about which their opinions are correct have been shown in juxtaposition with all those of which they are ignorant. Being shown in this way they become examples and bring it about that every letter is in all syllables always called by the same name, either by differentiation from the other letters,

greek_head: δὲ ταὐτὸν ὡς ταὐτὸν ἀεὶ κατὰ ταὐτὰ ἑαυτῷ προσαγορεύεσθαι Παντάπασι μὲν οὖν

english_head: in case it is different, or because it is the same. Certainly. Is this, then, a satisfactory definition, that an example is formed when that which is the same in some second unconnected thing is rightly conceived and compared with the first, so that the two together form one true idea? Evidently. Can we wonder, then, that our soul, whose nature involves it in the same uncertainty about the letters or elements of all things,

greek_head: φύσει περὶ τὰ τῶν πάντων στοιχεῖα πεπονθυῖα τοτὲ μὲν ὑπ' ἀληθείας περὶ ἓν ἕκαστον ἔν τισι συνίσταται τοτὲ δὲ περὶ

english_head: is sometimes in some cases firmly grounded in the truth about every detail, and again in other cases is all at sea about everything, and somehow or other has correct opinions about some combinations, and then again is ignorant of the same things when they are transferred to the long and difficult syllables of life? Surely we need not wonder at that. No; for could anyone, my friend, who begins with false opinion, ever attain to even a small

greek_head: δόξης ψευδοῦς ἐπί τι τῆς ἀληθείας καὶ μικρὸν μέρος ἀφικόμενος κτήσασθαι φρόνησιν
greek_tokens: doca yeudhs epi tis o alhqeia kai mikros meros afikneomai ktaomai fronhsis sxedon oudamos oukoun outos ei outos fuw oudeis dh plhmmelew an egw te kai su proteros men epixeirew oloc paradeigma eidon o fus en mikros kata meros allos paradeigma meta de outos mellw epi o o basileus megas eimi tautos eidos apo elasswn ferw poqen dia paradeigma epixeirew au o o kata polis qerapeia texnh gnwrizw ina upar anti oneiros egw gignomai panu men oun orqos
english_head: part of truth and acquire wisdom? No; it is hardly possible. Then if this is the case, would it be a bad thing if you and I first tried to see in another small and partial example the nature of example in general, with the intention of transferring afterwards the same figurative method from lesser things to the most exalted eminence of the king, and trying by means of an example to become acquainted in a scientific way with the management of states, in order that this may be waking knowledge for us, not dream knowledge? That is a very good idea.

greek_head: Πάλιν δὴ τὸν ἔμπροσθε λόγον ἀναληπτέον ὡς ἐπειδὴ τῷ βασιλικῷ γένει τῆς περὶ τὰς πόλεις ἐπιμελείας

english_head: Then we must take up our former argument again, and since there are countless others who contend that they, rather than the royal class, have the care of states, we must accordingly remove all these and isolate the king; and, as we said, to accomplish this we need an example. Certainly. What example could we apply which is very small, but has the same kind of activity as statesmanship and would enable us satisfactorily to discover that which we seek?

greek_head: ζητούμενον βούλει πρὸς Διός ὦ Σώκρατες εἰ μή τι πρόχειρον ἕτερον ἔχομεν ἀλλ' οὖν τήν γε ὑφαντικὴν προελώμεθα
greek_tokens: zhtew boulomai pros zeus eimi swkraths ei mh tis proxeiron eteros exw alla oun o ge ufantikos proairew kai outos ei dokew mh pas apoxrhsis gar isos o peri o ek o erion ufasma taxa gar an egw kai outos o meros autos marturew proairew o boulomai tis gar ou tis dhta ou kaqa en o emprosqen temnw mera mera ekastos diairew kai nun peri ufantikos
english_head: What do you say, Socrates, if we have nothing else at hand, to taking at random the art of weaving, and, if you please, not the whole of that? For I fancy the art of weaving wool will be enough; if we choose that part only it will probably furnish us with the illustration we desire. Agreed. Then just as we divided each subject before by cutting off parts from parts,

greek_head: ταὐτὸν τοῦτ' ἐδράσαμεν καὶ κατὰ δύναμιν ὅτι μάλιστα διὰ βραχέων ταχὺ πάντ' ἐπελθόντες πάλιν ἤλθομεν ἐπὶ τὸ νῦν
greek_tokens: tautos outos draw kai kata dunamis ostis malista dia braxea taxus panth eperxomai palin erxomai epi o nun xrhsimos pws legw autos o diecodos apokrisls su poiew kallista eipon eimi toinun pas egw oposos dhmiourgew kai ktaomai o men eneka o poiew tis o de o mh pasxw amunthrion kai o amunthrion o men alecifarmakon kai
english_head: why not now apply the same process to the art of weaving and, by going through all the steps as briefly as we possibly can, arrive quickly at that which serves our present purpose? What do you mean? I will answer you by actually going through the process. Excellent Well, then, all things which we make or acquire are for the sake of doing something or else they are for defence against suffering; and of the defensive class some are spells and antidotes, both divine and human, and some are material defences;

greek_head: θεῖα καὶ ἀνθρώπινα τὰ δὲ προβλήματα τῶν δὲ προβλημάτων τὰ μὲν πρὸς τὸν πόλεμον ὁπλίσματα τὰ δὲ φράγματα καὶ

english_head: and of the material defences some are equipment for war and some are protections; and of protections some are screens and some are defences against heat and cold; and such defences are either shelters or coverings; and coverings are either rugs to spread under us or wrappings to wrap round us; and wrappings are either all of one piece or composed of several pieces;

greek_head: τὰ μὲν τρητά τὰ δὲ ἄνευ τρήσεως συνδετά καὶ τῶν ἀτρήτων τὰ μὲν νεύρινα φυτῶν ἐκ γῆς τὰ δὲ τρίχινα τῶν δὲ
greek_tokens: o men trhtos o de aneu trhsis sundeta kai o atrhtos o men neurinos futon ek gaia o de trixinos o de trixinos o men udwr kai gaia kollhths o de autos eautou sundeta outos dh o ek o eautou sundeomai ergazomai amunthrion kai skepasma o men onoma imation kalew o de o imation malista epimeleomai
english_head: and of the composite garments some are stitched and others put together without stitching; and of the unstitched some are made of the fibres of plants and some are of hair; and of those made with hair some are stuck together with liquids and cement and others are fastened without any such extraneous matter. Now to these protective coverings made of materials fastened without extraneous matter we give the name of clothes;

greek_head: τέχνην ὥσπερ τότε τὴν τῆς πόλεως πολιτικὴν εἴπομεν οὕτω καὶ νῦν ταύτην προσείπωμεν ἀπ' αὐτοῦ τοῦ πράγματος
greek_tokens: texnh wsper tote o o polis politikos eipon outws kai nun outos proseipon apo autos o pragma imatiourgikos fhmi de kai ufantikos osos epi o o imation ergasia megas eimi morion mhdeis diaferw plhn onoma outos o imatiourgikos kaqa akeomai tote o basilikos o politikos orqos ge o meta outos dh sullogizomai ostis o imation
english_head: and just as we called the art statecraft which was concerned with the state, so we shall call the art concerned with clothes, from the nature of its activity, clothes-making, shall we not? And may we say further that weaving, in so far as the greatest part of it is, as we saw, concerned with the making of clothes, differs in name only from this art of clothes-making, just as in the other case the royal art differed from statecraft? That is perfectly correct. Let us next reflect that a person might think that this description of the art of weaving was satisfactory,

greek_head: ὑφαντικὴν οὕτω ῥηθεῖσάν τις τάχ' ἂν ἱκανῶς εἰρῆσθαι δόξειεν μὴ δυνάμενος συννοεῖν ὅτι τῶν μὲν ἐγγὺς συνεργῶν οὔπω
greek_tokens: ufantikos outws erw tis taxa an ikanos erw dokew mh dunamai sunnoew ostis o men eggus sunergew oupw diaorizw polus de eteros suggenhs apomerizw poios eipon suggenhs ou epomai o legw ws fainw palin oun eoika epanitaw arxw apo teleutaw ei gar sunnoew o oikeioths o men diatemnw apo autos nundi o o strwma sunqesis peribolh xwrizw kai upoboleus manqanw
english_head: because he cannot understand that it has not yet been distinguished from the closely co-operative arts, though it has been separated from many other kindred arts. What kindred arts? You do not seem to have followed what I have been saying; so I think I had better go back again and begin at the end. For if you understand what I mean by kinship, we distinguished from clothing something akin to it a moment ago when we separated rugs from it by the distinction between spreading under and wrapping round. I understand.

greek_head: Καὶ μὴν τὴν ἐκ τῶν λίνων καὶ σπάρτων καὶ πάντων ὁπόσα φυτῶν ἄρτι νεῦρα κατὰ λόγον εἴπομεν δημιουργίαν

english_head: And we removed the entire manufacture of cloth made from flax and broom-cords and all that we just now called vegetable fibres; and then, too, we separated off the process of felting and the kind of joining that employs piercing and sewing, most important of which is the shoemaker’s art. Yes, to be sure. And we separated off the art of making coverings of leather in single pieces and all the arts of making shelters, which we find in house-building and carpentering in general

greek_head: στεκτικαὶ γίγνονται συμπάσας ἀφείλομεν ὅσαι τε περὶ τὰς κλοπὰς καὶ τὰς βίᾳ πράξεις διακωλυτικὰ ἔργα παρέχονται
greek_tokens: stektikos gignomai sumpas afairew osos te peri o kloph kai o bia prassw diakwlutikos ergon parexw texnh fragma peri te genesis epiqhmatourgia eimi kai o o qurwma phgnumi gomfwtikos aponemw morion texnh o te oplopoiikh apotemnw megas kai pantoios o problhmatourgikos tmhma eimi dunamis
english_head: and in other methods of protection against water, and all the arts which furnish protection against theft and acts of violence, the arts, that is to say, of making lids and constructing doors, which are regarded as parts of the joiner’s art; and we cut off the armorer’s art, which is a section of the great and various function of making defences; and at the very beginning we cut off the whole art of magic which is concerned with antidotes and spells,

greek_head: καὶ δὴ καὶ τὴν μαγευτικὴν τὴν περὶ τὰ ἀλεξιφάρμακα κατ' ἀρχὰς εὐθὺς διωρισάμεθα σύμπασαν καὶ λελοίπαμεν ὡς
greek_tokens: kai dh kai o mageutikos o peri o alecifarmakon kata arxh euqus diaorizw sumpas kai leipw ws dokew an autos o zhtew amuntikos xeimwn ereous problhma ergastikos onoma de ufantikos legw eoika gar oun alla ou eimi pw teleios eimi pais outos legw o gar en arxh o o imation ergasia aptw enantion
english_head: and we have left, as it would seem, just the art we were seeking, which furnishes protection from the weather, manufactures a defence of wool, and is called the art of weaving. That seems to be the case. But, my boy, this is not yet completely stated; for the man who is engaged in the first part of the making of clothes

greek_head: ὑφῇ δρᾶν φαίνεται Πῶς
greek_tokens: ufaw draw fainw pws o men o ufaw sumplokh tis eimi pou nai o de ge o sunisthmi kai sunpilew dialutikos o poios dh o o o cainw texnh ergnumi h o cantikos tolmaw ufantikos kai o canths ws eimi ufanths kalew oudamos kai meis o ge au sthmwn ergastikos kai krokh ei tis ufantikos prosagoreuw paradocos te kai yeudos
english_head: appears to do something the opposite of weaving. How so? The process of weaving is, I take it, a kind of joining together. Yes. But the first part I refer to is a separation of what is combined and matted together. What do you mean? The work of the carder’s art. Or shall we have the face to say that carding is weaving and the carder is a weaver? No, certainly not. And surely if we say the art of making the warp or the woof is the art of weaving,

greek_head: ὄνομα λέγει Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: onoma legw pws gar ou tis de knafeutikos sumpas kai o akestikos poteros mhdeis epimeleia mhde tis qerapeia esqhs qazw h kai outos passw ws ufantikos legw oudamos alla meis o ge qerapeia amfisbhtew outos sumpas kai o genesis o o imation o o ufantikos dunamis megas men meros ekeinos didwmi megas de kai sfeis autos aponemw
english_head: we are employing an irrational and false designation. Of course. Well then, shall we say that the whole arts of fulling and mending are no part of the care and treatment of clothes, or shall we declare that these also are entirely included in the art of weaving? By no means. But surely all these will contest the claim of the art of weaving in the matter of the treatment and the production of clothes; they will grant that the part of weaving is the most important, but will claim that their own parts are of some importance, too.

greek_head: Πάνυ γε Πρὸς τοίνυν ταύταις ἔτι τὰς τῶν ἐργαλείων δημιουργοὺς
greek_tokens: panu ge pros toinun outos eti o o ergaleion dhmiourgos texnh dia os apotelew o o ufaw ergon dokew xrh o ge sunaitios eimi prospoiew pas ufasma orqos poteros oun egw o peri o ufantikos logos e proairew meros ikanos eimi diaorizw ean ara autos o epimeleia oposos peri o erea esqhs eis o
english_head: Yes, certainly. Then we must believe that besides these the arts which produce the tools by means of which the works of weaving are accomplished will claim to be collaborators in every work of weaving. Quite right. Will our definition of the art of weaving (I mean the part of it we selected) be satisfactory if we say that of all the activities connected with woollen clothing it is the noblest and the greatest?

greek_head: καλλίστην καὶ μεγίστην πασῶν τιθῶμεν ἢ λέγοιμεν μὲν ἄν τι ἀληθές οὐ μὴν σαφές γε οὐδὲ τέλεον πρὶν ἂν καὶ ταύτας
