english_head: Or would that, although it contains some truth, yet lack clearness and completeness until we separate from weaving all these other arts? You are right. Then shall our next move be to do this, that our discussion may proceed in due order? Certainly. First, then, let us observe that there are two arts involved in all production. What are they? The one is a contingent cause, the other is the actual cause. What do you mean? Those arts which do not produce the actual thing in question,

greek_head: Ὅσαι μὲν τὸ πρᾶγμα αὐτὸ μὴ δημιουργοῦσι ταῖς δὲ δημιουργούσαις ὄργανα παρασκευάζουσιν ὧν μὴ παραγενομένων
greek_tokens: osos men o pragma autos mh dhmiourgew o de dhmiourgew organon paraskeuazw os mh paragignomai ou an pote ergazomai o prostassw ekastos o texnh outos men sunaitios o de autos o pragma apergazomai aitios exis goun logos meta outos dh o men peri te atraktos kai kerkis kai oposos allos organon o peri o amfiesma genesis koinwnew passw sunaitios eipon o de autos qerapeuw kai dhmiourgew aitios orqos
english_head: but which supply to the arts which do produce it the tools without which no art could ever perform its prescribed work, may be called contingent causes, and those which produce the actual thing are causes. At any rate, that is reasonable. Next, then, shall we designate all the arts which produce spindles, shuttles, and the various other tools that partake in the production of clothing as contingent causes, and those which treat and manufacture the clothing itself as causes? Quite right.

greek_head: Τῶν αἰτιῶν δὴ πλυντικὴν μὲν καὶ ἀκεστικὴν καὶ πᾶσαν τὴν περὶ ταῦτα θεραπευτικήν πολλῆς οὔσης τῆς
greek_tokens: o aitia dh pluntikos men kai akestikos kai pas o peri outos qerapeutikos polus eimi o kosmhtikos entauqa autos morion eoika malista perilambanw onomazw pas o texnh o knafeutikos kalos kai meis cantikos ge kai nhstikos kai pas au o peri o poihsis autos o esqhs eis legw mera eis tis eimi texnh o upo pas legw o talasiourgikos pws gar ou
english_head: And among the causal arts we may properly include washing and mending and all the care of clothing in such ways; and, since the art of adornment is a wide one, we may classify them as a part of it under the name of fulling. Good. And, again, carding and spinning and all the processes concerned with the actual fabrication of the clothing under consideration, form collectively one art familiar to every one—the art of wool-working.

greek_head: Τῆς δὴ ταλασιουργικῆς δύο τμήματά ἐστον καὶ τούτοιν ἑκάτερον ἅμα δυοῖν πεφύκατον τέχναιν μέρη
greek_tokens: o dh talasiourgikos duo tmhma eimi kai outos ekateros ama duo fuw texnh mera pws o men cantikos kai o o kerkistikh hmisus kai osos o sugkeimai apo allhlwn afisthmi pas outos ws eis frazw o te talasiourgia autos eimi pou kai megas tis kata pas egw eimi texnh o sugkritikos te kai diakritikos nai o toinun diakritikos o te cantikos kai o nundi
english_head: Of course. And wool-working comprises two divisions, and each of these is a part of two arts at once. How is that? Carding, and one half of the use of the weaver’s rod, and the other crafts which separate things that are joined—all this collectively is a part of the art of wool-working; and in all things we found two great arts, that of composition and that of division. Yes. Now carding and all the other processes just mentioned are parts of the art of division;

greek_head: ῥηθέντα ἅπαντά ἐστιν ἡ γὰρ ἐν ἐρίοις τε καὶ στήμοσι διακριτική κερκίδι μὲν ἄλλον τρόπον γιγνομένη χερσὶ δὲ ἕτερον
greek_tokens: erw apas eimi o gar en erion te kai sthmwn diakritikos kerkis men allos tropos gignomai xeir de eteros exw osos artios onoma erw panu men oun auqis dh palin sugkritikos morion ama kai talasiourgia en autos gignomai lambanw osos de o diakritikos eimi autoqi metaiew sumpas dixa temnw o talasiourgia diakritikos te kai sugkritikos tmhma diairew o sugkritikos toinun au su kai talasiourgikos
english_head: for the art of division in wool and threads, exercised in one way with the rod and in another with the hands, has all the names just mentioned. Yes, certainly. Then let us again take up something which is at once a part of the arts of composition and of wool-working. Let us put aside all that belongs to division, making two parts of wool-working, by applying the principles of division and of composition. Let us make that distinction. The part which belongs at once to composition and to wool-working,

greek_head: ἅμα μόριον ὦ Σώκρατες διαιρετέον εἴπερ ἱκανῶς μέλλομεν τὴν προρρηθεῖσαν ὑφαντικὴν αἱρήσειν
greek_tokens: ama morion eimi swkraths diaireteon eiper ikanos mellw o proerew ufantikos airew oukoun xrh xrh men oun kai legw ge autos o men eimi streptikos o de sumplektikos ara oun manqanw dokew gar egw o peri o o sthmwn ergasia legw streptikos ou monos ge alla kai krokh h genesis astrofos tis autos euriskw oudamos
english_head: Socrates, you must allow us to divide again, if we are to get a satisfactory concept of the aforesaid art of weaving. Then we must divide it. Yes, we must; and let us call one part of it the art of twisting threads, and the other the art of intertwining them. I am not sure I understand. By the art of twisting I think you mean the making of the warp. Not that only, but also the making of the woof. We shall not find that the woof is made without twisting, shall we? No, of course not. Well, just define warp and woof; perhaps the definition

greek_head: Διόρισαι δὴ καὶ τούτοιν ἑκάτερον ἴσως γὰρ ὁ διορισμὸς ἔγκαιρος ἄν σοι γένοιτο
greek_tokens: diorizw dh kai outos ekateros isos gar o diorisis egkairos an su gignomai ph ode o peri cantikos ergnumi mhkunw te kai exw platos legw eimi katagma tis nai outos dh o men atraktos te straptw kai stereos nhma gignomai sthmwn men fhmi o nhma o de apeuqunw autos texnh eimi sthmononhtikos orqos osos de ge au o men sustrofh xaunos lambanw o de o sthmwn emplecis pros o o gnayis olkh emmetros o malakoths isxw outos ara krokh men o new o de epitassw autos eimi texnh o
english_head: would serve you well at this junction. How shall I do it? In this way: A piece of carded wool, which is lengthened out and is wide, is said to be a lap of wool, is it not? Yes. And if any such lap of wool is twisted with a spindle and made into a hard thread, we call the thread warp, and the art which governs this process is the art of spinning the warp. Right. And the threads, in turn, which are more loosely twisted and have in respect to the force used in the carding a softness adapted to the interweaving with the warp we will call the woof, and the art devoted to these

greek_head: κροκονητικὴν φῶμεν Ὀρθότατα
greek_tokens: krokonhtikh fhmi orqos kai meis o ge o ufantikos meros o protiqhmi pas pou dhlos hdh o gar sugkritikos o en talasiourgia morion otan euquplokia krokh kai sthmwn apergazomai plegma o men flegw sumpas esqhs erea o de epi outos texnh eimi prosagoreuw ufantikos orqos
english_head: we will call the art of preparing the woof. Quite right. So now the part of the art of weaving which we chose for our discussion is clear to pretty much every understanding; for when that part of the art of composition which is included in the art of weaving forms a web by the right intertwining of woof and warp, we call the entire web a woollen garment, and the art which directs this process we call weaving. Quite right. Very good. Then why in the world did we not say at once

greek_head: Εἶεν τί δή ποτε οὖν οὐκ εὐθὺς ἀπεκρινάμεθα πλεκτικὴν εἶναι κρόκης καὶ στήμονος ὑφαντικήν ἀλλὰ περιήλθομεν
greek_tokens: eien tis dh pote oun ou euqus apokrinw plektikos eimi krokh kai sthmwn ufantikos alla perierxomai en kuklos pampolus diorizw math oukoun egw eimi cenos math oudeis o erw dokew erw kai qaumastos ge oudeis alla taxa an eimi makarios dokew pros dh o noshma o toioutos an ara pollakis usteron efhmai qaumastos gar oudeis logos akouw tis
english_head: that weaving is the intertwining of woof and warp? Why did we beat about the bush and make a host of futile distinctions? For my part, I thought nothing that was said was futile, Stranger. And no wonder; but perhaps you might change your mind. Now to avoid any such malady, in case it should, as is not unlikely, attack you frequently hereafter, I will propose a principle of procedure which is applicable

greek_head: προσήκοντα περὶ πάντων τῶν τοιούτων ῥηθῆναι Λέγε μόνον
greek_tokens: proshkw peri pas o toioutos erw legos monos proteros toinun eidon pas o te uperbolh kai o elleiyis ina kata logos epainew kai yegw o makros o deon ekastote legw kai enantios peri o toiosde diatribh oukoun xrh peri dh outos autos o logos egw oimh gignomai orqos an gignomai tinw mhkos te peri kai braxuths kai pas uperoxew
english_head: to all cases of this sort. Do so. First, then, let us scrutinize the general nature of excess and deficiency, for the sake of obtaining a rational basis for any praise or blame we may bestow upon excessive length or brevity in discussions of this kind. Yes, that is a good thing to do. Then the proper subjects for our consideration would, I fancy, be these. What? Length and shortness and excess and deficiency in general;

greek_head: τε καὶ ἐλλείψεως ἡ γάρ που μετρητικὴ περὶ πάντ' ἐστὶ ταῦτα
greek_tokens: te kai elleiyis o gar pou metrhtikos peri panth eimi outos nai diairew toinun autos duo mera deomai gar dh pros o nun speudw legos an o diairesis oph ode o men kata o pros allhlwn megeqos kai mikroths koinwnia o de o kata o o genesis anagkh ousia pws legw ara ou kata fus dokew su o megas mhdeis eteros dew megas legw h o elasswn kai elasswn au
english_head: for all of them may be regarded as the subjects of the art of measurement. Yes. Let us, then, divide that art into two parts; that is essential for our present purpose. Please tell how to make the division. In this way: one part is concerned with relative greatness or smallness, the other with the something without which production would not be possible. What do you mean? Do you not think that, by the nature of the case, we must say that the greater is greater than the less and than nothing else,

greek_head: τοῦ μείζονος ἔλαττον ἄλλου δὲ μηδενός Ἔμοιγε
greek_tokens: o megas elasswn allos de mhdeis egw tis de o o o metrios fus uperballw kai uperballw upo autos en logos eite kai en ergon ara ou au legw ws ontws gignomai en os kai diaferw malista egw e te kakos kai e agaqos fainw dissos ara outos ousia kai krisis o megas kai o mikros qeteos alla ou ws fhmi arti pros allhlwn monos dew alla wsper nun eromai mallon o men pros allhlwn lekteos o de au pros o metrios e de eneka manqanw ara an boulomai tis meis
english_head: and that the less is less than the greater and than nothing else? Yes. But must we not also assert the real existence of excess beyond the standard of the mean, and of inferiority to the mean, whether in words or deeds, and is not the chief difference between good men and bad found in such excess or deficiency? That is clear. Then we must assume that there are these two kinds of great and small, and these two ways of distinguishing between them; we must not, as we did a little while ago, say that they are relative to one another only, but rather, as we have just said, that one kind is relative in that way, and the other is relative to the standard of the mean. Should we care to learn the reason for this? Of course.

greek_head: Εἰ πρὸς μηδὲν ἕτερον τὴν τοῦ μείζονος ἐάσει τις φύσιν ἢ πρὸς τοὔλαττον οὐκ ἔσται ποτὲ πρὸς τὸ μέτριον
greek_tokens: ei pros mhdeis eteros o o megas eaw tis fus h pros elasswn ou eimi pote pros o metrios eimi gar outws oukoun o texnh te autos kai targa autos sumpas diollumi outos o logos kai dh kai o zhtew nun politikos kai o erw ufantikos afanizw apas gar o toioutos pou o o metrios pleos kai elasswn ou ws ou eimi alla ws eimi xalepos peri o prassw
english_head: If we assert that the greater has no relation to anything except the less, it will never have any relation to the standard of the mean, will it? No. Will not this doctrine destroy the arts and their works one and all, and do away also with statesmanship, which we are now trying to define, and with weaving, which we did define? For all these are doubtless careful about excess and deficiency in relation to the standard of the mean; they regard them not as non-existent, but as real difficulties in actual practice,

greek_head: παραφυλάττουσι καὶ τούτῳ δὴ τῷ τρόπῳ τὸ μέτρον σῴζουσαι πάντα ἀγαθὰ καὶ καλὰ ἀπεργάζονται
greek_tokens: parafulassw kai outos dh o tropos o metron swzw pas agaqos kai kalos apergazomai tis meis oukoun an o politikos afanizw aporos egw o meta outos eimi zhthsis o basilikos episthmh kai mala poteros oun kaqa en o sofisths prosanagkazw eimi o mh eimi epei kata outos diafeugw egw o logos outws kai nun o pleos au kai elasswn metrhths prosanagkasteon gignomai mh pros allhlwn monos alla kai
english_head: and it is in this way, when they preserve the standard of the mean, that all their works are good and beautiful. Certainly. And if we do away with the art of statesmanship, our subsequent search for the kingly art will be hopeless, will it not? Certainly. Then just as in the case of the sophist we forced the conclusion that not-being exists, since that was the point at which we had lost our hold of the argument, so now we must force this second conclusion, that the greater and the less are to be measured in relation,

greek_head: πρὸς τὴν τοῦ μετρίου γένεσιν οὐ γὰρ δὴ δυνατόν γε οὔτε πολιτικὸν οὔτ' ἄλλον τινὰ τῶν περὶ τὰς πράξεις ἐπιστήμονα
greek_tokens: pros o o metrios genesis ou gar dh dunatos ge oute politikos oute allos tis o peri o prassw episthmwn anamfisbhthtos gignomai outos mh sunomologew oukoun kai nun ostis malista xrh tautos poiew pleos eimi swkraths eti outos o ergnumi h ekeinos kaitoi ekeinos ge mimnhskw o mhkos osos eimi alla upotiqhmi men o toiosde peri autos kai mala dikaios o poios
english_head: not only to one another, but also to the establishment of the standard of the mean, must we not? For if this is not admitted, neither the statesman nor any other man who has knowledge of practical affairs can be said without any doubt to exist. Then we must by all means do now the same that we did then. This, Socrates, is a still greater task than that was; and yet we remember how long that took us; but it is perfectly fair to make about them some such assumption as this. As what?

greek_head: Ὥς ποτε δεήσει τοῦ νῦν λεχθέντος πρὸς τὴν περὶ αὐτὸ τἀκριβὲς ἀπόδειξιν ὅτι δὲ πρὸς τὰ νῦν καλῶς καὶ
greek_tokens: os pote dehsis o nun legw pros o peri autos akribhs apodeicis ostis de pros o nun kalos kai ikanos deiknumi dokew egw bohqew megaloprephs egw outos o logos ws ara hghteon omoios o texnh passw eimi megas te ama kai elasswn metrew mh pros allhlwn monos alla kai pros o o metrios genesis outos te gar eimi ekeinos eimi ekeinos eimi eimi kai outos mh de eimi poteros outos oudeteros autos eimi pote
english_head: That sometime we shall need this principle of the mean for the demonstration of absolute precise truth. But our belief that the demonstration is for our present purpose good and sufficient is, in my opinion, magnificently supported by this argument—that we must believe that all the arts alike exist and that the greater and the less are measured in relation not only to one another but also to the establishment of the standard of the mean. For if this exists, they exist also, and if they exist, it exists also, but neither can ever exist if the other does not.

greek_head: Τοῦτο μὲν ὀρθῶς ἀλλὰ τί δὴ τὸ μετὰ τοῦτο Δῆλον ὅτι διαιροῖμεν ἂν τὴν μετρητικήν καθάπερ
greek_tokens: outos men orqos alla tis dh o meta outos dhlos ostis diairew an o metrhtikos kaqa erw outos dixa temnw eis men tiqhmi autos morion sumpas texnh oposos o ariqmos kai mhkos kai baqos kai plath kai taxuths pros enantion metrew o de eteros oposos pros o metrios kai o prepw kai o kairos kai o deon kai panth oposos eis o mesos apoikizw o esxatos kai megas ge ekateros tmhma eipon kai polus diaferw allhlwn o gar eniote eimi swkraths oiomai dh tis sofos
english_head: That is quite right. But what comes next? We should evidently divide the science of measurement into two parts in accordance with what has been said. One part comprises all the arts which measure number, length, depth, breadth, and thickness in relation to their opposites; the other comprises those which measure them in relation to the moderate, the fitting, the opportune, the needful, and all the other standards that are situated in the mean between the extremes. Both of your divisions are extensive, and there is a great difference between them. Yes, for what many clever persons occasionally say, Socrates, fancying that it is a wise remark,

greek_head: φράζειν πολλοὶ τῶν κομψῶν λέγουσιν ὡς ἄρα μετρητικὴ περὶ πάντ' ἐστὶ τὰ γιγνόμενα τοῦτ' αὐτὸ τὸ νῦν λεχθὲν ὂν
greek_tokens: frazw polus o komyos legw ws ara metrhtikos peri panth eimi o gignomai outos autos o nun legw eimi tugxanw metrhsis men gar dh tis tropos panth oposos entexnos metalambanw dia de o mh kata eidos suneqizw skopaw diairew outos te tosoutos diaferw sumballw euqus eis tautos omoios nomizw kai enantion au outos draw eteros ou kata mera diairew deon otan
english_head: namely, that the science of measurement has to do with everything, is precisely the same as what we have just said. For in a certain way all things which are in the province of art do partake of measurement; but because people are not in the habit of considering things by dividing them into classes, they hastily put these widely different relations into the same category, thinking they are alike; and again they do the opposite of this when they fail to divide other things into parts. What they ought to do is this: when a person at first sees only the unity or common quality of many things,

greek_head: μὲν τὴν τῶν πολλῶν τις πρότερον αἴσθηται κοινωνίαν μὴ προαφίστασθαι πρὶν ἂν ἐν αὐτῇ τὰς διαφορὰς ἴδῃ πάσας
greek_tokens: men o o polus tis proteros aisqanomai koinwnia mh proafistamai prin an en autos o diafora eidon passw oposos en eidos keimai o de au pantodapos anomoioths otan en plhqos oraw mh dunatos eimi duswpew pauw prin an sumpas o oikeios entos eis omoioths erdw genos tis ousia periballw outos men oun ikanos peri te outos kai peri o elleiyis kai uperbolh erw fulassw de monos ostis duo genos
english_head: he must not give up until he sees all the differences in them, so far as they exist in classes; and conversely, when all sorts of dissimilarities are seen in a large number of objects he must find it impossible to be discouraged or to stop until has gathered into one circle of similarity all the things which are related to each other and has included them in some sort of class on the basis of their essential nature. No more need be said, then, about this or about deficiency and excess; let us only bear carefully in mind that two kinds of measurement which apply to them have been found,

greek_head: περὶ αὐτὰ ἐξηύρηται τῆς μετρητικῆς καὶ ἅ φαμεν αὔτ' εἶναι μεμνώμεθα
greek_tokens: peri autos eceuriskw o metrhtikos kai o fhmi autos eimi mimnhskw mimnhskw meta outos dh o logos eteros prosdexomai peri autos te o zhtew kai peri pas o en o toiosde logos diatribh o poios ei tis aneromai egw o peri gramma sunousia o manqanw opotan tis otioun onoma erwtaw tinw eimi gramma poteros autos tote fhmi gignomai o
english_head: and let us remember what those kinds are. We will remember. Now that we have finished this discussion, let us take up another which concerns the actual objects of our inquiry and the conduct of such discussions in general. What is it? Suppose we were asked the following question about a group of pupils learning their letters: When a pupil is asked of what letters some word or other composed,

greek_head: ζήτησιν ἑνὸς ἕνεκα μᾶλλον τοῦ προβληθέντος ἢ τοῦ περὶ πάντα τὰ προβαλλόμενα γραμματικωτέρῳ γίγνεσθαι
greek_tokens: zhthsis eis eneka mallon o proballw h o peri pas o proballw grammatikos gignomai dhlos ostis o peri apas tis de au nun egw o peri o politikos zhthsis eneka autos outos proballw mallon h o peri pas dialektikos gignomai kai outos dhlos ostis o peri pas eimi pou o o ufantikos ge logos autos outos eneka qhreuw oudeis an eqelw new exw alla oimh o pleistos lanqanw ostis o men o eimi radios
english_head: is the question asked for the sake of the one particular word before him or rather to make him more learned about all words in the lesson? Clearly to make him more learned about them all. And how about our own investigation of the statesman? Has it been undertaken for the sake of his particular subject or rather to make us better thinkers about all subjects? Clearly this also is done with a view to them all. Of course no man of sense would wish to pursue the discussion of weaving for its own sake; but most people, it seems to me, fail to notice that some things have sensible resemblances which are easily perceived;

greek_head: καταμαθεῖν αἰσθηταί τινες ὁμοιότητες πεφύκασιν ἃς οὐδὲν χαλεπὸν δηλοῦν ὅταν αὐτῶν τις βουληθῇ τῷ λόγον αἰτοῦντι
greek_tokens: katamanqanw aisqhths tis omoioths fuw os oudeis xalepos dhlow otan autos tis boulomai o logos aitew peri tis mh meta pragma alla xwris logos radios endeiknumi o de au megas eimi kai timios
english_head: and it is not at all difficult to show them when anyone wishes, in response to a request for an explanation of some one of them, to exhibit them easily without trouble and really without explanation. But, on the other hand, the greatest and noblest conceptions

greek_head: οὐκ ἔστιν εἴδωλον οὐδὲν πρὸς τοὺς ἀνθρώπους εἰργασμένον ἐναργῶς οὗ δειχθέντος τὴν τοῦ πυνθανομένου ψυχὴν ὁ
greek_tokens: ou eimi eidwlon oudeis pros o anqrwpos ergazomai enarghs e deiknumi o o punqanomai yuxh o boulomai apoplhrow pros o aisqhsis tis prosarmozw ikanos plhrwsis dio deomai meleth logos ekastos dunatos eimi didwmi kai dexomai o gar aswmatos kallista eimi kai megas logos monos allos de oudeis safhs deiknumi outos de eneka panth eimi o nun legw raios
english_head: have no image wrought plainly for human vision, which he who wishes to satisfy the mind of the inquirer can apply to some one of his senses and by mere exhibition satisfy the mind. We must therefore endeavor by practice to acquire the power of giving and understanding a rational definition of each one of them; for immaterial things, which are the noblest and greatest, can be exhibited by reason only, and it is for their sake that all we are saying is said. But it is always easier to practise

greek_head: δ' ἐν τοῖς ἐλάττοσιν ἡ μελέτη παντὸς πέρι μᾶλλον ἢ περὶ τὰ μείζω
greek_tokens: de en o elasswn o meleth pas peri mallon h peri o megas kallista eipon os toinun xaris apas egw outos erw peri outos mimnhskw tinw outos te ou hkistos autos eneka o dusxereia os peri o makrologia o peri o ufantikos apodexomai dusxerhs kai o peri o o pas aneilicis kai o o sofisths peri o o mh eimi ousia ennoew ws exw mhkos pleos kai epi outos dh pas epiplhssw
english_head: in small matters than in greater ones. Excellent. Let us, then, remember the reason for all that we have said about these matters. What is the reason? The reason is chiefly just that irritating impatience which we exhibited in relation to the long talk about weaving and the revolution of the universe and the sophist’s long talk about the existence of not-being. We felt that they were too long, and we reproached ourselves for all of them,

greek_head: ἡμῖν αὐτοῖς δείσαντες μὴ περίεργα ἅμα καὶ μακρὰ λέγοιμεν ἵν' οὖν εἰς αὖθις μηδὲν πάσχωμεν τοιοῦτον τούτων ἕνεκα
greek_tokens: egw autos deidw mh periergos ama kai makros legw ina oun eis auqis mhdeis pasxw toioutos outos eneka pas o prosqen egw erw fhmi outos eimi legos echs monos legos toinun ostis xrh dh mimnhskw egw kai sos o nun erw o te yogos ekastote kai epainos poiew braxuths ama kai mhkos os an aei peri legw mh pros allhlwn o mhkos krinw alla kata o
english_head: fearing that our talk was not only long, but irrelevant. Consider, therefore, that the reason for what has just been said is my wish to avoid any such impatience in the future. Very well. Please go on with what you have to say. What I have to say, then, is that you and I, remembering what has just been said, must praise or blame the brevity or length of our several discussions, not by comparing their various lengths with one another, but with reference to that part of the science of measurement

greek_head: τῆς μετρητικῆς μέρος ὃ τότε ἔφαμεν δεῖν μεμνῆσθαι πρὸς τὸ πρέπον
greek_tokens: o metrhtikos meros o tote fhmi dew mimnhskw pros o prepw orqos ou toinun oude pros outos pas oute gar pros o hdona mhkos armozw oudeis prosdeomai plhn ei parergon tis o te au pros o o proballw zhthsis ws an radios kai taxistos euriskw deuteros alla ou proteros o logos agaph paraggellw polus de malista kai proteros o meqodos autos timaw o kata eidos dunatos eimi diairew
english_head: which we said before must be borne in mind; I mean the standard of fitness. Quite right. But we must not always judge of length by fitness, either. For we shall not in the least want a length that is fitted to give pleasure, except, perhaps, as a secondary consideration; and again reason counsels us to accept fitness for the easiest and quickest completion of the inquiry in which we are engaged, not as the first, but as the second thing to be desired. By far our first and most important object should be to exalt the method itself of ability to divide by classes,

greek_head: καὶ δὴ καὶ λόγον ἄντε παμμήκης λεχθεὶς τὸν ἀκούσαντα εὑρετικώτερον ἀπεργάζηται τοῦτον σπουδάζειν καὶ τῷ μήκει
greek_tokens: kai dh kai logos antomai pammhkhs legw o akouw euretikos apergazomai outos spoudazw kai o mhkos mhdeis aganaktew anta au braxus wsautws eti de au pros outos o peri o toiosde sunousia yegw logos mhkos kai o en kuklos periodos ou apodexomai ostis xrh o toioutos mh panu taxus mhde euqus outws meqihmi yegw
english_head: and therefore, if a discourse, even though it be very long, makes the hearer better able to discover the truth, we should accept it eagerly and should not be offended by its length, or if it is short, we should judge it in the same way. And, moreover, anyone who finds fault with the length of discourses in our discussions, or objects to roundabout methods, must not merely find fault with the speeches for their length and then pass them quickly and hastily by,

greek_head: μόνον ὡς μακρὰ τὰ λεχθέντα ἀλλὰ καὶ προσαποφαίνειν οἴεσθαι δεῖν ὡς βραχύτερα ἂν γενόμενα τοὺς συνόντας
greek_tokens: monos ws makros o legw alla kai prosapofainw oiomai dew ws braxus an gignomai o suneimi apergazomai dialektikos kai o o eimi logos dhlwsis euretikos o de allos kai pros allos atta yogos kai epainos mhdeis frontizw mhde o parapan akouw dokew o toioutos logos kai outos men alis ei kai sos outos sundokew pros de dh o politikos
english_head: but he must also show that there is ground for the belief that if they had been briefer they would have made their hearers better dialecticians and quicker to discover through reason the truth of realities. About other people and the praise or blame they direct towards other qualities in discourse, we need not be concerned; we need not even appear to hear them. But enough of this, if you feel about it as I do; so let us go back to the statesman

greek_head: ἴωμεν πάλιν τῆς προρρηθείσης ὑφαντικῆς αὐτῷ φέροντες τὸ παράδειγμα
greek_tokens: eimi palin o proerew ufantikos autos ferw o paradeigma kalos eipon kai poiew o legw oukoun apo ge o polus o basileus osos sunnomos mallon de apo pas o peri o agelh diaxwrizw loipos de fhmi o kata polis autos o te sunaitios kai o aitios os proteros apo allhlwn diaireteon orqos oistos oun ostis xalepos autos temnw dixa o de
english_head: and apply to him the example of weaving that we spoke of a while ago. Very well; let us do so. The art of the king, then, has been separated from most of the kindred arts, or rather from all the arts that have to do with herds. There remain, however, the arts that have to do with the state itself. These are both causes and contingent causes, and our first duty is to separate them from one another. Quite right. It is not easy to divide them into halves, you know.

greek_head: αἴτιον ὡς οἶμαι προϊοῦσιν οὐχ ἧττον ἔσται καταφανές Οὐκοῦν χρὴ δρᾶν οὕτως
greek_tokens: aitios ws oimh proeimi ou hsswn eimi katafanhs oukoun xrh draw outws kata melh toinun autos oios iereion diairw epei dixa adunatew deomai gar eis o eggus ostis malista temnw ariqmos aei pws oun poiew o nun wsper emprosqen oposos parexw organon peri o ufantikos passw dhpou tote tiqhmi ws sunaitios nai kai nun dh tautos men outos eti de mallon h toqi
english_head: But I think the reason will nevertheless be clear as we go on. Then we had better divide in another way. Let us divide them, then, like an animal that is sacrificed, by joints, since we cannot bisect them; for we must always divide into a number of parts as near two as possible. How shall we do it in the present instance? Just as in the previous case, you know, we classed all the arts which furnished tools for weaving as contingent causes. Yes. So now we must do the same thing, but it is even more imperative.

greek_head: ἡμῖν ποιητέον ὅσαι γὰρ σμικρὸν ἢ μέγα τι δημιουργοῦσι κατὰ πόλιν ὄργανον θετέον ἁπάσας ταύτας ὡς οὔσας συναιτίους
greek_tokens: egw poihteos osos gar mikros h megas tis dhmiourgew kata polis organon qeteos apas outos ws eimi sunaitios aneu gar outos ou an pote gignomai polis oude politikos outos de au basilikos ergnumi texnh oudeis pou tiqhmi ou gar kai men dh xalepos epixeirew draw apoxwrizw outos apo o allos o genos ostis gar oun o eimi eimi ws eis ge tis organon eipon dokew erw
english_head: For all the arts which furnish any implement, great or small, for the state, must be classed as contingent causes; for without them neither state nor statesmanship could ever exist, and yet I do not suppose we shall reckon any of them as the work of the kingly art. No. We shall certainly be undertaking a hard task in separating this class from the rest; for it might be said that everything that exists is the instrument of something or other, and the statement seems plausible. But there are possessions of another kind in the state,

greek_head: τι πιθανόν ὅμως δὲ ἕτερον αὖ τῶν ἐν πόλει κτημάτων εἴπωμεν τόδε
greek_tokens: tis piqanos omws de eteros au o en polis kthma eipon ode o poios ws ou eimi outos o dunamis exw ou gar epi genesis aitios phgnumi kaqa organon alla eneka o dhmiourgew swthria o poios outos o dh chros kai ugros kai empuros kai apuros pantodapos eidos ergazomai aggeion o dh eis klhsis prosfqeggomai kai mala ge suxnos eidos kai o zhtew
english_head: about which I wish to say something. What do you wish to say? That they do not possess this instrumental function. For they are not, like tools or instruments, made for the purpose of being causes of production, but exist for the preservation of that which has been produced. What is this class of possessions? That very various class which is made with dry and wet materials and such as are wrought by fire and without fire; it is called collectively the class of receptacles; it is a very large class and has, so far as I can see,

greek_head: γε ὡς οἶμαι προσῆκον οὐδὲν ἀτεχνῶς ἐπιστήμῃ Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: ge ws oimh proshkw oudeis atexnos episthmh pws gar ou outos dh tritos eteros eidos kthma pampolus katoptaw pezos kai enudros kai poluplanhs kai aplanhs kai timios kai atimos eis de onoma exw dioti pas eneka tis efedra eimi qakos aei tis gignomai o poios oxhma autos pou legw ou panu politikos ergnumi alla mallon polus tektonikos kai keramikos kai xalkotupikh manqanw
english_head: nothing at all to do with the art we are studying. No, of course not. And there is a third very large class of possessions to be noticed, differing from these; it is found on land and on water, it wanders about and is stationary, it is honorable and without honor, but it has one name, because the whole class is always a seat for some one and exists to be sat upon. What is it? We call it a vehicle, and it certainly is not at all the work of statesmanship, but much rather that of the arts of carpentry, pottery and bronze-working. I understand.

greek_head: Τί δὲ τέταρτον ἆρ' ἕτερον εἶναι τούτων λεκτέον ἐν ᾧ τὰ πλεῖστά ἐστι τῶν πάλαι ῥηθέντων ἐσθής τε σύμπασα
greek_tokens: tis de tetartos ara eteros eimi outos lekteos en os o pleistos eimi o pala erw esqhs te sumpas kai o oplon o polus kai teixos pas su osos ghinos periblhma kai liqinos kai murios eteros proboleus de eneka sumpas autos ergazomai dikaios an oloc prosagoreuw problhma kai polus mallon texnh oikodomikos ergnumi kai ufantikos o pleistos nomizw an orqos h politikos panu men oun
english_head: And is there a fourth class? Shall we say that there is one, differing from those three, one to which most of the things we have mentioned belong—all clothing, most arms, all circuit walls of earth or of stone, and countless other things? And since they are all made for defence, they may most rightly be called by the collective name of defence, and this may much more properly be considered for the most part the work of the art of building or of weaving than of statesmanship. Certainly.

greek_head: Πέμπτον δὲ ἆρ' ἂν ἐθέλοιμεν τὸ περὶ τὸν κόσμον καὶ γραφικὴν θεῖναι καὶ ὅσα ταύτῃ προσχρώμενα καὶ μουσικῇ
greek_tokens: pemptos de ara an eqelw o peri o kosmos kai grafikos qeinw kai osos outos prosxraomai kai mousikh mimhma tellw pros o hdona monos egw apergazomai dikaios de an onoma perilambanw eis poios paignion pou tis legw tis meis outos toinun outos eis onoma apas prepw prosagoreuw ou gar spoudazw oudeis autos xaris alla paidih eneka pas draw
english_head: And should we care to make a fifth class, of ornamentation and painting and all the imitations created by the use of painting and music solely for our pleasure and properly included under one name? What is its name? It is called by some such name as plaything. To be sure. So this one name will properly be applied to all the members of this class; for none of them is practised for any serious purpose, but all of them merely for play.

greek_head: Καὶ τοῦτο σχεδόν τι μανθάνω Τὸ δὲ πᾶσιν τούτοις σώματα παρέχον ἐξ ὧν καὶ
greek_tokens: kai outos sxedon tis manqanw o de pas outos swma parexw ek os kai en os dhmiourgew oposos o texnh nun erw pantodapos eidos polus eteros texnh ekgonos eimi ara ou ektos tiqhmi o poios dh legw xrusos te kai arguros kai panth oposos metalleuw kai osos druotomikh kai koura sumpas temnw paraxew tektonikos kai plektikos kai eti floistikos futon
english_head: I understand that pretty well, too. And shall we not make a sixth class of that which furnishes to all these the materials of which and in which all the arts we have mentioned fashion their works, a very various class, the offspring of many other arts? What do you mean? Gold and silver and all the products of the mines and all the materials which tree-felling and wood-cutting in general cut and provide for carpentry and basket-weaving; and then, too, the art of stripping the bark from plants and the leather-worker’s art which takes off the skins of animals,

greek_head: τε καὶ ἐμψύχων δέρματα σωμάτων περιαιροῦσα σκυτοτομική καὶ ὅσαι περὶ τὰ τοιαῦτά εἰσιν τέχναι καὶ φελλῶν καὶ βύβλων
greek_tokens: te kai emyuxos derma swma periairew skutotomikos kai osos peri o toioutos eimi texnh kai fellos kai bublos kai desmh ergastikos parexw dhmiourgew sunqetos ek mh suntiqhmi eidos genos eis de autos prosagoreuw pas o prwtogenhs anqrwpos kthma kai asunqetos kai basilikos episthmh oudamos ergnumi eimi kalos o dh o trefw ktaomai kai osos eis o swma sugkatamignumi eautou meros mera swma eis o
english_head: and all the other arts which have to do with such matters, and those that make corks and paper and cords and enable us to manufacture composite classes of things from kinds that are not composite. We call all this, as one class, the primary and simple possession of man, and it is in no way the work of the kingly science. Good. And property in food and all the things which, mingling parts of themselves with parts of the body, have any function of keeping it in health,

greek_head: θεραπεῦσαί τινα δύναμιν εἴληχε λεκτέον ἕβδομον ὀνομάσαντας αὐτὸ σύμπαν ἡμῶν εἶναι τροφόν εἰ μή τι κάλλιον
greek_tokens: qerapeuw tis dunamis lagxanw lekteos ebdomos onomazw autos sumpas egw eimi trofos ei mh tis kallion exw allos tiqhmi gewrgikos de kai qhreutikos kai gumnastikos kai iatrikos kai mageirikos pas upotiqhmi orqos apodidwmi h o politikos pws gar ou sxedon toinun osos exw kthsis plhn o hmeros zwion en outos epta oimh genesis erw skopaw de eimi gar dikaios men an tiqhmi kata arxh o
english_head: we may say is the seventh class, and we will call it collectively our nourishment, unless we have some better name to give it. All this we can assign to the arts of husbandry, hunting, gymnastics, medicine, and cooking more properly than to that of statesmanship. Of course. Now I think I have in these seven classes mentioned nearly all kinds of property except tame animals. See: there was the primary possession, which ought in justice to have been placed first, and after this

greek_head: πρωτογενὲς εἶδος μετὰ δὲ τοῦτο ὄργανον ἀγγεῖον ὄχημα πρόβλημα παίγνιον θρέμμα ἃ παραλείπομεν δέ εἴ
greek_tokens: prwtogenhs eidos meta de outos organon aggeion oxhma problhma paignion qremma o paraleipw de ei tis mh megas lanqanw eis tis outos dunatos armozw oios o o nomisma idea kai sfragis kai pas xarakthr genos te gar en eautou outos oudeis exis megas sunnomos alla o men eis kosmos o de eis organon bia men omws de pantws elkw sumfwnhsis o de peri zwion ktaomai o hmeros plhn doulos o
english_head: the instrument, receptacle, vehicle, defence, plaything, nourishment. Whatever we have omitted, unless some important thing has been overlooked, can find its place in one of those classes; for instance, the group of coins, seals, and stamps, for there is not among these any kinship such as to form a large class, but some of them can be made to fit into the class of ornaments, others into that of instruments, though the classification is somewhat forced. All property in tame animals,

greek_head: πρότερον ἀγελαιοτροφικὴ διαμερισθεῖσα πάντ' εἰληφυῖα ἀναφανεῖται
greek_tokens: proteros agelaiotrofikos diamerizw panth lambanw anafainw panu men oun o de dh doulos kai pas uphreths loipos en os pou kai manteuomai o peri autos o plegma amfisbhtew o basileus katafainw gignomai kaqa o ufanths tote o peri o nhqw te kai cainw kai osos allos eipon e de allos pas ws sunaitios legw ama o ergon o nundi erw anallomai kai apoxwrizw
english_head: except slaves, is included in the art of herding, which has already been divided into parts. Yes quite true. There remains the class of slaves and servants in general, and here I prophesy that we shall find those who set up claims against the king for the very fabric of his art, just as the spinners and carders and the rest of whom we spoke advanced claims against the weavers a while ago. All the others, whom we called contingent causes, have been removed along with the works we just mentioned and have been separated

greek_head: ἀπὸ βασιλικῆς τε καὶ πολιτικῆς πράξεως Ἐοίκασι γοῦν
greek_tokens: apo basilikos te kai politikos pracis eoika goun eimi dh skeptomai o loipos proserxomai egguqen ina autos oida bebaios oukoun xrh o men dh megas uphreths ws enqende eidon enantion exw euriskw os upopteuw epithdeuma kai paqos tis o wnhtos te kai o tropos outos kthtos os
english_head: from the activity of the king and the statesman. That seems to be the case, at least. Come then, let us step up and look from close at hand at those who are left, that so we may know them more surely. Yes, that is what we should do. We shall find, then, that the greatest servants, when seen from near at hand, are in conduct and condition the opposite of that which we suspected. Who are they? The bought servants, acquired by purchase, whom we can without question call slaves.

greek_head: ἀναμφισβητήτως δούλους ἔχομεν εἰπεῖν ἥκιστα βασιλικῆς μεταποιουμένους τέχνης
greek_tokens: anamfisbhthtos doulos exw eipon hkistos basilikos metapoiew texnh pws de ou tis de o eleuqeros osos o nundi erw eis uphretikos ekwn eautou tassw o te gewrgia kai o o allos texnh ergon diakomizw epi allhlwn kai anisow e men kata agora e de polis ek polis allassw kata qalassa kai pezos nomisma te pros o allos kai autos pros eautou diameibw os arguramoibos te
english_head: They make no claim to any share in the kingly art. Certainly not. How about those free men who put themselves voluntarily in the position of servants of those whom we mentioned before? I mean the men who carry about and distribute among one another the productions of husbandry and the other arts, whether in the domestic marketplaces or by travelling from city to city by land or sea, exchanging money for wares or money for money, the men whom we call brokers,

greek_head: καὶ ἐμπόρους καὶ ναυκλήρους καὶ καπήλους ἐπωνομάκαμεν μῶν τῆς πολιτικῆς ἀμφισβητήσουσί τι
greek_tokens: kai emporos kai nauklhros kai kaphlos eponomazw mhs o politikos amfisbhtew tis taxa an isos o ge o emporeutikos alla ou meis os ge oraw misqwtos kai qhs pas etoimos uphretew mh pote basilikos metapoiew euriskw pws gar tis de ara o o toiosde diakonew egw ekastote o poios eipon kai tis
english_head: merchants, shipmasters, and peddlers; do they lay any claim to statesmanship? Possibly to commercial statesmanship But certainly we shall never find laborers, whom we see only too glad to serve anybody for hire, claiming a share in the kingly art. Certainly not. But there are people who perform services of another kind. How about them? What services and what men do you mean?

greek_head: Ὧν τὸ κηρυκικὸν ἔθνος ὅσοι τε περὶ γράμματα σοφοὶ γίγνονται πολλάκις ὑπηρετήσαντες καὶ πόλλ' ἄττα
greek_tokens: os o khrukikos eqnos osos te peri gramma sofos gignomai pollakis uphretew kai polus atta eteros peri o arxh diaponew tis eteros pandeinos tis outos au legw osper eipon nun uphreths alla ou autos en o polis arxw alla ou meis oimh ge enupnion eidon eipon outos ph fainw o diaferontws amfisbhtew o politikos kaitoi sfodra ge atopos an eimi dokew o zhtew
english_head: The class of heralds and those who become by long practice skilled as clerks and other clever men who perform various services in connection with public offices. What shall we call them? What you called the others, servants; they are not themselves rulers in the states. But surely it was no dream that made me say we should find somewhere in this region those who more than others lay claim to the art of statesmanship; and yet it would be utterly absurd

greek_head: τούτους ἐν ὑπηρετικῇ μοίρᾳ τινί Κομιδῇ μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: outos en uphretikos moira tis komidh men oun eti dh prosmeignumi egguteros epi o mhpw basanizw eimi de e te peri mantikos exw tis episthmh diakonos morion ermhneuths gar pou nomizw para qea anqrwpos nai kai meis kai o o iera au genos ws o nomimos fhmi para men egw dwrea qeaw dia qusia episthmwn
english_head: to look for them in any servile position. Certainly. But let us draw a little closer still to those whom we have not yet examined. There are men who have to do with divination and possess a portion of a certain menial science; for they are supposed to be interpreters of the gods to men. Yes. And then, too, the priests, according to law and custom, know how to give the gods, by means of sacrifices, the gifts that please them from us

greek_head: ἐστι κατὰ νοῦν ἐκείνοις δωρεῖσθαι παρὰ δὲ ἐκείνων ἡμῖν εὐχαῖς κτῆσιν ἀγαθῶν αἰτήσασθαι ταῦτα δὲ διακόνου τέχνης
greek_tokens: eimi kata new ekeinos dwreomai para de ekeinos egw euxh ktaomai agaqos aitew outos de diakonos texnh eimi pou morion amfoteros fainw goun hdh toinun egw dokew oios ge tis ixnos epi o poreuw prosaptw o gar dh o iera sxhma kai o o mantis eu mala fronhma plhrow kai doca semnos lambanw dia o megeqos o egxeirhma wste peri men aiguptos oude ecesti basileus xwris ieratikos
english_head: and by prayers to ask for us the gain of good things from them; now these are both part of a servant’s art. At least they seem to be so. At last, then, I think we are, as it were, on the track of our quarry. For the bearing of the priests and prophets is indeed full of pride, and they win high esteem because of the magnitude of their undertakings. In Egypt, for example, no king can rule without being a priest,

greek_head: ἄρχειν ἀλλ' ἐὰν ἄρα καὶ τύχῃ πρότερον ἐξ ἄλλου γένους βιασάμενος ὕστερον ἀναγκαῖον εἰς τοῦτο εἰστελεῖσθαι αὐτὸν
greek_tokens: arxw alla ean ara kai tuxh proteros ek allos genos biaw usteron anagkaion eis outos eistelew autos o genos eti de kai o ellhn pollaxou o megas arxh o megas o peri o toioutos quma euriskw tis an prostassw quw kai dh kai para su ou hkistos dhlos o legos o gar lagxanw basileus fhmi ode o semnos kai malista patrios o arxaios qusia apodidwmi kai panu ge
english_head: and if he happens to have forced his way to the throne from some other class, he must enroll himself in the class of priests afterwards; and among the Greeks, too, you would find that in many states the performance of the greatest public sacrifices is a duty imposed upon the highest officials. Yes, among you Athenians this is very plain, for they say the holiest and most national of the ancient sacrifices are performed by the man whom the lot has chosen to be the King. Yes, certainly.

greek_head: Τούτους τε τοίνυν τοὺς κληρωτοὺς βασιλέας ἅμα καὶ ἱερέας καὶ ὑπηρέτας αὐτῶν καί τινα ἕτερον πάμπολυν
greek_tokens: outos te toinun o klhrwtos basileus ama kai iereia kai uphreths autos kai tis eteros pampolus oxlos skepteos os arti katadhlos nun egw gignomai apoxwrizw o emprosqen tis de autos kai legw kai mala tis atopos tis dh pamfulos tis genos autos os ge arti skopaw fainw polus men gar lewn o androw eoika kai kentauros
english_head: We must, then, examine these elected kings and priests and their assistants, and also another very large crowd of people which has just come in sight now that the others are out of the way. Who are these people? A very queer lot. How so? They are of very mixed race at least they seem so now, when I can just see them. For many of them are like lions and centaurs and other fierce creatures,

greek_head: καὶ τοιούτοισιν ἑτέροις πάμπολλοι δὲ Σατύροις καὶ τοῖς ἀσθενέσι καὶ πολυτρόποις θηρίοις ταχὺ δὲ μεταλλάττουσι
greek_tokens: kai toioutos eteros pampolus de saturos kai o asqenhs kai polutropos qhraw taxus de metallassw o te idea kai o dunamis eis allhlwn kai mentoi egw nun eimi swkraths arti dokazw katanoew o anhr legos an eoika gar atopos tis kaqoraw nai o gar atopos ek agnoia pas sumbainw kai gar dh kai nun autos outos pasxw ecaifnhs amfignoew
english_head: and very many are like satyrs and the weak and cunning beasts; and they make quick exchanges of forms and qualities with one another. Ah, but now, Socrates, I think I have just made out who they are. Tell me; for you seem to have caught sight of something strange. Yes, for ignorance makes things seem strange to everybody. That was what happened to me just now; when I suddenly caught sight of them I did not recognize the troop of those who busy themselves

greek_head: κατιδὼν τὸν περὶ τὰ τῶν πόλεων πράγματα χορόν Ποῖον
greek_tokens: kateidon o peri o o polis pragma xoros poios o pas o sofisths megas gohs kai outos o texnh empeiros os apo o ontws eimi politikos kai basilikos kaiper pagxalepos eimi afairew afaireteos ei mellw eidon enarghs o zhtew alla meis outos ge ou aneteon oukoun dh kata ge o emos kai egw frazw ode o poios
english_head: with the affairs of the state. What troop? That which of all the sophists is the greatest charlatan and most practised in charlatanry. This, although it is a hard thing to do, must be separated from the band of really statesmanlike and kingly men, if we are to get a clear view of the object of our search. But we certainly cannot give that up. No, of course not. I agree to that. And now please answer a question. What is it? We agree that monarchy is one of the forms

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὐ μοναρχία τῶν πολιτικῶν ἡμῖν ἀρχῶν ἐστι μία Ναί
greek_tokens: ara ou monarxia o politikos egw arxh eimi eis nai kai meta monarxia eipon tis an oimh o upo o oligos dunasteia pws de ou tritos de sxhma politeia ou o o plhqos arxh dhmokratia onoma kalew kai panu ge trew de eimi mhs ou pente tropos tis gignomai duh ek eautou allos pros eautou onoma tiktw poios dh
english_head: of government, do we not? Yes. And after monarchy one might, I should say, mention the rule of the few. Yes, of course. And a third form of government is the rule of the multitude, called democracy, is it not? Yes, certainly. Do not these three become after a fashion five, producing out of themselves two additional names? What names?

greek_head: Πρὸς τὸ βίαιόν που καὶ ἑκούσιον ἀποσκοποῦντες νῦν καὶ πενίαν καὶ πλοῦτον καὶ νόμον καὶ ἀνομίαν ἐν αὐταῖς
greek_tokens: pros o biaios pou kai ekousios aposkopew nun kai penia kai ploutos kai nomos kai anomia en autos gignomai diploos ekateros o duo diairew monarxia men prosagoreuw ws duo parexw eidos duo onoma turannis o de basilikos tis meis o de upo oligos ge ekastote kratew polis aristokratia kai oligarxia kai panu ge dhmokratia ge meis ean oun biaios ean ekousios
english_head: People nowadays are likely to take into consideration enforced subjection and voluntary obedience, poverty and wealth, law and lawlessness as they occur in governments, and so they divide two of the forms we mentioned, giving to the two aspects of monarchy the two names tyranny and royalty. Certainly. And the state that is ruled by the few is called, as the case may be, aristocracy or oligarchy. To be sure. In the case of democracy, however,

greek_head: τῶν τὰς οὐσίας ἐχόντων τὸ πλῆθος ἄρχῃ καὶ ἐάντε τοὺς νόμους ἀκριβῶς φυλάττον ἐάντε μή πάντως τοὔνομα οὐδεὶς
greek_tokens: o o ousia exw o plhqos arxw kai ean o nomos akribhs fulassw ean mh pantws onoma oudeis autos eqw metallassw alhqhs tis oun oiomai tis outos o politeia orqos eimi outos o oros orizw eis kai oligos kai polus kai ploutos kai penia kai o biaios kai ekousios kai meta gramma kai aneu nomos sumbainw gignomai tis gar dh kai kwluw
english_head: whether the multitude rule those who have property by violence or with their willing consent, and whether the laws are carefully observed or not, no one ever habitually changes the name. True. Now then, do we believe that any of these forms of government which are defined by the distinctions between the one, the few, and the many, or wealth and poverty, or violence and willingness, or written constitution and absence of laws, is a right one? I don’t see why not.

greek_head: Σκόπει δὴ σαφέστερον τῇδε ἑπόμενος Πῇ
greek_tokens: skopaw dh safhs ode epomai ph o erw kata proteros poteros emmenw h diafwnew o dh poios legw o basilikos arxh o episthmh eimi tis fhmi oimh nai kai outos ge ou apas alla kritikos dhpou tis kai epistatikos ek o allos proairew nai ek o epistatikos o men epi ayuxos ergon
english_head: Look a bit more closely along the line I am going to point out. What is it? Shall we abide by what we said in the beginning, or dissent from it? To what do you refer? We said, I believe, that royal power was one of the sciences. Yes. And not only a science, but we selected it from the rest as a science of judgement and command. Yes. And from the science of command we distinguished one part which rules inanimate works,

greek_head: τὴν δ' ἐπὶ ζῴοις καὶ κατὰ τοῦτον δὴ τὸν τρόπον μερίζοντες δεῦρ' ἀεὶ προεληλύθαμεν ἐπιστήμης οὐκ ἐπιλανθανόμενοι τὸ
greek_tokens: o de epi zwion kai kata outos dh o tropos merizw deuro aei proerxomai episthmh ou epilanqanomai o de ostis ou ikanos pw dunamai diakribow legw orqos outos autos toinun ara ennoew ostis o oros ou oligos oude polus oude o ekousios oude o aekousios oude penia oude ploutos gignomai peri autos xrewn alla tis episthmh eiper akolouqew o prosqen
english_head: and one which rules living beings; and so we have gone on dividing in this manner to the present moment, never forgetting that it is a science, but as yet unable to state with sufficient accuracy what science it is. You are right. Then is this our understanding, that the distinction between forms of government ought not to be found in the words few or many, or voluntary or unwilling, or wealth or poverty, but some science must be the distinguishing feature, if we are to be consistent with our previous statement?

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν τοῦτό γε ἀδύνατον μὴ ποιεῖν Ἐξ ἀνάγκης δὴ νῦν τοῦτο οὕτω σκεπτέον ἐν τίνι
greek_tokens: alla meis outos ge adunatos mh poiew ek anagkh dh nun outos outws skepteos en tis pote outos episthmh sumbainw gignomai peri anqrwpos arxh sxedon o xalepos kai megas ktaomai deomai gar eidon autos ina qeaomai tis afaireteos apo o fronimos basileus e prospoiew men eimi politikos kai peiqw polus eimi de oudamos deomai gar dh poiew outos ws o logos egw proerew
english_head: Yes, indeed; it cannot be otherwise. Necessarily, then, our present duty is to inquire in which, if any, of these forms of government is engendered the science of ruling men, which is about the greatest of sciences and the most difficult to acquire. We must discover that in order to see what men are to be distinguished from the wise king—men, I mean, who pretend to be, and make many believe that they are, statesmen, but are really not such at all. Yes, we must do this; that is implied in what was said before.

greek_head: Μῶν οὖν δοκεῖ πλῆθός γε ἐν πόλει ταύτην τὴν ἐπιστήμην δυνατὸν εἶναι κτήσασθαι
greek_tokens: mhs oun dokew plhqos ge en polis outos o episthmh dunatos eimi ktaomai kai pws alla ara en xiliandros polis dunatos ekaton tis h kai penthkonta autos ikanos ktaomai radios mentoi outws ge eaw pas o texnh oida gar ostis xilioi androw akros pesseuths tosoutos pros o en o allos ellhn ou an gignomai pote mh tis dh basileus ge deomai gar dh o ge o basilikos exw episthmh an su arxw kai ean mh kata o emprosqen
english_head: Does it seem at all possible that a multitude in a state could acquire this science? By no means. But in a state of one thousand men could perhaps a hundred or as many as fifty acquire it adequately? No, in that case this would be the easiest of all the arts; for we know that a city of a thousand men could never produce that number of finished draught-players in comparison with those in other Greek cities, still less so many kings. For the man who possesses the kingly science, whether he rule or not,

greek_head: λόγον ὅμως βασιλικὸν προσαγορεύεσθαι Καλῶς ἀπεμνημόνευσας ἑπόμενον δὲ οἶμαι τούτῳ
greek_tokens: logos omws basilikos prosagoreuw kalos apomnhmoneuw epomai de oimh outos o men orqos arxh peri enos tis kai duo kai pantapasi oligos deomai zhtew otan orqos gignomai tis meis outos de ge ean ekwn anta akwn arxw ean kata gramma ean aneu gramma kai ean ploutew h penomai nomisteos wsper nun ageomai kata texnh ostis arxh arxw o iatros
english_head: must be called kingly, as our previous argument showed. You did well to remind me. And in agreement with this, we must, I suppose, look for the right kind of rule in one or two or very few men, whenever such right rule occurs. Certainly. And these men, whether they rule over willing or unwilling subjects, with or without written laws, and whether they are rich or poor, must, according to our present opinion, be supposed to exercise their rule in accordance with some art or science.

greek_head: δὲ οὐχ ἥκιστα νενομίκαμεν ἐάντε ἑκόντας ἐάντε ἄκοντας ἡμᾶς ἰῶνται τέμνοντες ἢ κάοντες ἤ τινα ἄλλην ἀλγηδόνα
greek_tokens: de ou hkistos nomizw ean ekwn ean akwn egw iaomai temnw h kaiw h tis allos alghdwn prosaptw kai ean kata gramma h xwris gramma kai ean penhs eimi h plousios pantws oudeis hsswn iatros fhmi ewsper an epistatew texnh kaqairw eite allos isxnainw eite kai aucanw an monos epi agaqos o o swma beltiwn poiew ek xeirwn
english_head: And physicians offer a particularly good example of this point of view. Whether they cure us against our will or with our will, by cutting us or burning us or causing us pain in any other way, and whether they do it by written rules or without them, and whether they are rich or poor, we call them physicians just the same, so long as they exercise authority by art or science, purging us or reducing us in some other way, or even adding to our weight, provided only that they who treat their patients treat them for the benefit of their health

greek_head: σῴζωσιν οἱ θεραπεύοντες ἕκαστοι τὰ θεραπευόμενα ταύτῃ θήσομεν ὡς οἶμαι καὶ οὐκ ἄλλῃ τοῦτον ὅρον ὀρθὸν εἶναι
greek_tokens: swzw e qerapeuw ekastos o qerapeuw outos tiqhmi ws oimh kai ou allh outos oros orqos eimi monos iatrikos kai allos ostis arxh komidh men oun anagkaion dh kai politeia ws eoika outos orqos diaferontws eimi kai monos politeia en h tis an euriskw o arxw alhqhs episthmwn kai ou dokew monos ean kata nomos ean aneu nomos arxw kai ekwn h
english_head: and preserve them by making them better than they were. In this way and no other, in my opinion, shall we determine this to be the only right definition of the rule of the physician or of any other rule whatsoever. Very true. It is, then, a necessary consequence that among forms of government that one is preeminently right and is the only real government, in which the rulers are found to be truly possessed of science, not merely to seem to possess it, whether they rule by law or without law, whether their subjects are willing or unwilling,

greek_head: ἀκόντων καὶ πενόμενοι ἢ πλουτοῦντες τούτων ὑπολογιστέον οὐδὲν οὐδαμῶς εἶναι κατ' οὐδεμίαν ὀρθότητα
greek_tokens: akwn kai penomai h ploutew outos upologisteon oudeis oudamos eimi kata oudeis orqoths kalos kai ean ge apokteinw tis h kai ekballw kaqairw epi agaqos o polis eite kai apoikia oios smhnh melissa ekpempw poi mikros poiew h tis epeisagw poqen allos ecwqen poliths poiew autos aucanw ewsper an episthmh kai o dikaios prosxraomai swzw ek xeirwn beltiwn poiew kata dunamis
english_head: and whether they themselves are rich or poor—none of these things can be at all taken into account on any right method. Excellent. And whether they purge the state for its good by killing or banishing some of the citizens, or make it smaller by sending out colonies somewhere, as bees swarm from the hive, or bring in citizens from elsewhere to make it larger, so long as they act in accordance with science and justice and preserve and benefit it by making it better than it was, so far as is possible,

greek_head: ταύτην τότε καὶ κατὰ τοὺς τοιούτους ὅρους ἡμῖν μόνην ὀρθὴν πολιτείαν εἶναι ῥητέον ὅσας δ' ἄλλας λέγομεν οὐ γνησίας
greek_tokens: outos tote kai kata o toioutos oros egw monos orqos politeia eimi rhteos osos de allos legw ou gnhsios oude ontws eimi lekteos alla mimeomai outos os men ws eunomos legw epi o kallias o de allos epi o aisxros mimeomai o men allos eimi cenos metrios eoika erw o de kai aneu nomos dew arxw xalepos akouw erw mikros ge fqanw egw eromai eimi swkraths mellw
english_head: that must at that time and by such characteristics be declared to be the only right form of government. All other forms must be considered not as legitimate or really existent, but as imitating this; those states which are said to be well governed imitate it better, and the others worse. Everything else that you have said seems reasonable; but that government should be carried on without laws is a hard saying. You got ahead of me a little with your question, Socrates;

greek_head: γάρ σε διερωτήσειν ταῦτα πότερον ἀποδέχῃ πάντα ἤ τι καὶ δυσχεραίνεις τῶν λεχθέντων νῦν δ' ἤδη φανερὸν ὅτι
greek_tokens: gar su dierwtaw outos poteros apodexomai pas h tis kai dusxerainw o legw nun de hdh faneros ostis outos boulomai o peri o o aneu nomos arxw orqoths dierxomai egw pws gar ou tropos tis mentoi dhlos ostis o basilikos eimi o nomoqetikos o de ariston ou o nomos eimi isxuw alla anhr o meta fronhsis basilikos oistos oph ph dh legw ostis nomos ou an pote dunamai o te ariston kai
english_head: for I was just going to ask whether you accepted all I have said, or were displeased with anything. But now it is clear that we shall have to discuss the question of the propriety of government without laws. Of course we shall. In a sense, however, it is clear that law-making belongs to the science of kingship; but the best thing is not that the laws be in power, but that the man who is wise and of kingly nature be ruler. Do you see why? Why is it? Because law could never, by determining exactly what is noblest

greek_head: τὸ δικαιότατον ἀκριβῶς πᾶσιν ἅμα περιλαβὼν τὸ βέλτιστον ἐπιτάττειν αἱ γὰρ ἀνομοιότητες τῶν τε ἀνθρώπων καὶ τῶν
greek_tokens: o dikaios akribhs pas ama perilambanw o beltistos epitassw o gar anomoioths o te anqrwpos kai o pracis kai o mhdepote mhdeis ws epos eipon hsuxia agw o anqrwpinos oudeis eaw aploos en oudeis peri apas kai epi pas o xronos apofainw texnh oude ostis outos dh sugxwrew pou tis meis o de ge nomos oraw sxedon epi autos outos
english_head: and must just for one and all, enjoin upon them that which is best; for the differences of men and of actions and the fact that nothing, I may say, in human life is ever at rest, forbid any science whatsoever to promulgate any simple rule for everything and for all time. We agree to that, I suppose? Yes, of course. But we see that law aims at pretty nearly this very thing, like a stubborn and ignorant man

greek_head: συντείνοντα ὥσπερ τινὰ ἄνθρωπον αὐθάδη καὶ ἀμαθῆ καὶ μηδένα μηδὲν ἐῶντα ποιεῖν παρὰ τὴν ἑαυτοῦ τάξιν μηδ'
greek_tokens: sunteinw wsper tis anqrwpos auqadhs kai amaqhs kai mhdeis mhdeis eaw poiew para o eautou tacis mhde eperwtaw mhdeis mhde an tis neos ara tis sumbainw beltiwn para o logos os autos epitassw alhqhs poiew gar atexnos kaqa erw nun o nomos egw ekastos oukoun adunatos eu exw pros o mhdepote aploos o dia pas gignomai aploos kinduneuw dia tis dh pote oun anagkaion nomoqetew epei
english_head: who allows no one to do anything contrary to his command, or even to ask a question, not even if something new occurs to some one, which is better than the rule he has himself ordained. True; the law treats each and all of us exactly as you describe. So that which is persistently simple is inapplicable to things which are never simple? I suppose so. Why in the world, then, is it necessary to make laws, since law is not the most perfect right?

greek_head: οὐκ ὀρθότατον ὁ νόμος ἀνευρετέον τούτου τὴν αἰτίαν Τί μήν
greek_tokens: ou orqos o nomos aneureteon outos o aitios tis meis oukoun kai para su eimi tis oios kai en allos polis aqroos anqrwpos askhsis eite pros dromos eite pros allos tis filoneikia eneka kai panu ge polus ferw nun analambanw palin mnhmh o o texnh gumnazw epitacis en o toioutos arxh o poios ostis leptourgew ou egxwrew hgeomai kaqa enos ekastos o swma o proshkw ekastos prostassw
english_head: We must ask the reason for this. Yes, of course. Well, there are here at Athens, as in other cities, classes for practice in athletics to prepare for contests in running or the like, are there not? Yes, a great many of them. Now let us recall to mind the orders given by the professional trainers when they are in charge of such classes. What do you mean? They think they cannot go into details in individual cases and order what is best for each person’s physique;

greek_head: ἀλλὰ παχύτερον οἴονται δεῖν ὡς ἐπὶ τὸ πολὺ καὶ ἐπὶ πολλοὺς τὴν τοῦ λυσιτελοῦντος τοῖς σώμασι ποιεῖσθαι τάξιν
greek_tokens: alla paxus oiomai dew ws epi o polus kai epi polus o o lusitelew o swma poiew tacis kalos dio dh ge kai isos ponos nun didwmi aqroos ama men ecormaw ama de kai katapauw dromos kai pala kai pas o kata o swma ponos eimi outos kai o nomoqeths toinun hgeomai o o agelh epistatew o dikaios peri kai o pros allhlwn
english_head: they think they must employ a rougher method and give a general rule which will be good for the physique of the majority. Good. And therefore they nowadays assign equal exercise to whole classes; they make them begin at the same time and stop at the same time, whether they run or wrestle or practise any other kind of bodily exercise. That is true. And so we must believe that the law-maker who is to watch over the herds and maintain justice and the obligation of contracts,

greek_head: συμβολαίων μή ποθ' ἱκανὸν γενήσεσθαι πᾶσιν ἁθρόοις προστάττοντα ἀκριβῶς ἑνὶ ἑκάστῳ τὸ προσῆκον ἀποδιδόναι
greek_tokens: sumbolaion mh poqh ikanos gignomai pas aqroos prostassw akribhs eis ekastos o proshkw apodidwmi o goun eoika alla o o polus ge oimh kai ws epi o polus kai pws outwsi paxus ekastos o nomos tiqhmi kai en gramma apodidwmi kai en agrammatos patrios de eqos nomoqeths orqos orqos mentoi pws gar an tis ikanos gignomai an
english_head: will never be able by making laws for all collectively, to provide exactly that which is proper for each individual. Probably not, at any rate. But he will, I fancy, legislate for the majority and in a general way only roughly for individuals, whether he issues written laws or his enactments follow the unwritten traditional customs. Quite right. Yes, quite right. For how could anyone, Socrates,

greek_head: ποτε ὦ Σώκρατες ὥστε διὰ βίου ἀεὶ παρακαθήμενος ἑκάστῳ δι' ἀκριβείας προστάττειν τὸ προσῆκον ἐπεὶ τοῦτ' ἂν δυνατὸς
greek_tokens: pote eimi swkraths wste dia bios aei parakaqhmai ekastos dia akribeia prostassw o proshkw epei outos an dunatos eimi ws oimh o o basilikos ostis ontws episthmh lambanw sxolazw pote an eautou qew empodisma grafw o legw outos nomos ek o nun goun eimi cenos erw mallon de ge eimi beltistos ek o mellw erw tinw dh o toiosde eipon gar dh pros ge egw autos
english_head: sit beside each person all his life and tell him exactly what is proper for him to do? Certainly anyone who really possessed the kingly science, if he were able to do this, would hardly, I imagine, ever put obstacles in his own way by writing what we call laws. No, at least not according to what has just been said. Or rather, my friend, not according to what is going to be said. What is that? Something of this sort: Let us suppose

greek_head: ἰατρὸν μέλλοντα ἢ καί τινα γυμναστικὸν ἀποδημεῖν καὶ ἀπέσεσθαι τῶν θεραπευομένων συχνόν ὡς οἴοιτο χρόνον
greek_tokens: iatros mellw h kai tis gumnastikos apodhmew kai apeimi o qerapeuw suxnos ws oiomai xronos mh mnhmoneuw oiomai o prosstazw o gumnazw h o kamnw upomnhma grafw an eqelw autos h pws outws tis de ei para doca elasswn xronos apodhmew erxomai palin ara ou an para ekeinos o gramma tolmaw allos upotiqhmi sumbainw allos beltiwn o kamnw
english_head: that a physician or a gymnastic trainer is going away and expects to be a long time absent from his patients or pupils; if he thinks they will not remember his instructions, he would want to write them down, would he not? Yes. What if he should come back again after a briefer absence than he expected? Would he not venture to substitute other rules for those written instructions if others happened to be better for his patients,

greek_head: διὰ πνεύματα ἤ τι καὶ ἄλλο παρὰ τὴν ἐλπίδα τῶν ἐκ Διὸς ἑτέρως πως τῶν εἰωθότων γενόμενα καρτερῶν δ' ἂν
greek_tokens: dia pneuma h tis kai allos para o elpis o ek zeus eteros pws o eqw gignomai karterew de an hgeomai dew mh ekbainw arxaios pote nomoqetew mhte autos prostassw allos mhte o kamnw eteros tolmaw para o grafw draw ws outos eimi iatrikos kai ugieinos o de eteros gignomai noswdhs te kai ou entexnos h pas o toioutos en ge episthmh sumbainw kai
english_head: because the winds or something else had, by act of God, changed unexpectedly from their usual course? Would he persist in the opinion that no one must transgress the old laws, neither he himself by enacting new ones nor his patient by venturing to do anything contrary to the written rules, under the conviction that these laws were medicinal and healthful and anything else was unhealthful and unscientific? If anything of that sort occurred in the realm of science and true art,

greek_head: ἀληθεῖ τέχνῃ περὶ ἅπαντα παντάπασι γέλως ἂν ὁ μέγιστος γίγνοιτο τῶν τοιούτων νομοθετημάτων
greek_tokens: alhqhs texnh peri apas pantapasi gelws an o megas gignomai o toioutos nomoqethma pantapasi men oun o de o dikaios dh kai adikos kai kalos kai aisxros kai agaqos kai kakos grafw kai agrafos nomoqetew o o anqrwpos agelh oposos kata polis en ekastos nomeuw kata o o grafw nomos an o meta texnh grafw h tis eteros omoios afikneomai mh ecesti dh
english_head: would not any such regulations on any subject assuredly arouse the greatest ridicule? Most assuredly. But he who has made written or unwritten laws about the just and unjust, the honorable and disgraceful, the good and the bad for the herds of men that are tended in their several cities in accordance with the laws of the law-makers, is not to be permitted to give other laws contrary to those, if the scientific law-maker, or another like him, should come!

greek_head: παρὰ ταῦτα ἕτερα προστάττειν ἢ καὶ τοῦτο τὸ ἀπόρρημα οὐδὲν ἧττον ἂν ἐκείνου τῇ ἀληθείᾳ γελοῖον φαίνοιτο
greek_tokens: para outos eteros prostassw h kai outos o aporrhma oudeis hsswn an ekeinos o alhqeia geloios fainw tis meis oistos oun epi o toioutos logos o para o polus legw ou ennoew nun ge outws kai meis euprephs fhmi gar dh dew ei tis gignwskw para o o emprosqen beltiwn nomos nomoqetew o eautou polis ekastos peiqw allos de mh tis oun ou orqos
english_head: Would not such a prohibition appear in truth as ridiculous as the other? It certainly would. Do you know what people in general say about such a case? I don’t recall it just now off-hand. Yes, it is very plausible; for they say that if anyone has anything better than the old laws to offer, he must first persuade the state, and then he may make his laws, but not otherwise. And is that not right?

greek_head: Ἴσως ἂν δ' οὖν μὴ πείθων τις βιάζηται τὸ βέλτιον ἀπόκριναι τί τοὔνομα τῆς βίας ἔσται μὴ μέντοι πω
greek_tokens: isos an de oun mh peiqw tis biazw o beltiwn apokrinw tis onoma o bia eimi mh mentoi pw peri de o emprosqen proteros poios dh legw an tis ara mh peiqw o iatreuw exw de orqos o texnh para o grafw o beltiwn anagkazw draw pais h tis anhr h kai gunh tis onoma o bia eimi outos ara ou pas mallon h o para o texnh legw amarthma o noswdhs kai pas orqos
english_head: Perhaps. But suppose a man does not use persuasion, but makes an improvement by force. What is this force to be called? Answer me—or, no, not yet; first answer in reference to what we were talking of before. What do you mean? Suppose a physician who has right knowledge of his profession does not persuade, but forces, his patient, whether man, woman, or child, to do the better thing, though it be contrary to the written precepts, what will such violence be called? The last name in the world to call it would be unscientific and baneful error, as the phrase is, would it not?

greek_head: εἰπεῖν ἔστι πρότερον τῷ βιασθέντι περὶ τὸ τοιοῦτον πλὴν ὅτι νοσώδη καὶ ἄτεχνα πέπονθεν ὑπὸ τῶν βιασαμένων ἰατρῶν
greek_tokens: eipon eimi proteros o biazw peri o toioutos plhn ostis noswdhs kai atexnos pasxw upo o biaw iatros alhqhs legw tis de egw dh o para o politikos texnh amarthma legw eimi ara ou o aisxros kai o kakos kai adikos pantapasi ge o dh biazw para o grafw kai patrios draw eteros dikaios kai ameinwn kai kallias o emprosqen
english_head: And the patient so forced might rightly say anything else rather than that he had been treated in a baneful or unscientific way by the physicians who used force upon him. Very true. But what can we call the unscientific error in the field of statesmanship? Is it not baseness and evil and injustice? Certainly. Now if people are forced, contrary to the written laws and inherited traditions, to do what is juster and nobler and better than what they did before,

greek_head: φέρε τὸν τῶν τοιούτων αὖ ψόγον περὶ τῆς τοιαύτης βίας ἆρ' εἰ μέλλει μὴ καταγελαστότατος εἶναι πάντων πάντ'
greek_tokens: ferw o o toioutos au yogos peri o toioutos bia ara ei mellw mh katagelastos eimi pas panth autos mallon lekteos ekastote plhn ws aisxros kai adikos kai kakos pasxw e biazw upo o biaw alhqhs legw alla ara ean men plousios o biaw eimi dikaios an de ara penhs adikos o biazw eimi h kan peiqw kan mh peiqw tis plousios h penhs h kata gramma h
english_head: tell me, will not anyone who blames such use of force, unless he is to be most utterly ridiculous, always say anything or everything rather than that those who have been so forced have suffered base and unjust and evil treatment at the hands of those who forced them? Very true. But would the violence be just if he who uses it is rich, and unjust if he is poor? Or if a man, whether rich or poor, by persuasion or by other means, in accordance with written laws or contrary to them, does what is for the good of the people, must not this be the truest criterion of right government,

english_head: which is committed in relation to it nowadays. What is the error? I will tell you what we must investigate; it is not at all familiar or easy to see, but let us try to grasp it nevertheless. Tell me this: Assuming that the form of government we have described is the only right form, don’t you see that the other forms must employ its written laws if they are to be preserved by doing that which is approved of nowadays, although it is not perfectly right? What is not perfectly right? That no citizen shall dare to do anything contrary to the laws, and that he who does shall be punished by death

english_head: and the most extreme penalties. And this is perfectly right and good as a second choice, as soon as you depart from the first form of which we were just speaking. Now let us tell in some detail how this which we called the second choice comes about. Shall we do so? By all means. Let us return once more to the images which we always have to use in portraying kingly rulers. What images? The noble captain of a ship and the physician who is worth as much as many others. Let us make a simile of them and use it to help us to discover something. What is your simile?

They cut us up and burn us and order us to bring them payments of money, as if they were exacting tribute, of which they spend little or nothing for their patients; they themselves and their servants use the rest.
