Thus, we say, the tyrant has arisen, and the king and oligarchy and aristocracy and democracy, because men are not contented with that one perfect ruler, and do not believe that there could ever be any one worthy of such power or

greek_head: ὥστε ἐθέλειν καὶ δυνατὸν εἶναι μετ' ἀρετῆς καὶ ἐπιστήμης ἄρχοντα τὰ δίκαια καὶ ὅσια διανέμειν ὀρθῶς πᾶσιν λωβᾶσθαι
greek_tokens: wste eqelw kai dunatos eimi meta aretaw kai episthmh arxw o dikaios kai osios dianemw orqos pas lwbaomai de kai apokteinw kai kakow os an boulomai ekastote egw epei gignomai ge an oios legw agapaw te an kai oikew diakubernaw eudaimwn orqos akribhs monos politeia pws de ou nun de ge opote ou eimi gignomai ws dh
english_head: willing and able by ruling with virtue and knowledge to dispense justice and equity rightly to all, but that he will harm and kill and injure any one of us whom he chooses on any occasion, since they admit that if such a man as we describe should really arise, he would be welcomed and would continue to dwell among them, directing to their weal as sole ruler a perfectly right form of government. Certainly. But, as the case now stands, since, as we claim, no king is produced in our states who is, like the ruler of the bees in their hives, by birth pre-eminently fitted from the beginning

greek_head: φαμεν ἐν ταῖς πόλεσι βασιλεὺς οἷος ἐν σμήνεσιν ἐμφύεται τό τε σῶμα εὐθὺς καὶ τὴν ψυχὴν διαφέρων εἷς δεῖ δὴ
greek_tokens: fhmi en o polis basileus oios en smhnos emfuw o te swma euqus kai o yuxh diaferw eis deomai dh sunerxomai suggramma grafw ws eoika metaqew o o alhqhs politeia ixnos kinduneuw qaumazw dhta eimi swkraths en o toioutos politeia osos sumbainw gignomai kakos kai osos sumbainw toioutos o krhpis upokeimai autos o kata gramma kai eqos mh meta episthmh prassw o
english_head: in body and mind, we are obliged, as it seems, to follow in the track of the perfect and true form of government by coming together and making written laws. Yes, I suppose we are. Can we wonder, then, Socrates, at all the evils that arise and are destined to arise in such kinds of government, when they are based upon such a foundation, and must conduct their affairs in accordance with written laws and with customs, without knowledge?

greek_head: πράξεις ᾗ ἑτέρα προσχρωμένη παντὶ κατάδηλος ὡς πάντ' ἂν διολέσειε τὰ ταύτῃ γιγνόμενα ἢ ἐκεῖνο ἡμῖν θαυμαστέον
greek_tokens: prassw h eteros prosxraomai pas katadhlos ws panth an diollumi o outos gignomai h ekeinos egw qaumasteos mallon ws isxuros tis polis eimi fusis pasxw gar dh toioutos o polis nun xronos aperantos omws enioi tis autos monimos su eimi kai ou anatrepw polus meis eniote kai kaqa ploion kataduw diollumi kai diollumi kai eti diollumi dia o o kubernhths kai nauths moxqhria o peri o megas megas agnoia
english_head: For every one can see that any other art built upon such a foundation would ruin all its works that are so produced. Ought we not rather to wonder at the stability that inheres in the state? For states have labored under such conditions for countless ages, nevertheless some of them are lasting and are not overthrown. Many, to be sure, like ships that founder at sea, are destroyed, have been destroyed, and will be destroyed hereafter, through the worthlessness of their captains and crews who have the greatest ignorance of the greatest things,

greek_head: εἰληφότων οἳ περὶ τὰ πολιτικὰ κατ' οὐδὲν γιγνώσκοντες ἡγοῦνται κατὰ πάντα σαφέστατα πασῶν ἐπιστημῶν ταύτην
greek_tokens: lambanw e peri o politikos kata oudeis gignwskw hgeomai kata pas safhs pas episthmh outos lambanw alhqhs tis oun dh o ou orqos politeia outos hkistos xalepos suzaw pas xalepos eimi kai tis barus deomai tis kateidon egw kaiper pros ge o nun protiqhmi egw parergon legw ou meis alla eis ge o oloc isos apas eneka o toioutos pas draw xaris deomai pws de ou
english_head: men who have no knowledge of statesmanship, but think they have in every respect most perfect knowledge of this above all other sciences. Very true. Is it, then, our duty to see which of these not right forms of government is the least difficult to live with, though all are difficult, and which is the most oppressive, although this is somewhat aside from the subject we had proposed for ourselves? On the whole, however, perhaps all of us have some such motive in mind in all that we are doing. Yes, it is our duty, of course.

greek_head: Τὴν αὐτὴν τοίνυν φάθι τριῶν οὐσῶν χαλεπὴν διαφερόντως γίγνεσθαι καὶ ῥᾴστην
greek_tokens: o autos toinun fhmi treis eimi xalepos diaferontws gignomai kai radios pws fhmi ou allos plhn monarxia fhmi kai oligos arxh kai polus eimi trew outos egw legw o nun epixew logos kata arxh eimi gar oun outos toinun dixa temnw eis ekastos ec poiew o orqos xwris apokrinw outos ebdomos pws
english_head: Well then, you may say that of the three forms, the same is both the hardest and the easiest. What do you mean? Just this: I mean that there are three forms of government, as we said at the beginning of the discussion which has now flowed in upon us—monarchy, the rule of the few, and the rule of the many. Yes, there were those three. Let us, then, by dividing each of these into two parts, make six, and by distinguishing the right government from these, a seventh. How shall we make the division?

greek_head: Ἐκ μὲν τῆς μοναρχίας βασιλικὴν καὶ τυραννικήν ἐκ δ' αὖ τῶν μὴ πολλῶν τήν τε εὐώνυμον ἔφαμεν εἶναι
greek_tokens: ek men o monarxia basilikos kai turannikos ek de au o mh polus o te euwnumos fhmi eimi aristokratia kai oligarxia ek de au o polus tote men aploos eponomazw tiqhmi dhmokratia nun de au kai outos egw qeteos eimi diploos pws dh kai tis diairew outos oudeis diaferw o allos oude ei onoma hdh
english_head: We said that monarchy comprised royalty and tyranny, and the rule of the few comprised aristocracy, which has a name of good omen, and oligarchy; but to the rule of the many we gave then only a single name, democracy; now, however, that also must be divided. How? On what principle shall we divide that? On the same that we used for the others, though the name of this form is already twofold in meaning. At any rate, the distinction between ruling according to law

greek_head: διπλοῦν ἐστι ταύτης ἀλλὰ τό γε κατὰ νόμους ἄρχειν καὶ παρανόμως ἔστι καὶ ταύτῃ καὶ ταῖς ἄλλαις
greek_tokens: diploos eimi outos alla o ge kata nomos arxw kai paranomos eimi kai outos kai o allos eimi gar oun tote men toinun o orqos zhtew outos o tmhma ou eimi xrhsimos ws en o prosqen apodeiknumi epei de ecairew ekeinos o de allos eqemen anagkh en outos dh o paranomos kai ennomos ekastos dixotomew outos eoika outos nun erw o logos monarxia toinun zeugnumi men en gramma agaqos os nomos legw aristos pas o ec anomos de xalepos kai barus sunoikew
english_head: and without law applies alike to this and the rest. Yes, it does. Before, when we were in search of the right government, this division was of no use, as we showed at the time but now that we have set that apart and have decided that the others are the only available forms of government, the principle of lawfulness and lawlessness bisects each of them. So it seems, from what has been said. Monarchy, then, when bound by good written rules, which we call laws, is the best of all the six; but without law it is hard and most oppressive to live with.

greek_head: Κινδυνεύει Τὴν δέ γε τῶν μὴ πολλῶν ὥσπερ ἑνὸς καὶ πλήθους
greek_tokens: kinduneuw o de ge o mh polus wsper eis kai plhqos o oligos mesos outws hgeomai mesh epi amfoteros o de au o plhqos kata pas asqenhs kai mhdeis mhte agaqos mhte kakos megas dunamai ws pros o allos dia o o arxh en outos dianemw kata mikros eis polus dio gignomai pas men nomimos o politeia eimi outos xeiristos paranomos de eimi sumpas beltistos
english_head: I fancy it is. But just as few is intermediate between one and a multitude, so the government of the few must be considered intermediate, both in good and in evil. But the government of the multitude is weak in all respects and able to do nothing great, either good or bad, when compared with the other forms of government, because in this the powers of government are distributed in small shares among many men; therefore of all these governments when they are lawful, this is the worst, and when they are all lawless it is the best;

greek_head: καὶ ἀκολάστων μὲν πασῶν οὐσῶν ἐν δημοκρατίᾳ νικᾷ ζῆν κοσμίων δ' οὐσῶν ἥκιστα ἐν ταύτῃ βιωτέον ἐν τῇ πρώτῃ
greek_tokens: kai akolastos men pas eimi en dhmokratia nikaw zaw kosmion de eimi hkistos en outos biwteon en o proteros de polus proteros te kai ariston plhn o ebdomos pas gar ekeinos ge ekkriteon oios qeos ek anqrwpos ek o allos politeia fainw outos outws sumbainw te kai gignomai kai poihteos hper legw oukoun dh kai o koinwnos outos o politeia
english_head: and if they are all without restraint, life is most desirable in a democracy, but if they are orderly, that is the worst to live in; but life in the first kind of state is by far the first and best, with the exception of the seventh, for that must be set apart from all the others, as God is set apart from men. That statement appears to be true to the facts, and we must do as you say. Then those who participate in all those governments with the exception of the scientific one—are to be eliminated

greek_head: πασῶν πλὴν τῆς ἐπιστήμονος ἀφαιρετέον ὡς οὐκ ὄντας πολιτικοὺς ἀλλὰ στασιαστικούς καὶ εἰδώλων μεγίστων προστάτας
greek_tokens: pas plhn o episthmwn afaireteos ws ou eimi politikos alla stasiastikos kai eidwlon megas prostaths eimi kai autos eimi toioutos megas de eimi mimhths kai gohs megas gignomai o sofisths sofisths kinduneuw outos eis o politikos legw peristrefw o rhma orqos eien outos men atexnos egw wsper drama kaqa erw nundi kentaurikos oraw kai saturikos tis
english_head: as not being statesmen, but partisans and since they preside over the greatest counterfeits, they are themselves counterfeits, and since they are the greatest of imitators and cheats, they are the greatest of all sophists. This term sophist seems to have come round quite rightly to the so-called statesmen. Well, this part has been exactly like a play. Just as we remarked a moment ago, a festive troop of centaurs or satyrs was coming into view, which we had to separate from the art of statesmanship;

greek_head: θίασον ὃν δὴ χωριστέον ἀπὸ πολιτικῆς εἴη τέχνης νῦν δ' οὕτω πάνυ μόγις ἐχωρίσθη
greek_tokens: qiasos os dh xwristeos apo politikos eaw texnh nun de outws panu mogis xwrizw fainw outos de ge eteros eti xalepos leipw o suggenhs te omos su eimi mallon o basilikos genos kai duskatamaqhtos kai egw fainw o o xrusos kaqairw paqos omoios pasxw pws gaia pou kai liqos kai polus atta eteros apokrinw kai ekeinos proteros e dhmiourgos meta de outos
english_head: and now we have succeeded in doing this, though it has been very difficult. So it seems. But another group remains, which is still more difficult to separate, because it is more closely akin to the kingly class and is also harder to recognize. I think we are in somewhat the same position as refiners of gold. How so? Why, the refiners first remove earth and stones and all that sort of thing;

greek_head: λείπεται συμμεμειγμένα τὰ συγγενῆ τοῦ χρυσοῦ τίμια καὶ πυρὶ μόνον ἀφαιρετά χαλκὸς καὶ ἄργυρος ἔστι δ' ὅτε καὶ
greek_tokens: leipw summignumi o suggeneus o xruseos timios kai pur monos afairetos xalkos kai arguros eimi de oste kai adamas o meta basanos o eyhsis mogis afairew o legw akhratos xrusos eaw egw eidon autos monos epi eautou legw gar oun dh outos outws gignomai kata o autos toinun logos eoika kai nun egw o men eteros kai oposos allotrios kai o mh filos politikos episthmh apoxwrizw leipw de o timios kai suggeneus outos de eimi pou strathgia kai dikastikos kai osos
english_head: and after that there remain the precious substances which are mixed with the gold and akin to it and can be removed only by fire—copper and silver and sometimes adamant. These are removed by the difficult processes of smelting and tests, leaving before our eyes what is called unalloyed gold in all its purity. Yes, that is said, at least, to be the process. By the same method I think all that is different and alien and incompatible has now been eliminated by us from the science of statesmanship, and what is precious and akin to it is left. Herein are included the arts

greek_head: βασιλικῇ κοινωνοῦσα ῥητορεία πείθουσα τὸ δίκαιον συνδιακυβερνᾷ τὰς ἐν ταῖς πόλεσι πράξεις ἃ δὴ τίνι τρόπῳ ῥᾷστά
greek_tokens: basilikos koinwnew rhtoreia peiqw o dikaios sundiakubernaw o en o polis prassw o dh tis tropos radios tis apomerizw deicis gumnos kai monos ekeinos kaqa eautou o zhtew ufh egw dhlos ostis outos ph draw peirateon peirw men toinun eneka faneros eimi dia de mousikh autos egxeirhteon dhlow kai egw legos o poios
english_head: of the general and of the judge and that kind of oratory which partakes of the kingly art because it persuades men to justice and thereby helps to steer the ship of state. Now in what way shall we most easily eliminate these and show him whom we seek alone by himself and undisguised? Clearly we must do this somehow. Then if it is a question of trying, he will be shown. But I think we had better try to disclose him by means of music. Please answer my question. What is it?

greek_head: Μουσικῆς ἔστι πού τις ἡμῖν μάθησις καὶ ὅλως τῶν περὶ χειροτεχνίας ἐπιστημῶν
greek_tokens: mousikh eimi pou tis egw maqhsis kai oloc o peri xeirotexnia episthmh eimi tis de o de au outos ostis eite deomai manqanw egw eite mh poteros fhmi episthmh au kai outos eimi tis peri autos outos h pws outws eimi fhmi oukoun eteros omologew ekeinos eimi outos nai poteros de autos oudeis arxw dew allos allos
english_head: Shall we agree that there is such a thing as learning music and the sciences of handicraft in general? There is. And how about this? Shall we say that there is another science connected with those, which tells whether we ought or ought not to learn any one of then? Yes, we shall say that there is. And shall we agree that this is different from those? Yes. And shall we say that none of them ought to have control of any other,

greek_head: ἢ ἐκείνας ταύτης ἢ ταύτην δεῖν ἐπιτροπεύουσαν ἄρχειν συμπασῶν τῶν ἄλλων
greek_tokens: h ekeinos outos h outos dew epitropeuw arxw sumpas o allos outos ekeinos o ei deomai manqanw h mh o manqanw kai didaskw ara su ge apofainw dew egw arxw sfodra ge kai o ei deomai peiqw ara h mh o dunamai peiqw pws de ou eien tis o peistikos oun apodidwmi episthmh
english_head: or that those sciences should control this one, or that this should control and rule all the others? This should control those others. You mean that the science which decides whether we ought to learn or not should control the science which is learnt or teaches? Emphatically. And the science which decides whether to persuade or not should control that which can persuade? Certainly. Well, then, to what science shall we assign the power of persuading a multitude or a mob by telling edifying stories,

greek_head: πλήθους τε καὶ ὄχλου διὰ μυθολογίας ἀλλὰ μὴ διὰ διδαχῆς
greek_tokens: plhqos te kai oxlos dia muqologia alla mh dia didaxh faneros oimh kai outos rhtorikos doteos eimi o de eite dia peiqw eite kai dia tis bia deomai prassw pros tis otioun h kai o parapan hsuxia exw outos au poa prosqew episthmh o o peistikos arxw kai lektikos eaw de an ou allos tis ws oimh plhn o o politikos dunamis kallista erw kai outos men eoika taxus xwrizw politikos o
english_head: not by teaching? It is, I think, clear that this must be added to rhetoric. But the power of deciding whether some action, no matter what, should be taken, either by persuasion or by some exercise of force, in relation to any person, or whether to take no action at all—to what science is that to be assigned? To the science which controls the sciences of persuasion and speech. And that would, I think, be no other than the function of the statesman. A most excellent conclusion. So rhetoric also seems to have been quickly separated from statesmanship

greek_head: ῥητορικόν ὡς ἕτερον εἶδος ὄν ὑπηρετοῦν μὴν ταύτῃ Ναί
greek_tokens: rhtorikos ws eteros eidos eimi uphretew meis outos nai tis de peri o toiosde au dunamis dianohteon poa o ws polemhteon ekastos os an proairew polemew eite autos atexnos eite entexnos erew kai pws an atexnos dianoeomai os ge o strathgikos kai pas o polemikos pracis prassw o de eite polemhteon eite dia filios apallakteon oios te kai episthmwn diabouleuomai outos eteros upolambanw h o autos outos o prosqen anagkaion epomai eteros
english_head: as a different species, subservient to the other. Yes. Here is another function or power; what are we to think about it? What is it? The power of determining how war shall be waged against those upon whom we have declared war, whether we are to call this a science or not a science? How could we think it is not a science, when generalship and all military activity practise it? And the power which is able and knows how to deliberate and decide whether to make war or peace, shall we assume that it is the same as this or different? If we are consistent, we must assume that it is different.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἄρχουσαν ταύτης αὐτὴν ἀποφανούμεθα εἴπερ τοῖς ἔμπροσθέν γε ὑποληψόμεθα ὁμοίως
greek_tokens: oukoun arxw outos autos apofainw eiper o emprosqen ge upolambanw omoios fhmi tinw oun pote kai epixeirew outws deinazw kai megas texnh sumpas o polemikos despotis apofainw plhn ge dh o ontws eimi basilikos oudeis allos ou ara politikos tiqhmi uphretikos ge eimi o o strathgew episthmh ou eoika
english_head: Shall we, then, assume that it controls the other, if we are to agree with our views in the former examples? Yes. And what other art shall we make bold to declare is mistress of that great and terrible art, the art of war as a whole, except the truly kingly art? No other. We shall, then, not call the art of the generals statesmanship, since it is subservient. No; that would not be reasonable.

greek_head: Ἴθι δή καὶ τὴν τῶν δικαστῶν τῶν ὀρθῶς δικαζόντων θεασώμεθα δύναμιν
greek_tokens: eimi dh kai o o dikasths o orqos dikazw qeaomai dunamis panu men oun ara oun epi pleos tis dunamai o peri o sumbolaion panth oposos kew nomimos para nomoqeths basileus paralambanw krinw eis ekeinos skopaw o te dikaios tassw eimi kai adikos o eautou idios areth parexw o mhte upo tis dwron mhte upo fobos mhte oiktos mhte
english_head: Now let us examine the function of the righteous judges. Certainly. Has it any power beyond that of judging men’s contracts with one another, pronouncing them right or wrong by the standard of the existing laws which it has received from the king and law-giver; showing its own peculiar virtue in that it is not so perverted by any bribes, or fears, or pity,

greek_head: ὑπό τινος ἄλλης ἔχθρας μηδὲ φιλίας ἡττηθεῖσα παρὰ τὴν τοῦ νομοθέτου τάξιν ἐθέλειν ἂν τἀλλήλων ἐγκλήματα
greek_tokens: upo tis allos exqra mhde filios hssaomai para o o nomoqeths tacis eqelw an allhlwn egklhma diairew ou alla sxedon osos erw outos eimi o dunamis ergnumi kai o o dikasths ara rwmh aneuriskw ou basilikos eimi alla nomos fulac kai uphretis ekeinos eoika ge ode dh katanohteon eidon sunapas o episthmh o erw ostis politikos ge autos oudeis anafainw
english_head: or enmity, or friendship, as ever to consent to decide the lawsuits of men with each other contrary to the enactments of the lawgiver? No; the business of this power is about as you have described it. Then we find that the strength of judges is not kingly, but is guardian of laws and a servant of the kingly power. So it appears. The consideration of all these arts which have been mentioned leads to the conclusion that none of them is the art of the statesman. For the art that is truly kingly ought not to act itself,

greek_head: τὴν γὰρ ὄντως οὖσαν βασιλικὴν οὐκ αὐτὴν δεῖ πράττειν ἀλλ' ἄρχειν τῶν δυναμένων πράττειν γιγνώσκουσαν τὴν ἀρχήν
greek_tokens: o gar ontws eimi basilikos ou autos deomai prassw alla arxw o dunamai prassw gignwskw o arxh te kai ormh o megas en o polis egkairia te peri kai akairia o de allos o prosstazw draw orqos dia outos ara os men arti dierxomai oute allhlwn oute eautou arxw peri de tis idios eautou eimi ekastos pracis kata o idioths o pracis onoma dikaios lambanw idios
english_head: but should rule over the arts that have the power of action; it should decide upon the right or wrong time for the initiation of the most important measures in the state, and the other arts should perform its behests. Right. Therefore those arts which we have just described, as they control neither one another nor themselves, but have each its own peculiar sphere of action, are quite properly called by special names corresponding to those special actions.

greek_head: Εἴξασι γοῦν Τὴν δὲ πασῶν τε τούτων ἄρχουσαν καὶ τῶν νόμων
greek_tokens: eoika goun o de pas te outos arxw kai o nomos kai sumpas o kata polis epimeleomai kai pas sunufainw orqos o koinos o klhsis perilambanw o dunamis autos prosagoreuw dikaios an ws eoika politikos pantapasi men oun oukoun dh kai kata o o ufantikos paradeigma boulomai an epecerxomai autos nun oste kai pas o genos o kata polis dhlos egw gignomai kai sfodra ge
english_head: That appears, at least, to be the case. But the art which holds sway over them all and watches over the laws and all things in the state, weaving them all most perfectly together, we may, I think, by giving to its function a designation which indicates its power over the community, with full propriety call statecraft. Most assuredly. Shall we then proceed to discuss it after the model supplied by weaving, now that all the classes in the state have been made plain to us? By all means. Then the kingly process of weaving must be described,

greek_head: Τὴν δὴ βασιλικὴν συμπλοκήν ὡς ἔοικε λεκτέον ποία τέ ἐστι καὶ τίνι τρόπῳ συμπλέκουσα ποῖον ἡμῖν
greek_tokens: o dh basilikos sumplokh ws eoika lekteos poa su eimi kai tis tropos sumplekw poios egw ufasma apodidwmi dhlos eimi xalepos endeiknumi pragma anagkaion ara gignomai ws fainw pantws ge meis rhteos o gar aretaw meros aretaw eidomai diaforos eimi tis tropos o peri logos amfisbhthtikos kai mala euepiqetos pros o o polus doca ou manqanw alla wde palin andreia gar oimh su hgeomai
english_head: its nature, the manner in which it combines the threads, and the kind of web it produces. Evidently. It has, apparently, become necessary, after all, to explain a difficult matter. But certainly the explanation must be made. It is difficult, for the assertion that one part of virtue is in a way at variance with another sort of virtue may very easily be assailed by those who appeal to popular opinion in contentious arguments. I do not understand. I will say it again in another way. I suppose you believe

greek_head: μέρος ἓν ἀρετῆς ἡμῖν εἶναι Πάνυ γε
greek_tokens: meros eis aretaw egw eimi panu ge kai meis swfrosunos ge andreia men eteros eis de oun kai outos morion eis ekeinos nai outos dh peri qaumastos tis logos apofainw tolmhteon poios ws eimi kata dh tis tropos eu mala pros allhlwn exqra kai stasis enantios exw en polus o eimi pws legw ou eqw logos oudamos pas gar oun dh
english_head: that courage is one part of virtue. Certainly. And, of course, that self-restraint is different from courage, but is also a part of virtue of which courage is a part. Yes. Now I must venture to utter a strange doctrine about them. What is it? That, in a way, they are in a condition of great hostility and opposition to each other in many beings. What do you mean? Something quite unusual; for, you know, all the parts of virtue

greek_head: ἀλλήλοις τά γε τῆς ἀρετῆς μόρια λέγεταί που φίλια Ναί

That also seems, at least, to be true.

english_head: The courageous natures, on the other hand, are deficient in justice and caution in comparison with the former, but excel in boldness of action; and unless both these qualities are present it is impossible for a state to be entirely prosperous in public and private matters. Yes, certainly. This, then, is the end, let us declare, of the web of the statesman’s activity, the direct interweaving of the characters of restrained and courageous men,

english_head: when the kingly science has drawn them together by friendship and community of sentiment into a common life, and having perfected the most glorious and the best of all textures, clothes with it all the inhabitants of the state, both slaves and freemen, holds them together by this fabric, and omitting nothing which ought to belong to a happy state, rules and watches over them. You have given us, Stranger, a most complete and admirable treatment of the king and the statesman.
